Regulation Status: Afghanistan under Taliban governance has no AI legislation, no binding AI rules, and no draft AI law in any legislative process. No AI-specific or AI-adjacent binding instrument of any kind exists or has been proposed as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: There are no policy instruments addressing AI in Afghanistan — no national AI strategy, no published guidance, no sandboxes, no funding programs, and no government body with any AI mandate.
Governance Type: The Taliban-controlled government operates as a highly centralized authority with no dedicated AI regulator, no inter-agency coordination mechanism for AI, no meaningful subnational governance role, and no independent judiciary or civil oversight body capable of reviewing AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: No industry consultations, civil society engagement, academic advisory input, or participation in international AI governance forums or declarations has been recorded for Afghanistan under the current Taliban administration.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework of any kind — no penalties, no enforcement actions, no designated authority, and no monitoring or audit practice related to AI.

Regulation Status: Albania has no dedicated AI legislation; the closest binding instruments are Law No. 124/2024 on Personal Data Protection (GDPR-aligned) and Law No. 25/2024 on Cybersecurity, both of which incidentally cover AI use-cases without AI-specific provisions. A National AI Strategy 2025–2030 has been signalled and formal AI-specific drafting is expected only after 2026, aligned with EU accession timetable. Albania signed the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (September 2024) but has not ratified it, meaning no binding AI-specific rules are yet in force.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instruments exist; policy relies on GDPR-aligned data-protection rules and cybersecurity law applied incidentally to AI. Some guidance has been issued by the IDP Commissioner (e.g. Guidance No. 05/2025 on personal data processing by competent authorities) and AKSHI oversees algorithmic systems in public administration, but AI-specific sandboxes or dedicated funding programmes remain absent. AKSHI's €2.6 million tender for AI-assisted EU acquis transposition represents the main economic tool so far.
Governance Type: Responsibility is distributed across the IDP Commissioner (data protection), AKCESK (cybersecurity), AKSHI (digital government and public-sector AI), the Bank of Albania, and the Competition Authority, constituting a lead-body-plus-sectoral pattern, but coordination is ad hoc rather than formalised. Albania is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role; courts can review decisions involving AI indirectly through administrative and data-protection channels but have not yet actively shaped AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society participation in AI policy-making is limited to occasional consultations in the EU accession and CoE processes rather than structured domestic forums; academic input is similarly informal. Albania is a signatory to the CoE AI Framework Convention, co-sponsored UN Resolution 78/241 on autonomous weapons, and engages through EU accession processes and Western Balkan digital cooperation, placing it at the level of signatory-to-declarations for international participation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific penalty framework exists; enforcement is channelled through data-protection law (the IDP Commissioner issued six fines in early 2026, triple the prior year's pace) and cybersecurity law, but no authority has an explicit AI remit and monitoring of AI systems per se is not yet systematic.
Specific Laws: Law No. 124/2024 on Personal Data Protection (2024), Law No. 25/2024 on Cybersecurity (2024), National Strategy for Digital Agenda 2022–2026, Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights (signed 2024, not ratified), National Cybersecurity Strategy 2025–2030 (2025)

Regulation Status: Algeria has no binding AI-specific legislation as of mid-2026. The National AI Strategy (adopted December 2024) is a non-binding strategic document with six pillars; the amended data protection law (Law No. 25-11, July 2025) and cybersecurity decrees (Presidential Decree No. 26-07, January 2026) touch AI incidentally but are not AI-directed. Implementation activity exists at the strategic and institutional level (Scientific Council for AI, AI Council, Dzair Digital portal) but no legally enforceable AI rules are in force.
Policy Lever: Policy rests entirely on the non-binding 2024 National AI Strategy and alignment with the AU Continental AI Strategy; no binding AI instruments exist. Soft-law outputs are limited to the strategy document itself with no published sectoral guidance or codes of practice. Algérie Télécom invested 1.5 billion dinars in AI/cybersecurity/robotics startups in 2025, and 500+ digitalization projects are planned for 2025-2026, representing nascent economic tooling. Institutional capacity is emerging—the AI Council and the Scientific Council for AI are designated advisory bodies—but remain thinly resourced and advisory only.
Governance Type: AI governance is centralized under the presidency with advisory input from the AI Council and oversight nominally shared between the Ministry of Higher Education (Scientific Council for AI) and the High Commission for Digitalisation; there is no plurality of independent regulators with distinct AI remits. Coordination between ministries appears ad hoc rather than through a formal interministerial AI mechanism, sub-national governments play no independent role, and judicial or independent review of AI decisions is limited to general administrative courts with no dedicated AI mandate.
Actor Involvement: Industry engagement is largely informal, with state-owned Algérie Télécom participating as an investor and private startups benefiting from funding programs, but no structured formal consultation mechanism exists. Civil society is not visibly included in AI policy processes. Academia is meaningfully engaged via the Scientific Council for AI (chaired by Professor Merouane Debbah), providing advisory input to strategy. Internationally, Algeria aligns with the AU Continental AI Strategy (2024) and maintains some OECD digital economy observer engagement, placing it at the signatory-to-declarations level.
Enforcement Level: No sanctions framework specific to AI exists; the ANPDP enforces data protection law (including incidental AI data processing) but has published no AI-specific enforcement actions, and no dedicated AI enforcement authority with explicit AI remit and dedicated resources has been established.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2024), Law No. 25-11 amending Law No. 18-07 on Personal Data Protection (2025), Presidential Decree No. 26-07 on Cybersecurity Units in Public Institutions (2026), Presidential Decree No. 25-298 amending Presidential Decree No. 20-05 on Information Systems Security (2025), African Union Continental AI Strategy (2024)

Regulation Status: Andorra has no dedicated AI legislation enacted or formally tabled in parliament as of mid-2026. AI is governed only indirectly through the general data-protection law (Llei 29/2021 on Personal Data Protection, amended by Llei 12/2024), and the 2024 Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (CETS 225) has been signed but not ratified or implemented domestically. The voluntary Code of Ethics for AI (February 2024) is non-binding.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments exist; the only soft-law output is the non-binding 2024 Code of Ethics for AI, aligned with UNESCO and OECD principles. The APDA provides indirect institutional capacity through data-protection enforcement, and the Data Intelligence Agency (AID) mandate within Andorra Digital was established by executive decree with no statutory enforcement powers.
Governance Type: Governance is effectively concentrated in the APDA for data-adjacent AI matters, with the AID advisory role created by decree; there is no multi-regulator structure, no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanism, and no subnational governance layer. Judicial oversight through general courts provides minimal independent review of data-related AI issues.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academic engagement in AI policy-making is negligible given Andorra's micro-state economy and limited tech sector. Internationally, Andorra is an early signatory to the Council of Europe AI Framework Convention (CETS 225, signed September 2024) and explicitly aligns its Code of Ethics with UNESCO, OECD, and UN AI governance frameworks.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework or enforcement actions exist; the APDA can apply data-protection penalties (up to €100,000) that incidentally cover some AI processing, but there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority, no AI audits, and no public AI monitoring or reporting regime.
Specific Laws: Llei 29/2021 de protecció de dades personals (LQPD) (2021), Llei 12/2024 (amendment to LQPD) (2024), Codi d'Ètica de la Intel·ligència Artificial d'Andorra (Code of Ethics for AI) (2024), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI and Human Rights Democracy and the Rule of Law CETS 225 (signed 2024)

Regulation Status: Angola's Draft AI Law — proposed by MINTTICS and modeled on the EU AI Act — received general parliamentary approval but had not been fully enacted as of mid-June 2026; it adopts a risk-based classification, strict liability, mandatory watermarking, and extraterritorial reach, placing it squarely in the legislative process. A parallel draft amendment to the Personal Data Protection Law adds a dedicated AI chapter, but neither instrument has yet entered into force, so only paper-level commitments exist.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instruments are yet in force; soft-law activity is limited to the 2023–2027 ICT Strategic Framework (LBTIC) and UNESCO's Readiness Assessment Methodology process launched in 2025, with the Agency for Data Protection (APD) designated but without a formal AI mandate or dedicated AI resources.
Governance Type: AI policy sits almost entirely with MINTTICS at the central government level, with APD playing a peripheral data-protection role; there are no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanisms, no subnational AI roles, and judicial or independent review of AI-related matters remains occasional and incidental.
Actor Involvement: The public consultation on the draft Data Protection Law revision (March–April 2025) opened limited channels for industry and civil society input, but no standing formal roles exist; Angola participates in African Union AI governance discussions and the UNESCO RAM process, qualifying as a signatory to multilateral declarations.
Enforcement Level: The draft AI law defines a substantial sanctions framework (fines up to 1.5 billion kwanzas, imprisonment up to 12 years), but it is not yet in force; APD has taken enforcement actions under data protection law but none with an explicit AI basis, and no AI-specific monitoring or auditing practice exists.
Specific Laws: Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence (proposed 2025, general parliamentary approval 2025–2026); Draft Amendment to Personal Data Protection Law No. 22/11 (public consultation 2025); ICT Strategic Framework 2023–2027 (LBTIC); Personal Data Protection Law No. 22/11 (2011)

Regulation Status: Antigua and Barbuda has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI regulatory instruments, and no draft AI law in the legislative pipeline as of mid-2026. The country remains in a pre-regulatory phase; signing the Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI (2023) constitutes an international soft-law commitment only, with no domestic legal translation.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy mechanisms, national AI strategy, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist; general ICT and telecommunications frameworks are not tailored to AI governance.
Governance Type: Digital and telecommunications matters fall under the Telecommunications Division and ECTEL (Eastern Caribbean Telecommunications Authority) without any dedicated AI oversight function; there is no multi-body coordination on AI and no independent review mechanism specific to AI.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic consultation processes involve industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy; international engagement is limited to Antigua and Barbuda's participation in CARICOM coordination and its signature on the Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI (2023).
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, designated authority, penalties, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in Antigua and Barbuda.
Specific Laws: Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI (2023) [international declaration, non-binding]

Regulation Status: Argentina has no enacted binding AI-specific legislation as of mid-2026. Multiple bills—including Bill 3003-D-2024 (responsible AI framework), Bill 4243-D-2025 (data protection in AI systems), and Bill 5945-D-2025 (image rights vs. AI-generated content)—remain in the legislative process without passage. Existing coverage relies on the general Personal Data Protection Law 25,326, with soft guidance from the 2023 AAIP Provision 2/2023 on Recommendations for Reliable AI, placing Argentina between a national-strategy-only posture and active drafting.
Policy Lever: Argentina's primary policy mechanisms are soft law: the National AI Strategy (2019), AAIP Provision 2/2023 with AI recommendations, and Resolución 90/2021 establishing an AI capacity-building program. The AAIP is designated for data-protection oversight including AI-adjacent enforcement, but no dedicated AI regulatory body or comprehensive binding instrument exists; economic tools are limited to the Resolución 90/2021 program and some provincial-level digital sandbox activities.
Governance Type: Oversight is fragmented across the AAIP (data protection), the Secretariat of Science and Technology (AI strategy coordination), and sector-specific bodies, but there is no dedicated AI regulator and no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism; some provinces have begun issuing AI-related protocols, and courts can apply existing law to AI cases but have not yet actively shaped a distinct AI jurisprudence.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academic actors participate in consultation processes around the pending bills and the AAIP's public-private dialogue on data-protection reform, while civil society engagement is occasional rather than institutionalized; internationally, Argentina is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration and Cartagena Declaration, engages with OECD AI policy processes, and maintains bilateral digital-standards cooperation with the EU.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is conducted by the AAIP under the general Personal Data Protection Law 25,326, which carries defined penalties applicable to AI-related data misuse; the AAIP has taken isolated ex-officio investigations (including a December 2025 data-breach probe) but there is no dedicated AI enforcement remit, no systematic AI auditing program, and no routine public AI-specific enforcement reporting.
Specific Laws: Personal Data Protection Law 25,326 (2000), National AI Strategy (2019), Resolución 90/2021 – Programa de Inteligencia Artificial, Provision 2/2023 – Recommendations for Reliable Artificial Intelligence (AAIP), Bill 3003-D-2024 (pending), Bill 4243-D-2025 (pending), Bill 5945-D-2025 (pending)

Regulation Status: Armenia signed the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (STCE n°225) on 27 January 2026, making it a signatory to a binding international treaty, but has not ratified it or enacted any domestic AI-specific legislation; no binding AI rules are in force nationally. The country's general ICT and high-tech support laws (e.g., Law No. HO-498-N on State Support for the High-Tech Sector, 2025) do not address AI specifically, leaving only indirect, incidental coverage.
Policy Lever: Armenia lacks any binding domestic AI-specific instruments; the Ministry of High-Tech Industry has launched soft-law initiatives including an AI Literacy Framework and the AI Virtual Institute (July 2025), and provides economic support through the high-tech sector law, but no dedicated, staffed AI regulatory body exists—coordination remains within the Ministry's broader tech remit.
Governance Type: AI governance is effectively concentrated in the Ministry of High-Tech Industry with no dedicated AI regulator, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism, and no meaningful subnational role; occasional civil society and journalistic scrutiny (e.g., concerns over the Firebird AI data center) provides minimal independent oversight but no structured review body.
Actor Involvement: Industry is engaged through public-private partnerships (Nvidia, Firebird.ai, Amazon Web Services) and the AI Virtual Institute, while Yerevan State University provides some academic input; civil society participation is ad hoc and limited, and international engagement is anchored by the Council of Europe Convention signature and Eastern Partnership digital cooperation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Armenia; enforcement of any AI-related obligations would have to rely on general ICT or data protection authorities with no explicit AI remit.
Specific Laws: Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law (STCE n°225) (signed 2026), Law No. HO-498-N on State Support for the High-Tech Sector (2025), Digital Transformation Strategy (2021-2025)

Regulation Status: Australia has no dedicated AI Act and has formally abandoned plans for mandatory guardrails in favour of a voluntary, standards-led approach under the National AI Plan (November 2025). The sole near-term binding AI-related obligation is the Privacy Act automated decision-making (ADM) transparency requirement, effective 10 December 2026 but not yet in force as of this assessment. Existing general laws (Privacy Act, Australian Consumer Law, Online Safety Act) apply to AI incidentally rather than by design, placing Australia at a transitional stage between soft and binding coverage.
Policy Lever: Policy relies primarily on voluntary AI6 guardrails, the National AI Plan 2025, and sector-specific guidance from OAIC, eSafety and ACCC, constituting a maintained suite of soft-law tools, while binding instruments are limited to the forthcoming Privacy Act ADM amendments; the AU$29.9M AI Safety Institute provides institutional capacity but remains advisory and lightly resourced relative to dedicated enforcement bodies.
Governance Type: Australia distributes AI oversight across multiple independent bodies—OAIC (privacy), eSafety Commissioner (online safety), ACCC (consumer law), AI Safety Institute (evaluation), and the Department of Industry (strategy)—with ad hoc inter-agency coordination rather than a formal statutory mechanism; states and territories implement national rules but do not regulate AI independently, and courts and ombudsman bodies provide occasional independent review (e.g. the 2026 Administrative Review Tribunal ruling on OAIC's Bunnings facial recognition determination).
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society are consulted through published government processes and the National AI Plan development, with the Privacy Commissioner and OAIC engaging NGOs and researchers in guidance work; internationally Australia is highly active as a member of the OECD AI Policy Observatory, GPAI, the Quad AI Partnership, AUKUS technology cooperation, and the International Network of AI Safety Institutes.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement relies on existing sector regulators: OAIC has pursued AI-related privacy investigations (I-MED inquiry 2025, Bunnings facial recognition review 2026) and can levy penalties up to $50 million; ACCC enforces consumer law with penalties up to $100 million, but there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority and no routine AI-specific audit or public reporting regime beyond ad hoc inquiries.
Specific Laws: Privacy Act 1988 (as amended, ADM transparency obligations effective December 2026), Australian Consumer Law (Competition and Consumer Act 2010), Online Safety Act 2021, National AI Plan 2025, Voluntary AI Safety Standard (2024)

Regulation Status: Austria is directly subject to the EU AI Act (Regulation EU 2024/1689), which is binding and in force: prohibited AI practices apply since February 2025, GPAI obligations since August 2025, and most high-risk AI provisions become applicable in August 2026. The framework provides horizontal, cross-sector coverage, but implementation remains in transition—national competent authority designation was not fully finalized as of mid-2026, and the KI-Servicestelle (established via BGBl I Nr. 6/2024) remains in advisory mode rather than full enforcement mode. Austria relies primarily on the directly applicable EU AI Act rather than a standalone national AI statute, supplemented by the Digital Austria Act 2.0 (2025) for public sector AI and the KI-Umsetzungsplan 2024.
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act is the primary binding instrument, with the DSG (Data Protection Act) and sector-specific EU legislation applying in parallel; Austria has not enacted additional binding national AI legislation. Soft law includes KI-Servicestelle guidance, the Leitfaden Digitale Verwaltung: KI, Ethik und Recht (v2.0), and the AIM AT 2030 strategy. Economic tools are relatively active: aws AI Adoption program, FFG/FWF AIM AT funding, AI for Green initiative, and the Digital Überall Plus program. Institutional capacity exists through the KI-Servicestelle and KI-Beirat, but these are advisory; market surveillance and enforcement powers are still being formally assigned.
Governance Type: Governance involves the DSB (data protection and AI enforcement), the RTR/KI-Servicestelle (advisory and competence centre), federal ministries, and the KI-Beirat (expert advisory board), constituting a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure. The AI Stakeholder Forum (March 2024) provides a formal but still maturing cross-actor coordination mechanism, while AI governance is primarily federal with no independent subnational regulatory role; courts and independent bodies (DSB, PDK) conduct occasional review of AI-related decisions.
Actor Involvement: The AI Stakeholder Forum (since March 2024) brings together industry associations, civil society organisations, and interest groups in structured consultation, while the KI-Beirat provides expert advisory input from technology, law, and ethics domains. Austria participates actively in the EU AI Board, OECD AI Policy Observatory, and the G7 Hiroshima AI Process, giving it a strong international profile, though domestic civil society and academic roles remain consultative rather than standing formal positions.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's comprehensive penalty framework (fines up to €35M or 7% of global turnover) is legally applicable in Austria, but formal designation of a national AI market surveillance authority was still pending as of mid-2026; the DSB handles data-protection-related AI enforcement (with prior actions on Clearview AI and Microsoft) and the KI-Servicestelle lacks sanctioning powers, so dedicated AI-specific enforcement infrastructure and routine monitoring remain limited.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), KommAustria-Gesetz amendment establishing KI-Servicestelle / BGBl. I Nr. 6/2024 (2024), TKG 2021 §194a (2024 amendment), Digital Austria Act 2.0 (2025), AIM AT 2030 / AI Mission Austria 2030 (2021), KI-Umsetzungsplan 2024 (2024), Austrian Data Protection Act / DSG (2018, amended), GDPR / Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (2016)

Regulation Status: Azerbaijan's primary AI governance instrument remains the AI Strategy 2025–2028 (Presidential Decree No. 530, March 2025), which is non-binding but mandates establishing a dedicated AI legal framework by 2027. The April 2026 Criminal Code amendments (Articles 148-2 and 242-1) introduced the first binding AI-specific criminal penalties—targeting deepfakes and non-consensual AI-generated content—but these are narrow, use-case-specific provisions rather than horizontal AI regulation. No comprehensive binding AI law is in force or in active parliamentary process.
Policy Lever: Policy relies on presidential strategies (AI Strategy 2025–2028, Digital Economy Strategy 2026–2029) and targeted criminal code amendments; there is one binding instrument (the April 2026 criminal penalties) limited to specific AI misuse. The strategy mentions preferential loans and a planned AI innovation hub, but no operational sandbox or mature suite of guidance exists, and AI oversight capacity sits within the Ministry of Digital Development and Transport without dedicated AI staffing.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralized: the Presidential Administration drives AI strategy via decree, with the Ministry of Digital Development and Transport as the lead implementing body and no independent AI regulator. Azerbaijan is a unitary state with no subnational AI role; courts may apply the new criminal provisions but no standing independent review mechanism for AI exists.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academia have limited structured input, mainly through working groups referenced in strategy documentation, while domestic civil society engagement is minimal given Azerbaijan's constrained NGO environment. Internationally, Azerbaijan participates in Turkic States AI cooperation frameworks, maintains bilateral AI partnerships with Turkey and Israel, and is a Council of Europe member engaged in digital governance discussions, though it has not signed the CoE AI Convention (CETS 225).
Enforcement Level: A narrow sanctions framework exists via the April 2026 criminal code amendments (imprisonment up to three years for AI-generated deepfakes/non-consensual content), but no dedicated AI enforcement authority exists, no AI-specific enforcement actions have been recorded, and no routine monitoring or public AI compliance reporting is in place.
Specific Laws: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Republic of Azerbaijan for 2025–2028 (Presidential Decree No. 530, 2025), Digital Economy Development Strategy 2026–2029 (Presidential Decree, 2025), Criminal Code amendments introducing Articles 148-2 and 242-1 on illegal AI use (2026), Action Plan for Accelerating Digital Development 2026–2028 (2026)

Regulation Status: The Bahamas has no AI-specific legislation, binding AI rules, or formal AI policy framework in force as of June 2026. The country relies solely on general laws that may incidentally touch AI but contain no AI-specific provisions, and no AI bill has entered the legislative process.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist; AI governance remains entirely unaddressed by dedicated policy levers.
Governance Type: There is no designated AI regulatory authority, no formal coordination mechanism across agencies, no subnational AI governance, and no independent body with an AI oversight remit; governance of AI-related matters, to the extent it occurs, is fragmented across general regulators.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic consultations involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy have been documented; the Bahamas has participated in regional CARICOM AI discussions and endorsed the Santiago Declaration on AI in the Americas, providing the only meaningful actor engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated enforcement authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in the Bahamas.

Regulation Status: Bahrain's 38-article standalone AI Regulation Law was unanimously approved by the Shura Council in April 2024 but faced government pushback in late 2024 and remained under parliamentary review as of late 2025, with no confirmed enactment; the IGA's National AI Policy (July 2025 final version) is a binding public-sector directive but is not statute-level law. The legislative process is well advanced and AI-specific in scope, covering risk classification, prohibited uses, licensing, and penalties, but the draft law has not entered force, so implementation remains limited to the soft-law policy layer.
Policy Lever: The IGA's National AI Policy (Version 1.0, July 2025) and the adopted GCC Guiding Manual on AI Ethics constitute published guidance and a binding public-sector directive, while the draft AI Regulation Law (not yet enacted) would add statutory instruments; a Regulatory Sandbox was proposed by the government as an alternative enforcement mechanism but has not been formally established, and the IGA coordinates AI policy without a standalone, fully resourced AI oversight unit operational.
Governance Type: Bahrain is a small unitary state with no subnational governance role, and the IGA acts as the single lead digital and AI policy authority, with the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology and the ICT Governance Committee providing some sectoral coordination but no formal multi-regulator mechanism. Judicial review exists in principle but no AI-specific court or ombudsman function has been established.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academic consultations informed the drafting of the AI Regulation Law, and Bahrain is active in GCC regional AI governance through the GCC Ministerial Committee for eGovernment, but civil society plays no documented formal role in domestic AI policy processes and no standing advisory bodies have been established for non-governmental actors.
Enforcement Level: The draft AI Regulation Law defines a penalty framework (up to 3 years imprisonment or BD 2,000 fines) and the National AI Policy requires compliance auditing by government entities, but the standalone law is not yet in force, no AI-specific enforcement actions have been reported, and the proposed AI oversight unit within IGA has not been established as a resourced operational body.
Specific Laws: Draft Artificial Intelligence Regulation Law (38 articles, Shura Council approved April 2024, pending enactment), General Policy for the Use of Artificial Intelligence Version 1.0 (July 2025), Personal Data Protection Law (2018), Information Technology Crimes Law, GCC Guiding Manual on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence Use (adopted 2025)

Regulation Status: Bangladesh has no enacted AI-specific legislation. The National AI Policy 2026-2030 (Draft v2, February 2026) remains in consultation by the ICT Division, with a Comprehensive AI Act directed for drafting only by 2028. Adjacent instruments—Personal Data Protection Ordinance 2025, National Data Governance Ordinance 2025, and Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025—are enacted but address AI only incidentally. The country is in a pre-legislative, strategy-drafting phase with binding force effectively absent for AI-specific rules.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instruments exist; the government relies on the draft National AI Policy 2026-2030 and voluntary sectoral guidelines as soft-law instruments. A regulatory sandbox and AI research hub (via the University Grants Commission) are proposed but not yet operational, and a Data Protection Authority is mandated under the PDPO 2025 but not yet fully resourced for AI oversight.
Governance Type: The ICT Division is the lead—and effectively sole—AI policy body, with informal inter-agency coordination referenced in the draft policy but no formal multi-regulator structure yet in place. Bangladesh is unitary with no subnational AI regulatory role, and while cyber tribunals exist under the Cyber Security Act 2023, independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI specifically remains nascent.
Actor Involvement: Multiple stakeholder consultations involving industry, academia, and civil society have been conducted through the AI policy drafting process and UNESCO/UNDP-supported RAM Report (2025), but involvement remains ad hoc and consultative rather than through formal standing roles. Bangladesh participates in UN digital governance forums and signed cooperative frameworks with UNESCO, representing moderate international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, or dedicated AI enforcement authority exists; the proposed AI Act is not expected until 2028 at the earliest, and current monitoring is limited to general cybersecurity oversight under the BGD e-Gov CIRT with no AI-specific audits or public reporting.
Specific Laws: National AI Policy 2026-2030 (Draft v2, 2026), National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024, Personal Data Protection Ordinance (2025), National Data Governance Ordinance (2025), Cyber Safety Ordinance (2025), Cyber Security Act (2023)

Regulation Status: Barbados has no AI-specific legislation, dedicated regulatory framework, or binding AI rules in force or formally proposed as of mid-2026. The country relies on general data protection and consumer protection laws that do not explicitly address AI systems, leaving AI governance without any binding foundation.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidance documents, sandbox programs, or dedicated institutional bodies exist in Barbados; digital and technology matters are handled by general-purpose government bodies without AI-specific mandates or resources.
Governance Type: Barbados is a small unitary state with no dedicated AI regulator; digital policy falls under general government ministries with no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanisms, no subnational AI governance role, and no independent body specifically reviewing AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: Domestic AI policy development lacks structured engagement from industry, civil society, or academia; Barbados participates in regional CARICOM digital governance discussions and has associated with broader Caribbean digital cooperation efforts, providing minimal international engagement on AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated enforcement authority, or monitoring practice exists in Barbados, as there are no AI-specific rules to enforce.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act (2019)

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, Belarus has no enacted AI-specific legislation. A draft Law on Fundamentals of State Regulation of the Fields of Application of Artificial Intelligence Technologies was under public consultation (March–April 2026) and a cross-ministerial working group of 42 specialists was formed to draft binding legislation, but no law has passed. The National Academy of Sciences has been designated as the prospective sectoral AI regulator, and AI is included in the draft National Sustainable Development Strategy through 2040 and the National Digital Development Strategy 2026–2030, but these remain planning documents rather than binding law.
Policy Lever: Belarus relies on the High-Tech Park regime for indirect tech-sector incentives and is actively developing AI technical standards through Gosstandart (20–25 new AI standards planned by end of 2026, with 42 already ready), drawing on CIS and international experience. No AI-specific binding policy instruments exist yet; a CIS Model Law on AI Technologies was developed by Belarus's National Academy of Sciences and approved by the CIS Interparliamentary Assembly in 2025, providing a template but not domestic binding force.
Governance Type: Belarus operates a centralized governance model: the National Academy of Sciences is designated as the future AI sectoral regulator, with a cross-ministerial working group coordinating draft legislation, but no independent AI authority or multi-regulator structure exists. There is no subnational AI governance role and no independent judicial or ombudsman body shaping AI rules.
Actor Involvement: A 2024–2025 national readiness assessment engaged over 10,000 professionals from public, private, and academic sectors, and a Minsk forum in 2025 brought together government, business, and academia; however, civil society plays only a limited consultative role and international engagement is restricted to CIS/EAEU frameworks due to political isolation. The National Academy of Sciences holds a formal advisory and drafting role in AI policy.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated enforcement authority, or monitoring practice exists in Belarus as of June 2026, as no AI-specific legislation has been enacted.
Specific Laws: CIS Model Law on Artificial Intelligence Technologies (2025, developed by Belarus, approved by CIS Interparliamentary Assembly), National Digital Development Strategy 2026–2030 (draft), draft Law on Fundamentals of State Regulation of the Fields of Application of Artificial Intelligence Technologies (2026, under consultation)

Regulation Status: Belgium is directly subject to the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), a binding horizontal AI regulation covering all sectors. Prohibited-AI rules have been in force since February 2025 and GPAI obligations since August 2025, but full high-risk AI system compliance is only just arriving (August 2, 2026 deadline), leaving the regime partially in transition. The instrument is wholly AI-specific and represents the global frontier in dedicated AI legislation.
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act is the primary binding instrument, supplemented by Belgium's National Convergence Plan for AI and the 2025-2029 Federal Government Agreement; soft-law outputs include APD/GBA guidance, Agoria materials, and regional plans (Flanders AI Policy Plan 2024-2028). Economic tools include the €32M TRAIL research program, Flemish AI Academie, and regional TEF-Health co-funding, while institutional capacity is building (BIPT designated as MSA, AI Observatory, AI Steering Committee, Data and AI Ethics Advisory Committee) but not yet fully operational at the national level.
Governance Type: Belgium has a complex multi-authority structure: BIPT as main AI Act MSA, 21 bodies designated under Article 77 for fundamental rights, the APD/GBA for data-AI matters, and FPS BOSA for policy coordination, all underpinned by an AI Steering Committee with federal, regional, university, and industry representation. Flanders, Wallonia, and Brussels each operate distinct AI policy plans, creating meaningful subnational activity, while courts and the APD provide occasional independent review but no routine AI-specific judicial oversight yet.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates through Agoria, AI4Belgium coalition, and the multi-stakeholder AI Steering Committee; academia holds formal seats on both the AI Steering Committee and the Data and AI Ethics Advisory Committee (Royal Decree, May 2024); civil society is consulted via GDPR processes and EU AI Act channels but lacks formal standing roles; Belgium is deeply embedded in international AI governance as host of EU institutions, active on the EU AI Board, and participating in the OECD Coordinated Plan on AI and EuroHPC JU.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act provides a comprehensive tiered penalty framework (up to €35M/7% turnover) applicable in Belgium, and BIPT has been designated as the main MSA via the January 2025 Government Declaration (though not yet legislatively confirmed), while the APD/GBA is preparing systemic enforcement under its 2026-2028 strategic plan; no AI-specific enforcement actions have been published yet, and monitoring is nascent with the AI Observatory established but high-risk compliance deadlines only just arriving.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), National Convergence Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (2019, updated), 2025-2029 Federal Government Agreement (2025), Royal Decree establishing AI Steering Committee (May 2024), Royal Decree establishing Data and AI Ethics Advisory Committee (October 2023/May 2024), Flanders AI Policy Plan 2024-2028 (2024), GDPR / Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (2018)

Regulation Status: Belize has enacted no AI-specific legislation or national AI strategy as of June 2026. The Data Protection Act 2021 and Telecommunications Act provide general digital governance, but contain no AI-specific provisions; the 2025 Global Digital Services Investment Policy references AI opportunities in the BPO sector but creates no binding regulatory obligations. Belize remains in a regulatory vacuum for AI.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, codes of practice, sandbox programs, or dedicated AI institutional bodies exist in Belize; the Data Protection Commissioner established under the Data Protection Act 2021 covers personal data generally but has no AI-specific mandate or resources.
Governance Type: Belize has no designated AI regulatory authority, no interagency AI coordination mechanism, and no subnational AI governance activity; general digital oversight is fragmented across the Office of the Prime Minister, the Utilities Commission (telecoms), and the Data Protection Commissioner without any formal AI coordination structure.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder consultation processes for AI policy exist; academic engagement is nascent (University of Belize published a 2026 journal article on AI and human rights), and Belize participates indirectly in regional AI governance through CARICOM membership and the CTU Caribbean AI Task Force launched July 2025, but has not signed dedicated AI declarations or taken active roles in international AI standards bodies.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring regime exists in Belize; even the Data Protection Commissioner established under the 2021 Act lacks operational capacity for AI-related oversight according to regional assessments.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act (2021), Telecommunications Act (Cap. 229), National Digital Agenda 2022-2025

Regulation Status: Benin has no binding AI-specific legislation; the only AI-focused instrument is the non-binding Stratégie Nationale d'Intelligence Artificielle et des Mégadonnées (SNIAM) 2023–2027, adopted by the Council of Ministers in January 2023. The general Code du Numérique (Law 2017-20, amended 2020-35) and the 2009 personal data protection law incidentally touch AI-adjacent areas but are not AI-specific, leaving Benin with only paper-level strategy commitments and no enforcement mechanism.
Policy Lever: Benin relies exclusively on the SNIAM strategy document and capacity-building initiatives (skills programs, university partnerships, startup ecosystem support) rather than binding regulatory mandates; the Ministry of Digitalisation coordinates implementation with some stakeholder engagement and identifies financing mechanisms for entrepreneurship, but no dedicated AI institution or compliance infrastructure exists.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalization is the sole coordinating body for AI policy with no dedicated AI regulatory authority or independent oversight body, governance is effectively centralized with ad hoc cross-ministerial coordination envisaged in the SNIAM but no formal inter-agency mechanism established; subnational governments play no distinct AI governance role, and no court or ombudsman has an active AI remit.
Actor Involvement: The SNIAM development process involved stakeholders including civil society, academia, startups, and the private sector in a consultative but non-standing capacity; at the international level, Benin participates in the African Union Continental AI Strategy (endorsed July 2024), the Smart Africa Alliance, and joined the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime in October 2024, giving it moderate international engagement without formal treaty-level AI governance roles.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework or dedicated enforcement authority exists; strategy implementation is monitored by the Ministry of Digitalisation through the SNIAM action plan, but monitoring is self-reported and administrative rather than independent, with no public enforcement actions recorded.
Specific Laws: Stratégie Nationale d'Intelligence Artificielle et des Mégadonnées (SNIAM) 2023-2027, Code du Numérique (Loi No. 2017-20 of 2018, amended by Loi No. 2020-35 of 2021), Loi No. 2009-09 of 2009 on Protection of Personally Identifiable Information

Regulation Status: Bhutan has no binding AI-specific legislation as of June 2026; the National Assembly passed a motion in June 2025 calling for a regulatory framework, but no bill has been enacted. The operative instruments remain the voluntary Guideline for Generative AI Usage in the Civil Service (2024) and the National AI Strategy (NAIS) 2025, both of which are soft guidance documents without legal force.
Policy Lever: Policy relies entirely on soft instruments: GovTech's 2024 civil-service AI guidelines and the NAIS 2025 strategy document pending formal GovTech Commission approval; no binding rules, no AI-specific sandbox or funding programs, and GovTech serves as a thinly resourced lead body without a dedicated AI compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: GovTech (Government Technology Agency) functions as the single effective authority on AI policy, with informal philosophical input from the GNH Commission; there are no formal inter-agency coordination mechanisms, no subnational AI role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters.
Actor Involvement: The NAIS 2025 was developed through stakeholder consultations that included some academic input, but there are no standing formal roles for industry or civil society; internationally, Bhutan participates in UNDP AI readiness assessments, joined the 50-in-5 DPI campaign in June 2026, and has engaged with UNESCO on AI readiness, placing it at signatory/participant level.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no sanctions defined for AI violations, no dedicated enforcement authority, and no systematic monitoring or audit regime in place.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy (NAIS) 2025, Guideline for Generative Artificial Intelligence Usage in the Civil Service 2024, Bhutan AI Readiness Assessment (AIRA) 2024

Regulation Status: Bolivia has no dedicated AI legislation enacted or formally in legislative process as of mid-2026. The country relies entirely on non-binding regional declarations (Santiago 2023, Cartagena 2024) with no domestic binding AI rules of any kind.
Policy Lever: Bolivia has not enacted any AI-specific binding instruments, issued national AI guidance or codes of practice, funded AI sandboxes or innovation programs, nor established any dedicated AI institutional body.
Governance Type: Digital and technology policy sits with the Ministry of Communications as a single central authority, with no sectoral AI regulators, no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanism, no subnational AI governance role, and no independent review body active on AI matters.
Actor Involvement: Bolivia has no structured domestic consultative process involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy; its only documented engagement is participation in regional Latin American declarations (Santiago 2023, Cartagena 2024) as a signatory.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, designated enforcement authority, or monitoring/audit practice exists in Bolivia.
Specific Laws: Santiago Declaration on Artificial Intelligence (2023), Cartagena Declaration on Artificial Intelligence (2024)

Regulation Status: Bosnia and Herzegovina has no dedicated AI legislation, no national AI strategy, and no binding AI-specific rules as of June 2026. The deeply fragmented constitutional structure dividing authority between the Federation of BiH, Republika Srpska, and the Brčko District means no unified binding instrument has been enacted or formally proposed at the state level.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments of any kind exist; there are no binding instruments, no published AI guidance or codes of practice, no sandbox or funding programs, and no designated AI institutional body at the state or entity level.
Governance Type: Bosnia and Herzegovina's governance is constitutionally split between two entities and a district, meaning the sub-national entities (Federation of BiH and Republika Srpska) effectively hold de facto regulatory authority by default in the absence of any state-level AI framework, though neither entity has enacted AI-specific rules either; there is no formal inter-entity coordination mechanism for AI and no independent oversight body with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic stakeholder engagement—no industry consultations, civil society input, or academic advisory role on AI policy at the state level; Bosnia and Herzegovina participates in Council of Europe processes and holds EU candidate status, providing some marginal international exposure to AI governance discussions but without active treaty or standards participation.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework for AI exists: no penalties are defined, no enforcement actions have been taken, no authority has been designated for AI oversight, and no monitoring or auditing practice is in place.

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, Botswana has no enacted AI-specific legislation; a National AI Policy was announced as being in its 'final drafting stage' in August 2025 but had not been adopted. General digital laws in force—the Data Protection Act 2024 (Act No. 18 of 2024, in force January 2025), the Digital Services Act 2025, and the Cybersecurity Act 2025—touch AI incidentally but contain no dedicated AI provisions.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instruments exist; the pending National AI Policy, informed by a UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment completed in 2024–2025 and Estonia collaboration, remains in draft. No AI-targeted funding programs, sandboxes, or dedicated AI institutional bodies have been established.
Governance Type: AI-adjacent governance is handled by BOCRA (communications regulator) and the Ministry of Communications and Innovation, with no dedicated AI body or formal multi-actor coordination mechanism; the newly passed Digital Services Act 2025 creates a Digital Services Authority and the Cybersecurity Act 2025 creates a National Cyber Security Authority, but neither has an explicit AI remit. Botswana is a unitary state with no subnational legislative role in AI.
Actor Involvement: There is no evidence of structured domestic industry, civil society, or academic input into AI policy-making; internationally, Botswana participates in the Smart Africa Alliance (which established the Africa AI Council in November 2025), the African Union digital economy frameworks, and engaged with UNESCO's AI Readiness Assessment Methodology across Southern Africa in 2025.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, dedicated enforcement authority, or monitoring mechanism exists; enforcement of AI-adjacent rules (e.g., data protection) rests with BOCRA under the Data Protection Act 2024, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been recorded.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act, 2024 (Act No. 18 of 2024), Digital Services Act, 2025, Cybersecurity Act, 2025

Regulation Status: Brazil's PL 2338/2023 (AI Legal Framework) was approved by the Senate in December 2024 and forwarded to the Chamber of Deputies in March 2025, where a special committee and rapporteur (Dep. Aguinaldo Ribeiro) prepared a plenary vote scheduled for May 27, 2026, but the bill remains unenacted as of June 13, 2026. Existing binding coverage of AI comes through the LGPD (Law 13,709/2018, especially Art. 20 on automated decision-making), the Consumer Defense Code, Law 15,123/2025 (AI-enabled violence), and Law 15,211/2025 (Digital ECA for children online, in force since March 2026) — none constitute a comprehensive horizontal AI statute. The PBIA 2024–2028 strategy and ANPD's AI sandbox (launched mid-2025) add policy depth but are not binding legislation.
Policy Lever: Brazil's binding instruments touching AI remain indirect — LGPD, ECA Digital, and Criminal Code amendments — while the dedicated AI bill is still pending; the ANPD (now an autonomous regulatory agency since Decree 12,881/2026) publishes regulatory agendas, generative AI reports, and guidance under LGPD Art. 20, and runs an AI regulatory sandbox. The PBIA 2024–2028 provides a national investment and innovation roadmap, but dedicated AI economic funding programs remain nascent and no multi-sectoral binding AI instruments exist yet.
Governance Type: Brazil's AI governance is structurally plural: ANPD (designated lead under the pending bill), CADE (competition), SENACON (consumer), TSE (elections), BACEN (financial AI), ANVISA (healthcare), and CFM (medical AI Resolution 2,454/2026) each exercise overlapping remits, but coordination is largely ad hoc without a formal cross-agency AI governance body. Subnational involvement is minimal — Goiás enacted a soft-law AI statute (No. 205/2025) and Ceará has Law 17,611/2021, but states do not independently regulate AI in a substantive way. Brazilian courts, the Ministério Público, and the Supreme Court have actively shaped digital platform rules, providing meaningful nongovernmental checks.
Actor Involvement: Industry, civil society (including groups like Data Privacy Brasil, IDEC), and academia participated in multiple public hearings and consultations during the Senate and Chamber legislative process, including the CTIA committee deliberations, though without standing formal co-governance roles. Brazil is exceptionally active internationally: it chaired the G20 in 2024 (shaping global AI governance declarations), participates in OECD AI Policy Observatory, co-authored the UNESCO AI Recommendation, and leads LAC regional AI governance through the Santiago and Cartagena Declarations.
Enforcement Level: ANPD has a defined sanctions framework under LGPD (fines up to 2% of Brazilian revenue) and has issued isolated enforcement actions — including two against governmental institutions in early 2024 and proceedings against 20 companies for DPO non-compliance — but AI-specific enforcement is absent since no dedicated AI statute is yet in force; the ANPD, now an autonomous agency with a 2025–2026 regulatory agenda that explicitly includes AI and generative AI, conducts periodic reviews and published a Generative AI Report, but routine AI-specific audits and public enforcement reporting remain limited.
Specific Laws: Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados - LGPD (Law No. 13,709/2018), Marco Civil da Internet (Law No. 12,965/2014), Estatuto da Criança e do Adolescente Digital - ECA Digital (Law No. 15,211/2025), Lei No. 15,123/2025 (AI-enabled violence against women - Criminal Code amendment), Decreto No. 12,881/2026 (ANPD autonomous agency structure), Plano Brasileiro de Inteligência Artificial - PBIA 2024-2028, Estratégia Brasileira de Inteligência Artificial - EBIA (2021), PL 2338/2023 (AI Legal Framework - pending), Goiás State Law No. 205/2025, Ceará State Law No. 17,611/2021

Regulation Status: Brunei has no binding national AI legislation in force or formally proposed as of mid-2026. The country relies on the voluntary ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics and the Digital Economy Masterplan 2025, neither of which carries legal force. AI is addressed at the regional/soft-law level only, with no dedicated domestic AI-specific binding instrument.
Policy Lever: Policy rests on non-binding instruments: the ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics and the Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 provide strategic direction without legal mandates. The Authority for Info-communications Technology Industry of Brunei (AITI) facilitates digital development including limited sandbox-type programs, but dedicated AI-specific funding or procurement schemes are minimal.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Transport and Infocommunications (MTIC) and AITI coordinate digital and AI-adjacent policy, functioning as a de facto single-authority structure with no dedicated AI regulator established. Brunei is a small unitary state with no subnational regulatory tier, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI decisions is not established.
Actor Involvement: Industry engagement occurs informally through AITI's digital economy programs, but structured consultations or standing working groups on AI policy are not publicly documented. Civil society and academia have no formal role in AI governance domestically; Brunei's most substantive engagement is at the ASEAN level through the ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics framework.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement actions, dedicated enforcement authority, or formal monitoring and audit regime exists in Brunei as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: Digital Economy Masterplan 2025, ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics (2024)

Regulation Status: Bulgaria is bound by the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), which entered into force August 2024 and has been applying in stages since February 2025, giving Bulgaria a horizontal, cross-sector binding framework as an EU member state. However, Bulgaria has not yet enacted supplementary national legislation: as of late 2025/early 2026, the Ministry of Electronic Governance confirmed no final decisions on a national coordination model, sanctioning regime, or national register of AI systems, with the full national regulatory framework targeted for March 2026. The AI specificity is moderate—Bulgaria relies on the EU Act itself (a dedicated AI instrument) but has not yet adapted it with national secondary rules or guidance.
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act constitutes the single binding instrument in force; Bulgaria designated seven fundamental-rights protection bodies (Council of Ministers Decision No. 398, June 18 2025) but has not yet designated a market surveillance authority or sanctioning body. A proposed AI regulatory laboratory with EUR 213,520 budget was deprioritized by the Ministry of Finance in October 2025, leaving economic tools and institutional capacity at an early/thin stage.
Governance Type: Bulgaria's governance structure features the Ministry of Electronic Governance as lead agency, seven designated fundamental-rights bodies, and the CPDP for personal-data-related AI oversight, creating a partial multi-body landscape; however, formal coordination mechanisms between these bodies remain nascent and an interdepartmental working group is still developing the model. Bulgaria is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role, and judicial or ombudsman review of AI decisions is occasional at best.
Actor Involvement: Bulgaria participates in the EU AI Board and broader European AI governance structures (including the OECD AI Policy Observatory), but domestic stakeholder engagement—industry, civil society, and academia—appears limited to occasional consultations with no standing formal roles established as of mid-2026. The Digital National Alliance (an industry body) has published analysis, indicating some industry engagement, but no formal policy-making roles are documented.
Enforcement Level: Bulgaria lacks a defined national sanctions regime for the EU AI Act as of early 2026—the Ministry of Electronic Governance explicitly acknowledged that sanctions remain unclear—and no dedicated AI market surveillance authority has been formally designated or resourced, meaning enforcement activity is effectively non-existent beyond the EU-level obligations already in force.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (applicable from 2024/2025), National AI Strategy (2020), Council of Ministers Decision No. 398 (2025)

Regulation Status: Burkina Faso has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no draft AI law in any legislative process. The country operates under military transitional governance since 2022, which has not prioritized AI regulation; general laws (e.g., on telecommunications and cybersecurity) may incidentally touch digital technology but contain no AI-specific provisions.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments of any kind—binding or non-binding—have been adopted. There are no AI sandboxes, dedicated funding programs, or institutional bodies with an AI remit.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralized under a military transitional authority with no distributed AI regulatory structure, no formal coordination mechanisms across bodies for AI, no subnational AI role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI governance domestically; Burkina Faso's junta government has largely withdrawn from multilateral frameworks and its engagement with African Union AI initiatives is nominal or non-existent as of mid-2026.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Burkina Faso.

Regulation Status: Burundi has no AI-specific legislation, national AI strategy, or any binding regulatory framework addressing artificial intelligence as of mid-2026. No draft AI laws have been identified in the legislative pipeline, and general laws do not explicitly address AI systems.
Policy Lever: No policy levers of any kind — binding instruments, voluntary guidelines, funding programs, or dedicated institutions — exist for AI governance in Burundi. The country lacks the institutional infrastructure to develop or enforce such mechanisms.
Governance Type: There is no identifiable AI governance structure at any level in Burundi; no lead authority, no inter-agency coordination, no subnational role, and no independent or judicial oversight mechanism specific to AI has been established.
Actor Involvement: Burundi has no structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy processes, and participates minimally or not at all in international AI governance forums or multilateral digital policy initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, designated authority, sanctions, or monitoring practice for AI exists in Burundi.

Regulation Status: Cambodia has no binding AI legislation as of June 2026. The Draft National AI Strategy 2025–2030 remains under consultation (MPTC's April 2026 stakeholder workshop gathered input on a separate draft MPTC AI Promotion Strategy), and the Law on Personal Data Protection had not been enacted as of February 2026. Cambodia's only relevant instruments are draft documents still in a pre-legislative consultation phase, with no enacted binding instruments of any kind covering AI.
Policy Lever: Cambodia has no enacted binding instruments; the primary soft-law output is the Draft National AI Strategy 2025–2030 prepared by MPTC with UN-ESCAP support, plus a UNESCO-backed AI Readiness Assessment report. No AI-specific funding programs, sandboxes, or dedicated AI institutions have been established, and the MPTC leads AI coordination without dedicated AI staff or resources.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC) is the single lead body for AI policy with no parallel sectoral AI regulators. An Inter-Ministerial Working Group on AI is referenced in the draft strategy's implementation plan, suggesting nascent ad hoc inter-agency coordination, but no formal coordination mechanism is operative. Cambodia is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role and no court or ombudsman with an active AI remit.
Actor Involvement: Industry, civil society, and academia have been invited to consultative workshops on the draft national strategy and the draft MPTC AI Promotion Strategy (approximately 80 participants at the April 2026 workshop), but these are one-off consultations without standing formal roles. Internationally, Cambodia participates in ASEAN AI governance frameworks, engaged UNESCAP for strategy formulation, and sent representation to the World Governments Summit 2026, constituting signatory-level multilateral engagement.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, penalties, or dedicated AI authority exists; with neither the National AI Strategy nor the Personal Data Protection Law enacted, there is no legal basis for AI-specific enforcement actions or monitoring.
Specific Laws: Draft National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025–2030 (2025, not yet adopted), Draft Law on Personal Data Protection (2025, not yet enacted), Draft MPTC AI Promotion Strategy (2026, not yet adopted)

Regulation Status: Cameroon has no binding AI-specific legislation or AI bill in the legislative process as of June 2026. The National AI Strategy (SNIA), unveiled in July 2025, is a non-binding policy roadmap proposing a future AI authority and framework law, while Law No. 2024/017 on personal data protection (December 2024) touches AI incidentally but is general-purpose and only entering its enforcement period; no AI-specific binding instrument exists.
Policy Lever: The only policy instrument with any AI relevance is the non-binding SNIA strategy (2025) and the general data protection law (2024); no AI-specific binding instruments, sandboxes, or dedicated funding programs exist, and the proposed AI authority and Presidential Council on AI remain unestablished.
Governance Type: AI governance is effectively absent institutionally: the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications manages digital policy broadly, existing bodies ANTIC and ART cover cybersecurity and telecoms respectively without AI-specific mandates, there is no formal inter-agency coordination on AI, and no subnational or independent non-governmental AI oversight role exists.
Actor Involvement: Domestic stakeholder involvement in AI policy-making is minimal, with no standing industry working groups, civil society consultation mechanisms, or formal academic advisory roles evident; Cameroon's international engagement is limited to participation in African Union continental AI strategy discussions and membership in multilateral forums as a state party.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework in Cameroon: no penalties for AI-specific obligations have been defined, no enforcement actions taken, no authority holds an AI enforcement remit, and no monitoring or audit practice for AI exists.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy - SNIA (2025), Law No. 2024/017 of 23 December 2024 on Personal Data Protection

Regulation Status: Canada has no binding federal AI legislation in force after AIDA was abandoned when Parliament prorogued in January 2025 and the Carney government declared it 'off the table as drafted.' Only provincial laws (Ontario Bill 194 for public-sector AI disclosure in hiring, Quebec Law 25 for privacy/automated decisions) provide binding AI-relevant rules in limited domains, leaving Canada reliant on voluntary federal guidance and fragmented subnational coverage rather than any horizontal or dedicated federal AI statute.
Policy Lever: Federal policy rests on voluntary Treasury Board Directive on Automated Decision-Making and the AI Impact Assessment tool, supplemented by the new Canadian AI Safety Institute (funded from a CAD $2.4B AI investment envelope) for frontier model evaluation; binding instruments exist only at the provincial level. The new Ministry of AI and Digital Innovation signals intent to build institutional capacity, but no comprehensive compliance infrastructure is yet operational at the federal level.
Governance Type: No single federal AI regulator exists; the Office of the Privacy Commissioner handles data-related AI issues, the Canadian AI Safety Institute evaluates frontier models, and the new Ministry of AI and Digital Innovation holds a coordination mandate, creating a fragmented multi-body landscape. Provinces regulate AI-adjacent matters independently (Ontario, Quebec), giving subnational actors genuine independent regulatory authority rather than merely implementing federal rules.
Actor Involvement: Canada conducted broad multi-stakeholder consultations during the AIDA legislative process and continues to engage industry, civil society, and academia through the AI Safety Institute and standards bodies like CEN-CENELEC alignment work. Internationally, Canada is exceptionally active—co-chairing GPAI, participating in OECD AI Policy Observatory, G7 Hiroshima AI Process, and the International Network of AI Safety Institutes—placing it at the global frontier of international AI governance engagement.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement of AI-specific obligations is weak federally: provincial laws carry penalties (e.g., Quebec Law 25 fines up to CAD $25M), but there is no federal AI-specific sanctions framework, no dedicated federal AI enforcement authority with explicit remit and resources, and monitoring is limited to voluntary AI Impact Assessments and occasional OPC investigations rather than routine audits.
Specific Laws: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (2019, amended 2023), Quebec Law 25 / Act to modernize legislative provisions as regards the protection of personal information (2021), Ontario Bill 194 / Strengthening Cyber Security and Building Trust in the Public Sector Act (2024), Artificial Intelligence and Data Act / Bill C-27 (proposed, died on Order Paper January 2025)

Regulation Status: Cape Verde has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI regulatory framework, and no national AI strategy as of June 2026. The country's digital governance relies on general ICT frameworks without any AI-specific provisions, leaving AI regulation entirely absent at the national level.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or funding programs exist. Cape Verde lacks any institutional body with an AI mandate or compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure at any level in Cape Verde; no designated regulator, no coordination mechanism, no subnational role, and no independent review body with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: Domestic actors—industry, civil society, and academia—show no structured involvement in AI policy development; Cape Verde participates passively in the African Union and Smart Africa Alliance frameworks, providing minimal international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanisms exist in Cape Verde.

Regulation Status: The Central African Republic has no AI-specific legislation, regulatory framework, or binding instruments of any kind as of June 2026. Ongoing armed conflict, extreme institutional fragility, and very limited state capacity mean no AI governance activity has been initiated or proposed.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments exist in any form; the country lacks the administrative and financial capacity to develop, adopt, or implement any such mechanisms.
Governance Type: There are no AI regulatory bodies, coordination mechanisms, subnational governance roles, or independent oversight functions; the state itself has severely limited functional reach across much of the territory.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy has been documented, and the CAR does not actively participate in international or African Union AI governance initiatives despite nominal AU membership.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists with respect to AI.

Regulation Status: Chad has no AI-specific legislation, regulatory framework, or binding instruments of any kind as of June 2026. The military-led transitional government has not initiated any AI policy process, and no AI-relevant law or executive order exists.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments exist in Chad—no binding rules, guidance documents, national AI strategy, dedicated funding programs, or institutional bodies with an AI mandate have been established.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure in Chad at any level of government; no regulatory authority, coordination mechanism, subnational body, or independent review process addresses AI.
Actor Involvement: No domestic or international actors—industry, civil society, academia, or multilateral bodies—are engaged in any AI policy process in Chad; the country has not participated in AU or other international AI governance initiatives in any meaningful way.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice for AI exists in Chad.

Regulation Status: Chile's dedicated AI bill (Boletín 16821-19), approved by the Chamber of Deputies on 13 October 2025, remains under Senate review as of June 2026 with no fixed enactment date, so no horizontal AI-specific statute is yet binding. However, Law 21.663 (cybersecurity, in force January 2025) and the enacted but not yet effective Law 21.719 (data protection, effective December 2026) create binding instruments directly relevant to AI systems. The AI bill is explicitly dedicated to AI governance with a four-tier risk framework modelled on the EU AI Act, giving Chile a high ai_specificity score despite non-enactment.
Policy Lever: One binding AI-adjacent instrument (Law 21.663 on cybersecurity) is enforceable, while the main AI bill and GDPR-style data protection law remain prospective; SERNAC's 2023 consumer protection circular on AI provides existing soft-law guidance alongside the Santiago Declaration ethics framework. Institutional capacity is thin: the Personal Data Protection Agency is mandated by Law 21.719 but not yet operational, and no dedicated AI supervisory authority exists yet.
Governance Type: Governance is led nationally by the Ministry of Science coordinating with SERNAC and the nascent ANCI (cybersecurity), with the draft AI bill proposing a formal AI authority—a lead-plus-sectoral structure, but coordination remains ad hoc rather than formalised. Chilean courts have already shaped AI-adjacent outcomes (Supreme Court Worldcoin ruling, January 2025), providing meaningful judicial checks, while subnational entities play no independent AI regulatory role in Chile's unitary system.
Actor Involvement: The AI bill process included published multi-stakeholder consultations engaging industry, civil society (including NGOs raising rights concerns), and academic experts in an advisory capacity, though none hold standing formal roles. Internationally, Chile is highly active: OECD member, host of the 2023 Santiago Declaration establishing the regional AI ethics framework, and leader of the Intergovernmental Council on AI for Latin America and the Caribbean, with subsequent ministerial summits institutionalised annually.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is embryonic: ANCI can levy fines up to 40,000 UTM under Law 21.663 for cybersecurity breaches (including AI-related incidents) and has designated 732 Operators of Vital Importance, but the main AI-specific penalty framework remains in the draft bill; SERNAC retains limited AI-adjacent consumer protection enforcement until December 2026, and the Supreme Court's Worldcoin ruling demonstrates occasional judicial action, yet routine AI-specific monitoring and published enforcement actions do not yet exist.
Specific Laws: AI Systems Regulation Bill (Boletín 16821-19, Chamber-approved October 2025, pending Senate), Law 21.719 on Protection of Personal Data (enacted December 2024, effective December 2026), Law 21.663 on Cybersecurity Framework (2024, in force January 2025), National AI Policy/Strategy (2021), Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI in Latin America and the Caribbean (2023)

Regulation Status: China has enacted multiple dedicated, binding AI-specific regulations fully in force: the Algorithm Recommendation Provisions (2022), Deep Synthesis (deepfake) Provisions (2022), Generative AI Service Measures (2023), mandatory AI content labeling standard GB 45438-2025 (effective September 2025), and an amended Cybersecurity Law with explicit AI provisions (effective January 2026). Coverage is horizontal across sectors including content platforms, finance, healthcare, and critical infrastructure, with over 100 generative AI services formally approved under the licensing regime by mid-2025. All major instruments are fully operational with detailed secondary rules, security assessment requirements, and algorithm filing obligations in place.
Policy Lever: China deploys multiple binding instruments (licensing, mandatory labeling, security assessments, algorithm filing, data localization, cybersecurity review) alongside a mature suite of GB national standards and TC260 technical guidance, and strategic economic tools including the AI Plus Action Plan (August 2025), semiconductor industrial policy, and state-backed AI investment funds. CAC, MIIT, and SAMR maintain staffed, operational compliance infrastructure with pre-launch approval pipelines and dedicated enforcement arms.
Governance Type: Governance is led by the CAC as the dominant authority, with secondary roles for MIIT, SAMR, and the TC260 standards body, constituting a lead-body-plus-sectoral-regulators structure rather than truly plural independent regulators. Coordination occurs through inter-agency processes but is largely administrative rather than formalized by statute, and subnational governments implement national rules without independent regulatory authority; independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI governance decisions is structurally absent.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates through formal comment periods, pilot programs, and standards working groups, but civil society and independent NGOs are effectively excluded from domestic AI governance processes; academic input is limited to occasional advisory roles within state-affiliated institutions. Internationally, China is highly active, leading AI governance at BRICS, advancing the Global AI Governance Initiative (30+ adherents), co-signing the Bletchley Declaration, and driving standard-setting at ISO and ITU.
Enforcement Level: China has a comprehensive penalty framework covering fines, service suspension, and criminal referral across all major AI regulations, with the CAC conducting published enforcement actions including takedowns and fines against non-compliant generative AI services and algorithm operators; dedicated enforcement units exist within CAC with explicit AI remit, and routine algorithmic filing reviews and security assessments constitute systematic monitoring, though public reporting of individual enforcement actions remains selective.
Specific Laws: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions (2022), Internet Information Service Deep Synthesis Management Provisions (2022), Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (2023), AI Content Labeling Measures / GB 45438-2025 (effective September 2025), Cybersecurity Law Amendment with AI Provisions (effective January 2026), AI Plus Action Plan (2025), New Generation Artificial Intelligence Development Plan (2017)

Regulation Status: Colombia has no enacted AI-specific law as of June 2026. The government-backed AI bill (filed as Bill 422/2025 in early 2025, re-filed as PL 043-25/324-25-C in July 2025) was undergoing first-debate legislative review in mid-2026 but had not been passed.
