CONPES 4144 (February 2025) is a non-binding national AI policy document, while SIC External Circular No. 2 of 2024 provides binding guidance on personal-data processing in AI systems under existing Law 1581/2012, meaning AI is explicitly addressed within adapted general law.
Policy Lever: The primary operative instrument is SIC Circular 002/2024—a binding circular under data protection law addressing AI—alongside CONPES 4144 as a soft-law policy roadmap with a COP 479 billion action plan; comprehensive AI-specific legislation and dedicated sandbox programs remain pending or nascent.
Governance Type: AI governance is centrally coordinated by MinCiencias and MinTIC at the national level, with the SIC exercising enforcement authority over data-related AI issues; there is no dedicated AI regulatory body, formal cross-agency coordination mechanism, or meaningful subnational regulatory role.
Actor Involvement: The July 2025 AI bill was described as a government-industry-academia-civil society compact after broad multi-stakeholder consultation; Colombia is an OECD accession country, a signatory to the Santiago and Cartagena Declarations, and actively participates in OECD AI Policy Observatory discussions, reflecting substantive international engagement.
Enforcement Level: AI-related enforcement flows exclusively through existing data-protection powers—SIC can fine up to ~COP 3.5 billion under Law 1581 and has increased sanctions 22% in 2024, but there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority, no AI-specific penalty framework, and no systematic AI audit or monitoring regime.
Specific Laws: CONPES 4144 – National AI Policy (2025), Law 1581/2012 – Data Protection Law (2012), SIC External Circular No. 2 of 2024 – AI Personal Data Processing Guidelines (2024), Bill PL 043-25/324-25-C – AI Regulation Bill (pending, 2025-2026)

Regulation Status: Comoros has no AI-specific legislation, binding rules, or formal proposals as of June 2026. No AI governance framework of any kind exists at the national level, and no legislative process addressing AI has been initiated.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any kind — binding or non-binding — exist for AI governance in Comoros. There are no dedicated bodies, guidelines, sandboxes, or funding programs related to AI.
Governance Type: No governance structures for AI have been established in Comoros; there is no designated ministry, agency, or inter-agency task force, and no sub-national or independent bodies play any AI oversight role.
Actor Involvement: No domestic actors — industry, civil society, or academia — have been mobilized around AI governance in Comoros, and while the country holds AU membership, it has not engaged substantively with continental or international AI governance processes.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, designated authority, penalties, or monitoring activity related to AI in Comoros.

Regulation Status: The Republic of Congo has no AI-specific legislation, regulatory framework, or formal governance structures as of June 2026. No binding AI rules have been drafted, proposed, or enacted at any level of government, and AI regulation remains absent from national policy priorities.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments of any kind exist in Congo — no binding rules, no published guidelines or codes of conduct, no funding programs or sandboxes, and no dedicated institutional bodies for AI governance.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance architecture in the Republic of Congo; no regulator, coordinating body, subnational mechanism, or independent review process addresses AI. The absence of any AI governance structure means all descriptive sub-indicators default to their lowest value.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI governance has been documented domestically, and Congo shows no active participation in continental or multilateral AI governance initiatives beyond nominal African Union membership.
Enforcement Level: With no AI regulatory framework in place, there are no penalties, no enforcement actions, no designated authority, and no monitoring or auditing practices related to AI.

Regulation Status: Costa Rica has three AI-specific draft bills before the Legislative Assembly (Expedientes 23771, 23919, 24484) and a National AI Strategy (ENIA 2024–2027) with an August 2025 Action Plan, but none of the AI bills has been enacted into law. The only binding instruments remain the general data-protection Law 8968 and the March 2026 voluntary technical standard INTE/ISO/IEC 42001:2026, meaning there is no binding, cross-sector AI legislation in force.
Policy Lever: Costa Rica relies on soft-law tools: the ENIA 2024–2027 strategy, its 2025 Action Plan, and the voluntary INTE/ISO/IEC 42001:2026 standard provide published guidance, while MICITT coordinates AI policy without a dedicated funded AI authority; no binding AI-specific instrument exists and economic programs remain nascent.
Governance Type: MICITT leads AI policy with PRODHAB handling data-protection enforcement; coordination between agencies is described as ad hoc rather than formalized, Costa Rica is a unitary state with no subnational AI role, and judicial or ombudsman review of AI has been only occasional.
Actor Involvement: The ENIA was developed with public sector, private sector, civil society, and academic input via structured consultations; Costa Rica is an OECD member adhering to OECD AI Principles, participates in the Cartagena and Santiago Declaration frameworks, and actively leads Central American regional AI governance dialogue.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists; PRODHAB enforces general data-protection law but has taken no published AI-specific enforcement actions, and no routine AI monitoring or auditing regime is in place.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy ENIA (2024), ENIA Action Plan (2025), INTE/ISO/IEC 42001:2026 (2026), Law No. 8968 – Protection in the Handling of Personal Data (2011), Bill 23771 – Ley de Regulación de la Inteligencia Artificial en Costa Rica (pending), Bill 23919 – Ley para la Promoción Responsable de la Inteligencia Artificial en Costa Rica (pending), Bill 24484 – Ley para la Implementación de Sistemas de Inteligencia Artificial (pending)

Regulation Status: Croatia is subject to the directly applicable EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), which entered into force in August 2024 with prohibited-practices provisions binding from February 2025 and most high-risk obligations reaching full application in August 2026. The binding force and horizontal scope derive from the EU Regulation itself; Croatia's national implementing legislation is still being drafted by an inter-ministerial working group, meaning some secondary rules and formal authority designations remain incomplete. The AI Act is AI-specific and horizontal, but Croatia has not yet finalized its own national AI-specific statute or the National Plan for Development of AI (2026–2028).
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act is the single binding instrument; Croatia has no additional national binding AI laws. AZOP published limited guidance, notably hosting a June 2025 workshop on Fundamental Rights Impact Assessments under Article 27 of the AI Act, but a mature suite of standards or codes is not yet in place. The inter-ministerial working group and AZOP provide some institutional capacity, though the bodies remain thinly resourced and the national implementing legislation is still pending.
Governance Type: Croatia's AI governance involves AZOP as the anticipated lead, a multi-ministerial working group chaired by the Ministry of Justice, Administration and Digital Transformation, and up to seven identified sector-level market surveillance authorities, indicating a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure; coordination is primarily through the working group rather than a formal statutory mechanism. Croatia is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role, and courts and the ombudsperson's office provide occasional independent checks.
Actor Involvement: The national working group formally includes representatives from key ministries, AZOP, ombudsperson offices, academia, and industry, providing structured but not standing formal roles. Internationally, Croatia participates in the EU AI Board, co-led a joint EU consultation on LLMs with Germany's data protection authority (BfDI), and engages in Southeast European digital cooperation frameworks.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's penalty framework (up to €35M or 7% of global turnover for the most serious violations) applies in Croatia, but formal national designation of AI market surveillance authorities is still in progress and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken; AZOP is a proven GDPR enforcer (€10.5M in fines in 2024, €7M in 2025) and has issued FRIA guidance, but dedicated AI monitoring and audit routines are not yet established.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Digital Croatia Strategy 2032, National Roadmap for Digital Decade Policy, Act on Implementation of DSA (2025), National Plan for Development of Artificial Intelligence 2026-2028 (draft)

Regulation Status: Cuba adopted the Estrategia para el Desarrollo y Uso de la Inteligencia Artificial in May 2024 as part of the Política para la Transformación Digital, approved by the Council of Ministers. This is a strategic and policy document rather than binding legislation; no dedicated AI law has been enacted, so there is no binding force. The 2023 Personal Data Protection Law (Ley 149) and Decree Law 35 (2021) incidentally touch AI-relevant areas but are general-purpose instruments. Implementation remains at the strategy and early rollout phase rather than fully operational binding rules.
Policy Lever: The 2024 AI Strategy provides soft-law guidance covering ethics, legal norms, and risk management but no binding AI-specific instruments exist. The Ministry of Communications (MINCOM) is designated as the governing body, and the Consorcio de Inteligencia Artificial (launched November 2025) brings together universities, state enterprises, and private actors under MINCOM/Havana Science Park coordination, representing nascent but thinly resourced institutional capacity without dedicated enforcement infrastructure.
Governance Type: Cuba maintains a highly centralized governance model with MINCOM as the single authority over AI policy; the 2025 AI Consortium represents an initial cross-institutional coordination mechanism but is not an independent regulator. There is no subnational AI governance role, and no independent courts or ombudsmen actively shape AI rules in Cuba's single-party system.
Actor Involvement: The AI Consortium includes state enterprises (ETECSA, DATYS, XETID), universities (UH, CUJAE, UCI), and the Union of Cuban Informatics as a civil society representative, reflecting structured but state-controlled participation. Internationally, Cuba signed the Santiago Declaration on AI (2023) and engages via UNESCO and CELAC forums, placing it at the signatory/declaratory level of international participation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, or monitoring mechanisms have been established; MINCOM is designated as governing body in the 2024 strategy but without a dedicated enforcement mandate or documented enforcement actions against AI deployments.
Specific Laws: Política para la Transformación Digital, Agenda Digital Cubana y Estrategia para el Desarrollo y Uso de la Inteligencia Artificial (2024), Ley 149 de Protección de Datos Personales (2023), Decreto Ley 35 de Telecomunicaciones (2021)

Regulation Status: Cyprus, as an EU member state, has the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) in force as directly applicable law, covering all sectors horizontally with prohibitions operative since February 2025 and GPAI/market surveillance obligations from August 2025; however, the full high-risk AI system obligations do not become mandatory until August 2026, placing Cyprus in a transitional implementation period. Cyprus issued formal governance decisions in January 2025 designating competent authorities, indicating dedicated AI-specific institutional arrangements within the EU framework rather than purely incidental general law coverage.
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act constitutes the single binding instrument applicable in Cyprus, supplemented by the existing GDPR framework; national soft-law guidance from Cypriot authorities remains nascent, though the government has launched an €5 million 'AI for Government' scheme and is developing the Pharos-CY AI Factory under EU programmes. The Commissioner for Communications and the Commissioner for Personal Data Protection have been designated as competent authorities but remain thinly resourced relative to the scale of obligations.
Governance Type: Cyprus uses a compact two-authority model: the Commissioner of Communications serves as Notifying Authority, primary Market Surveillance Authority, and Single Point of Contact, while the Commissioner for Personal Data Protection covers its competence area, with the Deputy Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digital Policy acting as national coordinator and EU AI Board representative. There is no sub-national governance layer given Cyprus's unitary structure, and coordination mechanisms are largely nascent rather than formally institutionalised.
Actor Involvement: Domestic stakeholder engagement is limited, with a National AI Taskforce established in 2025 to develop the new national AI strategy but no standing formal roles for industry, civil society, or academia in the policy process. Cyprus participates actively in EU-level governance through the European AI Board and is aligned with EU international AI diplomacy efforts including the Bletchley/Seoul/Paris AI Safety Summit declarations.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's penalty framework (up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover for prohibited AI violations) is directly applicable in Cyprus, but no national enforcement actions have been publicly recorded as of mid-2026, with designated authorities established but lacking published monitoring reports or audit programmes specific to AI compliance.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), EU AI Liability Directive (proposed), GDPR / Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (2016), Cyprus National Digital Decade Strategic Roadmap (2021), Cyprus AI Strategy (2020)

Regulation Status: Czechia applies the EU AI Act directly as binding law and has enacted sector-specific AI-adjacent statutes (police biometric ID amendment in force August 2025, autonomous vehicles amendment in force January 2026, Cybersecurity Act No. 265/2025 Coll.), while a dedicated national AI implementing Act (the Draft Act, designating CTO as primary market surveillance authority and creating a sandbox) was still moving through Parliament as of mid-2026. The EU AI Act's horizontal obligations are in force but full national enforcement infrastructure has not yet reached the operational stage, keeping implementation at a transitional level.
Policy Lever: Czechia relies on the EU AI Act as its primary binding instrument, supplemented by sector-specific legislation and the 2024 National AI Strategy 2030; CZK 232 million was earmarked (2026–2028) for implementation, an AI Competence Centre for eGovernment is being established, and a regulatory sandbox is mandated but not yet fully operational.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Industry and Trade leads AI implementation with the Czech Telecommunications Office as the designated primary market surveillance authority, plus sector authorities (ÚOOÚ for data, Office for Technical Standardisation for conformity assessment); coordination occurs through the Committee for AI under the inter-ministerial Committee for a Digital Economy and Society, though formal multi-body coordination mechanisms are still maturing and Czechia is a unitary state with no sub-national AI regulatory role.
Actor Involvement: The 2024 NAIS 2030 update was developed through a working group under the AI Committee that included ministries, social and economic partners, academic institutions, and non-profit organisations; Czechia is active in the EU AI Board, held the EU Council Presidency that advanced AI Act negotiations, and participates in OECD AI governance, reflecting strong international engagement.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act penalty regime applies in Czechia, and the Draft National AI Act defines administrative offences; the Czech Telecommunications Office is designated as market surveillance authority but lacks full operational resources pending adoption of the Draft Act, and formal AI-specific enforcement actions remain very limited (one notable court decision in 2023), with routine monitoring and public reporting not yet established.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Czech Republic 2030 (2024), Czech Cybersecurity Act No. 265/2025 Coll. (2025), Amendment to Act on Police of the Czech Republic No. 273/2008 Coll. – biometric ID at airports (in force August 2025), Amendment to Road Traffic Act No. 361/2000 Coll. – autonomous vehicles SAE Level 3 (in force January 2026), Draft Act on Artificial Intelligence (expected adoption 2026), AI Implementation Plan of the EU AI Act in the Czech Republic

Regulation Status: Côte d'Ivoire has no binding AI-specific legislation in force. The National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA 2030), officially launched on 13 March 2025, is a non-binding policy document built around investment, inclusion, and governance pillars. General digital laws (Law 2013-450 on personal data, Law 2017-803 on the Information Society) incidentally touch AI but no dedicated AI statute has been enacted or introduced in the legislature. A 2035 Digital Strategy unveiled in June 2026 lists AI as one of seven pillars but again remains a strategic declaration rather than binding law.
Policy Lever: Policy levers consist entirely of soft instruments: the SNIA 2030 strategy and a complementary National Data Governance Strategy (SNGD 2030, April 2025) provide directional guidance, and a proposed 'Safe AI Côte d'Ivoire' label signals intent for future compliance tools. Economic programs are nascent (a $1.8 billion national digital strategy envelope was cited, but AI-specific funding programs are not operationalized), and institutional capacity sits with ARTCI and the Ministry of Digital Transition, which have designated but thinly resourced roles.
Governance Type: Governance is effectively centralised under the Ministry of Digital Transition and Technological Innovation, with ARTCI playing a supporting regulatory role for digital/telecom matters; no dedicated AI agency exists. There is no meaningful subnational AI governance role, and formal cross-body coordination on AI remains ad hoc rather than institutionalised.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society were consulted during the preparation of the SNIA 2030 (the strategy notes inclusive development objectives) but lack standing formal roles. Côte d'Ivoire participates in the African Union Continental AI Strategy and the Smart Africa Alliance, and endorsed the May 2025 AU High-Level Policy Dialogue communiqué, placing it at signatory-to-declarations level internationally.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions regime, or dedicated supervisory authority exists; the SNIA 2030 and SNGD 2030 are purely strategic documents with no penalty provisions, and no monitoring or audit practice for AI has been established.
Specific Laws: Stratégie Nationale de l'Intelligence Artificielle (SNIA 2030, 2025), Stratégie Nationale de Gouvernance des Données (SNGD 2030, 2025), Loi 2013-450 relative à la protection des données à caractère personnel (2013), Loi 2017-803 sur l'orientation de la société de l'information (2017)

Regulation Status: The DRC has no AI-specific legislation, strategy, or regulatory framework of any kind as of mid-2026. Persistent institutional fragility, active armed conflict in eastern regions, and severely limited digital infrastructure continue to preclude any AI policy development; only general telecommunications and ICT laws exist and these do not address AI.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments of any type exist in the DRC; there are no binding rules, published guidance, sandboxes, or dedicated AI bodies, and general ICT regulatory capacity remains extremely weak.
Governance Type: Digital and ICT policy is nominally overseen by the Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and New Information Technologies (MPTNTIC) as a single, under-resourced body with no AI remit; there is no multi-regulator landscape, no formal coordination mechanism, no subnational AI role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic engagement involving industry, civil society, or academia in AI governance exists; the DRC holds nominal African Union membership and thus is nominally exposed to AU AI initiatives such as the AU AI Continental Strategy, but demonstrates no active documented participation in international AI governance forums or standard-setting processes.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no designated enforcement authority, no recorded enforcement actions, and no monitoring or auditing activity of any kind related to AI systems in the DRC.

Regulation Status: Denmark enacted Law No. 467 of 14 May 2025 (Act on Supplementary Provisions to the Regulation on Artificial Intelligence), becoming the first EU member state to implement national AI Act legislation, with the act applicable from 1 July 2025. The EU AI Act applies directly as horizontal cross-sector binding regulation covering all risk tiers; full high-risk provisions enter force August 2026. Implementation is substantially operational — prohibited-practice provisions active since February 2025, GPAI rules since August 2025 — though some high-risk annexes phase in through 2026-2027, keeping implementation just below fully complete.
Policy Lever: Denmark combines the directly applicable EU AI Act with a national supplementary act providing enforcement powers (injunctions, bans, administrative fines), plus Datatilsynet's extensive compliance guidance and 13+ updated national guidance documents published in 2025. Economic tools include AI-Boost (EDIH centred on AI), GUDP R&D funding, and digital innovation hub support for SMEs, representing a moderate but active suite of programs.
Governance Type: A lead body (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen/Agency for Digital Government) coordinates market surveillance alongside Datatilsynet and the Danish Court Administration, with a formal multi-authority framework established under Law No. 467; subnational actors such as municipalities implement national rules through KL guidance but do not independently regulate AI. Courts and the ombudsman provide occasional independent review.
Actor Involvement: Denmark engages industry and civil society through published consultations and the Joint Government Digital Strategy 2026-2029 process, with universities (e.g., University of Copenhagen AI Package) contributing advisory input; Denmark is highly active internationally via the EU AI Board, Nordic cooperation, OECD.AI, and GPAI.
Enforcement Level: A comprehensive penalty framework exists under Law No. 467 including administrative fines (bødeforelæg) and broader EU AI Act penalties, with Datatilsynet's 2026 supervisory priorities targeting AI in healthcare monitoring; however, routine published AI-specific enforcement actions are not yet established given the framework's recent entry into force.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Act on Supplementary Provisions to the Regulation on Artificial Intelligence / Lov nr. 467 af 14. maj 2025 (2025), Danish Data Protection Act (2018), Joint Government Digital Strategy 2026-2029

Regulation Status: Djibouti has no dedicated AI legislation, strategy, or regulatory framework as of June 2026. No binding AI rules have been drafted or enacted, and no AI-specific instruments of any kind are documented. General ICT governance remains rudimentary, with no AI coverage in any sector.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidelines, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist in Djibouti. There are no documented funding programs, procurement rules, or soft-law guidance targeting artificial intelligence.
Governance Type: AI governance responsibilities are entirely absent as a distinct function; no lead body, sectoral regulator, or coordination mechanism has been established. Djibouti is a highly centralized unitary state with no subnational regulatory role and no independent review mechanism for AI.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy development is documented in Djibouti, and the country has not been identified as a signatory or active participant in any international AI governance declaration or standards body.
Enforcement Level: With no AI legal framework in place, there are no penalties defined, no enforcement actions taken, no authority designated for AI oversight, and no monitoring or audit activity of any kind.

Regulation Status: Dominica has no dedicated AI legislation, regulatory framework, or national AI strategy as of mid-2026. The country's engagement with AI governance remains limited to participation in regional CARICOM mechanisms; no binding AI rules have been drafted or enacted at the national level.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist in Dominica; the country relies entirely on general existing legislation with no AI-specific adaptation.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated AI governance structure, oversight body, or assigned ministry in Dominica; no formal coordination mechanisms, subnational roles, or independent review processes for AI exist.
Actor Involvement: No domestic stakeholder engagement mechanisms—industry, civil society, or academic—have been established for AI policy; Dominica's only international AI governance participation is as a CARICOM member state that endorsed the Santiago Declaration.
Enforcement Level: There are no AI-specific sanctions, enforcement actions, designated enforcement authority, or monitoring practices in Dominica.

Regulation Status: The Dominican Republic has no enacted binding AI legislation as of mid-2026; an AI bill (introduced in the Senate by Senator Rafael Duluc and a separate bill by Congressman Tobías Crespo in February 2025) remains under committee study and has not been passed. The country's primary AI instrument is the National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (ENIA, 2023), which is a policy document without legal force, supplemented by participation in the Santiago and Cartagena regional declarations.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are confined to soft instruments: the ENIA (2023) sets strategic priorities, and the country has signed regional declarations; a Memorandum of Understanding with NVIDIA was reported in 2025, and OGTIC leads implementation with thin resources, citing weak inter-institutional coordination and resource constraints.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI regulatory body exists; OGTIC (Government Office for ICT) and the Cabinet of Innovation and Digital Development hold de facto AI governance leadership without a multi-regulator structure, and coordination among institutions is described by the government itself as weak. The judiciary has no active AI oversight role.
Actor Involvement: The ENIA was developed with input from public institutions, academia, and the private sector (including a CAF-funded consultancy), but structured civil society and industry participation remains ad hoc rather than institutionalized; internationally, the Dominican Republic participates in PARLATINO model law discussions and hosted the 3rd Ministerial Summit on AI Ethics.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, or dedicated authority exists; all existing instruments are non-binding strategies or pending bills, making any monitoring or enforcement action impossible at this stage.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy – ENIA (2023), AI Bill submitted to Dominican Senate (2023, still in committee), AI Bill submitted by Congressman Tobías Crespo (February 2025, still in committee), National Innovation Policy 2030 (2022)

Regulation Status: East Timor (Timor-Leste) has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules proposed or enacted, and no AI governance framework of any kind. The country's regulatory environment remains focused on foundational digital and telecommunications infrastructure, and AI governance is not an active legislative priority as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments of any type exist — no binding rules, no published guidance or codes of practice, no AI-specific funding or sandbox programs, and no dedicated institutional body for AI oversight.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure in Timor-Leste; no single authority, no inter-agency coordination mechanism, no subnational AI role, and no independent review body or court engagement on AI matters.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy exists domestically, and Timor-Leste has not participated in international AI governance declarations or multilateral AI standards processes.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, no designated authority, no sanctions, and no monitoring or auditing activity related to AI in Timor-Leste.

Regulation Status: As of mid-2026, Ecuador has enacted no AI-specific legislation and no binding AI rules are in force or formally in the legislative pipeline. The country continues to rely on the 2021 Organic Law of Personal Data Protection and general telecommunications regulations as indirect, non-AI-targeted frameworks, with no horizontal or sectoral AI statute drafted.
Policy Lever: Ecuador has no dedicated AI binding instruments, no published AI-specific guidance or codes of practice, no AI sandbox or public funding programs, and no designated institution with an AI mandate; digital governance remains fragmented across SUPERTEL and the data protection authority without AI-specific capacity.
Governance Type: Digital and data governance responsibilities are split across SUPERTEL and the data protection authority but without any formal coordination mechanism for AI; there is no subnational AI regulatory role and judicial review of AI-related matters remains incidental and rare.
Actor Involvement: Ecuador lacks structured domestic multi-stakeholder processes involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy; internationally, Ecuador participates in regional Latin American digital governance discussions and engages with OECD digital policy frameworks at a declaratory level.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, designated enforcement authority, documented enforcement actions, or systematic monitoring of AI systems exists in Ecuador as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales (2021)

Regulation Status: Egypt has published its second National AI Strategy (2025–2030), an Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023), and a March 2026 Guide to Egypt's National AI Governance Framework with a four-tier risk classification and mandatory compliance pathways for high-risk systems — but none of these constitute enacted legislation. The Draft AI Law remains in the parliamentary pipeline, meaning the only binding instruments touching AI are the Personal Data Protection Law No.151/2020 and the Cloud First Policy (2024), which are not AI-specific. AI is explicitly addressed across dedicated (non-binding) instruments, justifying a 3 on specificity.
Policy Lever: Egypt's policy toolkit is dominated by soft law: the AI Charter, the National Guidelines for Trustworthy and Responsible AI, and the March 2026 Governance Framework Guide form a growing suite, while binding instruments remain limited to the PDPL and Cloud First Policy. Economic tools include the 'Our Future is Digital' training initiative (100,000 beneficiaries), AI research partnerships, and the Egyptian AI Institute, and institutions such as the NCAI and the Egyptian Center for Responsible AI (ECRAI) are designated and operational though thinly resourced without statutory enforcement powers.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralised under the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, with the NCAI as the apex coordinating body and ECRAI operationalising compliance — a lead-body-plus-sectoral-regulator (NTRA) structure with limited independent regulatory plurality. Egypt is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance role, and while civil society actors like Masaar participate in public debate and there is a general judiciary, no independent review mechanism specifically for AI has been established.
Actor Involvement: The NCAI includes private-sector representatives and consultations for strategy development engaged industry and academia (including university AI departments and the Egyptian AI Institute), but civil society organisations such as Masaar lack formal standing roles in policymaking. Egypt is internationally active — participating in UNESCO AI readiness assessments, hosting the OECD AI Review, adhering to OECD AI Principles, and engaging with African Union AI governance frameworks.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific penalty framework exists in force; the March 2026 Governance Framework mandates ethical impact assessments and TEVV audits for high-risk AI, and ECRAI is designated to issue compliance certificates, but it lacks statutory enforcement powers pending the Draft AI Law, and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been recorded.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy First Edition (2021), Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023), National AI Strategy Second Edition (2025-2030), Personal Data Protection Law No.151/2020, Cloud First Policy (2024), Draft Artificial Intelligence Law (2024, pending), Guide to Egypt's National AI Governance Framework (2026)

Regulation Status: El Salvador enacted the Law for the Promotion of Artificial Intelligence and Technologies (Decreto Legislativo No. 234, published in the Diario Oficial No. 43 on 3 March 2025), a dedicated AI-specific statute covering IP rights for AI-generated outputs, a risk assessment framework, a national AI registry, and data protection requirements. The law is binding and in force but the implementing regulations and the newly created ANIA authority were still being established in their early operational phase as of mid-2026, making full implementation partial rather than complete.
Policy Lever: The 2025 AI Law is the sole binding instrument; it mandates ANIA to issue regulatory criteria within 90 days of enactment and establish a National Registry of AI developers, but secondary guidance, sandbox programs, or dedicated funding mechanisms are not yet publicly documented. ANIA was established as a decentralized presidential body (Resolución No. 0001/2025), though its staffing, budget, and compliance infrastructure remain nascent.
Governance Type: Governance is centralized around ANIA as the single lead authority attached to the Presidency, with the State Cybersecurity Agency (ACE) playing a secondary role for public-sector AI systems; no formal multi-regulator coordination mechanism or meaningful subnational role exists. Given El Salvador's restrictive Foreign Agents Law (2025) and the broader weakening of civic space, independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI decisions remains limited.
Actor Involvement: The AI Law was passed without documented public consultation; industry, civil society, and academia had no formal standing roles, and the passage of the 2025 Foreign Agents Law further curtails civil society engagement. El Salvador has engaged in regional AI dialogues (Montevideo Declaration on AI ethics in Latin America, 2024) and is subject to international scrutiny, providing modest international involvement.
Enforcement Level: The 2025 AI Law establishes ANIA as the designated enforcement authority and references a risk assessment framework, but no comprehensive penalty regime, documented enforcement actions, or routine monitoring or audit mechanisms have been reported as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: Ley de Fomento a la Inteligencia Artificial y Tecnologías, Decreto Legislativo No. 234 (2025)

Regulation Status: Equatorial Guinea has no documented AI-specific legislation, national strategy, or binding regulatory instrument of any kind as of mid-2026. No draft AI law has been reported in the legislative process, and no sector-specific AI rules exist.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any type—binding, voluntary, or economic—have been identified for AI governance. There are no dedicated bodies, sandboxes, or funding programs related to AI.
Governance Type: No AI governance architecture exists; there is no designated regulator, coordinating mechanism, subnational framework, or independent oversight body with any AI remit.
Actor Involvement: No structured engagement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy has been documented, and Equatorial Guinea has not been identified as an active participant in any international AI governance forum or declaration.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, no designated enforcement authority, and no recorded monitoring or enforcement actions relating to AI in Equatorial Guinea.

Regulation Status: Eritrea has no AI governance framework, no binding AI legislation, and no regulatory activity as of June 2026. The country's extreme internet restrictions, minimal digital infrastructure, and deep international isolation mean no AI-specific or AI-relevant legal instruments exist or are under development.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments of any kind exist in Eritrea — no binding rules, no published guidance, no funding programs, and no institutional body with any AI mandate.
Governance Type: Eritrea's highly centralized single-party state has no AI-specific regulatory structure; there are no sectoral regulators, no coordination mechanisms, no subnational governance role, and no independent judiciary or ombudsman capable of reviewing AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: Eritrea does not participate in any international AI governance forums, has no structured domestic industry or civil society engagement on AI, and its universities have no known role in AI policy; independent civil society organizations are effectively absent.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring activity related to AI exists in Eritrea.

Regulation Status: Estonia applies the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), which entered into force August 2024 with most major provisions becoming applicable on August 2, 2026 — placing Estonia at the global frontier for binding horizontal AI law as an EU member state. Estonia deliberately chose not to pursue standalone domestic AI legislation, relying instead on the directly applicable EU instrument supplemented by the Kratt AI and Data Action Plan 2024–2026 and the Data & AI White Paper 2024–2030. Implementation is partially in force (prohibited AI uses since Feb 2025, GPAI model obligations since Aug 2025, high-risk obligations activating Aug 2026), and the AI Act is a dedicated AI-specific horizontal instrument though not domestically authored.
Policy Lever: The primary binding instrument is the EU AI Act, supplemented by GDPR and sectoral laws; Estonia has not added binding national AI-specific instruments on top. Soft law is well-developed: the Kratt Action Plan 2024–2026, Data & AI White Paper 2024–2030, RIA AI risk assessment guidance, and TTJA public compliance guidance together form a substantial suite. Economic tools include the Eesti.ai national AI adoption programme, the AI Leap 2025 education initiative (with a dedicated AI Leap Foundation), and innovation-friendly regulatory sandboxes. Institutional capacity is being built — TTJA has been designated a competent authority and AKI retains data-related oversight, but dedicated AI enforcement resources remain thin and the designation structure is still being consolidated.
Governance Type: AI oversight is distributed across several bodies — MKM (policy lead), TTJA (AI Act market surveillance), AKI (data protection), RIA (information systems/risk), and the State Chancellery (Eesti.ai coordination) — constituting a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure rather than a single authority or a fully pluralistic independent regulator landscape. Estonia is a unitary state with no sub-national AI governance whatsoever. Formal coordination is facilitated through the Kratt steering mechanism and cross-ministerial working groups, but remains largely ad hoc rather than institutionalised in a standing forum; courts and the Data Protection Inspectorate provide some independent review of AI-related data processing.
Actor Involvement: Industry has a formal standing role through the Eesti.ai Advisory Board of entrepreneurs and close collaboration on the AI Leap programme with OpenAI and Anthropic. Civil society participation in AI governance is notably weak — commentary from Estonian World highlights a governance structure dominated by a small group of technology executives with limited NGO or ethics-body input. Academic involvement is modest, with some advisory contributions to strategy documents but no formal standing advisory role. Internationally, Estonia is active as an OECD AI Principles signatory, member of the EU AI Board, and participant in NATO and INAISI discussions, reflecting strong but not frontier-setting international engagement.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act penalty framework (up to €35M or 7% of global turnover) applies directly in Estonia and TTJA has been formally designated as a competent authority for AI Act supervision, but as of late 2025 no enforcement actions have been reported, dedicated AI enforcement resources are limited, and systematic monitoring and public reporting on AI compliance remains nascent.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), AI and Data Action Plan (Kratt) 2024–2026 (2024), Data and AI White Paper 2024–2030 (2024), GDPR / Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (2016), Personal Data Protection Act (2018), AI Action Plan (Kratt) 2022–2023 (2022), National AI Strategy (Kratt 1.0) (2019)

Regulation Status: Ethiopia's primary AI instrument is the National AI Policy adopted by the Council of Ministers in June 2024, which is a non-binding policy framework rather than enforceable legislation. The Personal Data Protection Proclamation No. 1321/2024 is binding law that incidentally covers AI-related data processing, and the EAII holds certification authority for imported AI products, but no dedicated AI Act or AI Proclamation has been enacted. Binding AI-specific rules remain aspirational, with the 2024 policy instructing sectoral regulators and the legislature to develop future implementing instruments.
Policy Lever: The only binding instrument touching AI is the Personal Data Protection Proclamation 1321/2024 (primarily a data law); the National AI Policy and Cybersecurity Policy (both June 2024) are soft-law frameworks. The EAII is a designated, operational institution with a research and certification mandate but limited compliance infrastructure, while AI startup centers and sandboxes remain at planning stage.
Governance Type: AI governance is strongly centralized around the EAII, which reports directly to the Prime Minister's Office, with ECA handling data enforcement, MInT overseeing digital strategy, and INSA covering cybersecurity—but no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanism exists. Ethiopia has no subnational AI regulatory role, and judicial or independent review of AI-specific decisions is minimal and ad hoc.
Actor Involvement: The National AI Policy was developed through a three-year consultative process involving industry, civil society, and academia, though these remain ad hoc consultation roles rather than standing formal ones; Ethiopia co-hosted the AU High-Level Dialogue on AI in Africa in May 2025, endorsed the Paris AI Action Summit Statement in February 2025, and participates in the Smart Africa Alliance and AU Continental AI Strategy.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework or enforcement actions exist; the ECA has data-protection enforcement powers under Proclamation 1321/2024 that can apply to AI-related data processing, but there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority, no routine AI monitoring, and no published AI enforcement cases.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (2024), Personal Data Protection Proclamation No. 1321/2024, Cybersecurity Policy (2024), Digital Ethiopia 2025 Strategy

Regulation Status: Fiji has no binding AI-specific legislation in force as of mid-2026; the National Digital Strategy 2025–2030 integrates AI into broader digital transformation goals but is a non-binding planning document, and a dedicated national AI framework is only planned for 2027. Existing oversight relies on general cybersecurity and online safety statutes (e.g., Online Safety Act 2018, Cybercrime Act 2021) that touch AI only incidentally.
Policy Lever: Policy levers are confined to non-binding instruments: the National Digital Strategy 2025–2030 and a government commitment to an AI user-protection framework announced in 2024, plus an AI Hub launched with Australian partnership. No binding instruments or comprehensive standards suite exist; institutional capacity remains thin with no dedicated AI body.
Governance Type: Digital and AI policy is coordinated primarily through the Department of Communications without a dedicated AI regulator, and sectoral regulators have no explicit AI remits; formal inter-agency coordination on AI is ad hoc at best, with no meaningful subnational role and only general judicial review available.
Actor Involvement: Fiji's AI governance process involves some industry and civil-society consultation through digital strategy development, with academic input from institutions like the University of the South Pacific; internationally, Fiji participates in Pacific Islands Forum digital discussions and engaged with the AI Asia Pacific Institute report process, reaching signatory-level regional engagement.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI-specific sanctions framework, no designated AI enforcement authority, and no recorded enforcement actions or monitoring practice related to AI governance in Fiji.
Specific Laws: National Digital Strategy 2025-2030, Online Safety Act (2018), Cybercrime Act (2021), Online Safety Act (2018)

Regulation Status: Finland has enacted national supplementing legislation to the EU AI Act in two stages: the first stage (Act on the Supervision of Certain AI Systems and amendments to market surveillance laws) was approved by the President on 22 December 2025 and entered into force on 1 January 2026, giving ten designated national authorities binding enforcement powers; the second stage, covering full high-risk AI system obligations, is expected by 2 August 2026. The EU AI Act itself is a dedicated, horizontal AI-specific instrument directly applicable since August 2024/2025, supplemented by Finland's own national AI supervision act—making the binding force and AI specificity both frontier-level, while implementation scores 4 rather than 5 as the second stage of national rules remains in legislative process as of June 2026.
Policy Lever: Finland deploys multiple binding instruments (EU AI Act, national AI supervision act, market surveillance amendments, sector-specific authority mandates) alongside soft-law guidance from the Data Protection Ombudsman (AI and data protection guidelines, May 2025) and Business Finland AI funding calls (2026), the Elements of AI public literacy programme, and innovation sandboxes under the EU AI Act framework; ten designated market surveillance authorities are operational but rely largely on repurposed existing bodies rather than a fully staffed dedicated AI agency.
Governance Type: Finland operates a highly plural model with ten designated market surveillance authorities (Traficom, Data Protection Ombudsman, Finnish Safety and Chemicals Agency, Valvira, FIN-FSA, Energy Authority, Finnish Medicines Agency, and others) coordinated through the Ministry of Transport and Communications and the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment, with a formal working group (April 2024–June 2026) for legislative preparation; Finland is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role, but the Data Protection Ombudsman and courts provide active independent checks.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society are consulted through government working groups and parliamentary processes but do not hold standing formal roles in AI policymaking; Finnish universities contribute advisory input (Elements of AI originated at University of Helsinki/Reaktor); internationally, Finland is a frontrunner in EU AI Act implementation, active in OECD, GPAI, Nordic AI cooperation, and Council of Europe AI Convention discussions, placing it at the global frontier for international engagement.
Enforcement Level: Finland has a comprehensive penalty framework under the EU AI Act and national supervision act (administrative fines tied to turnover thresholds), with ten designated authorities holding enforcement powers since 1 January 2026, but as of June 2026 published AI-specific enforcement actions under the new framework remain very limited, with monitoring practice still maturing under the newly operational regime.
Specific Laws: EU AI Regulation (2024/1689), Act on the Supervision of Certain Artificial Intelligence Systems (2025/Finland, in force 1 January 2026), Amendments to Market Surveillance of Products Act (2025), Finland National AI Programme (AuroraAI, 2019-2023), Finland AI Strategy (2017, updated)

Regulation Status: France is bound by the EU AI Act (in force August 2024), which provides horizontal, cross-sector coverage including prohibitions effective February 2025 and GPAI rules from August 2025; broader high-risk provisions become applicable August 2026. France has not yet formally enacted its domestic transposition decree designating national competent authorities (the draft multi-authority model with DGCCRF as single point of contact was published in September 2025 but not yet adopted by Parliament as of mid-2026), so implementation is partial and in transition. The AI Act is a dedicated AI-specific instrument supplemented by CNIL's binding GDPR-based algorithmic transparency recommendations and sector-specific guidance.
Policy Lever: France deploys the EU AI Act as a primary binding instrument alongside CNIL's binding GDPR-based enforcement (comprehensive fines totalling €486M+ in 2025) and sector-specific binding rules; CNIL has published a mature suite of AI recommendations (legitimate interest, development guidelines, PANAME audit tools) and the France 2030 plan commits ~€1.5–2.5B to AI with Bpifrance support programs and regulatory sandboxes. INESIA was established in February 2025 as a dedicated AI security and evaluation body, and CNIL, DGCCRF, ARCOM, and sector regulators form a resourced multi-authority structure.
Governance Type: France's AI governance involves multiple independent regulators—CNIL, DGCCRF, ARCOM, ACPR, ANSSI, INESIA—each with distinct remits, with DGCCRF proposed as single point of contact under the EU AI Act; inter-ministerial coordination exists through a national AI coordinator reporting to the Prime Minister and France 2030 governance, but formal statutory coordination mechanisms are still being finalized through the pending transposition decree. France is a unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI regulatory role, but courts and the Defender of Rights (Défenseur des droits) provide independent checks, and CNIL actively shapes rules through binding recommendations and enforcement.
Actor Involvement: Industry participated heavily in the February 2025 AI Action Summit (109B€ in pledges) and in CNIL's public consultations on AI recommendations; civil society was consulted in health AI strategy (70+ stakeholders in 2025) and at the Summit but lacks standing formal domestic policy roles; academia is involved through INESIA, the iPoP research programme, and advisory roles to CNIL's PANAME project; France leads internationally as co-chair of GPAI, active OECD participant, host of the 2025 AI Action Summit (61 signatories), and co-chair of international AI standards bodies.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act provides a comprehensive penalty framework (up to €35M or 7% of turnover) supplemented by GDPR fines enforced by CNIL, which issued record sanctions totalling over €486M in 2025 (including a €5M fine in May 2026), conducts routine audits with published outcomes, and has launched the PANAME AI audit methodology project; national competent authority designation for full AI Act enforcement is pending parliamentary adoption, slightly limiting dedicated AI-specific enforcement capacity.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, 2024), GDPR (Regulation 2016/679), France 2030 National AI Strategy (2021, updated 2025), National Strategy on Data and AI in Healthcare 2025-2028, Draft Law on EU AI Act National Competent Authority Designation (2025, pending), Proposed Law on AI and Copyright (2026, pending Senate)

Regulation Status: Gabon has no dedicated AI legislation, no AI-specific regulatory instruments in force, and no draft AI law in any legislative process as of mid-2026. General ICT and telecommunications laws exist but do not address AI systems in any meaningful way.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, codes of practice, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies have been established; the country lacks any formal AI policy infrastructure.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure of any kind in Gabon; no regulatory authority, coordination mechanism, subnational competence, or independent review body exists for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: No domestic stakeholder consultations involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy have occurred; Gabon holds nominal AU membership and has been peripherally exposed to AU AI governance declarations but has not actively engaged in international AI governance forums or signed AI-specific agreements.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, designated authority, or monitoring mechanism of any kind exists in Gabon.

Regulation Status: Gambia has no dedicated AI legislation, strategy, or binding regulatory instrument as of mid-2026. No AI-specific bills have been introduced into the National Assembly, and existing ICT frameworks do not address artificial intelligence directly.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy mechanisms of any kind exist; Gambia relies on general ICT governance and a nascent data protection framework without AI-targeted provisions or dedicated institutional capacity.
Governance Type: There is no designated regulatory authority or coordination mechanism for AI; governance of technology broadly falls to the Ministry of Communication and Digital Economy and the Gambia Information and Communication Infrastructure Authority (GICIA) without any AI-specific remit.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic consultations with industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy have been recorded; Gambia holds AU membership and is nominally covered by the African Union AI Continental Strategy, but domestic implementation and active international AI governance participation remain absent.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in Gambia.
Specific Laws: Information and Communications Act (2009), Gambia Data Protection Act (2021)

Regulation Status: Georgia has no dedicated AI legislation, no formal national AI strategy, and no binding AI-specific rules in force as of mid-2026; the UNESCO country report published in 2026 confirms the country is 'at a foundational stage of artificial intelligence governance.' The Law on Personal Data Protection (No. 3144/2023, in force March 2024) is GDPR-aligned and incidentally covers profiling and automated decision-making, providing minimal indirect AI coverage in one domain. Georgia signed the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (CETS No. 225) in September 2024 but has not ratified it, so it remains non-binding domestically.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instruments exist; the Personal Data Protection Law provides indirect governance but is technology-neutral. Soft-law outputs are nascent, driven mainly by the PMCG/UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment (kicked off September 2025, national forum held April 2026) and IDFI civil society analysis. Georgia's 2026 State Budget allocates USD 18.4 million for AI research (2026–2029), and GITA runs innovation and startup support programs touching AI, but no dedicated AI sandboxes or regulatory pilot programs exist. Institutional capacity is fragmented across GITA, the Ministry of Economy, and the Digital Governance Agency with no dedicated AI body and thin resources.
Governance Type: AI-relevant functions are split informally across GITA, the Ministry of Economy and Sustainable Development, the Digital Governance Agency, and the Personal Data Protection Service (PDPS), but with no formal coordination mechanism or lead AI authority. Georgia is a unitary state with no sub-national AI governance role, and while courts and the PDPS can handle data-related complaints, there is no active judicial or independent review specifically shaping AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry engagement occurs through GAIA (Georgian Artificial Intelligence Association) collaborating with GITA and participating in EU4Digital programs, but without standing formal policy-making roles. Civil society (notably IDFI) and academia contribute analysis and are included in the UNESCO/PMCG steering committee, but participation is project-based rather than institutionalized. Internationally, Georgia is a signatory to the Council of Europe AI Convention (CETS 225), adheres to the UNESCO Recommendation on AI Ethics, and participates in Eastern Partnership digital cooperation and Horizon Europe calls.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI-specific sanctions framework, no dedicated AI enforcement authority, and no documented AI enforcement actions; the Personal Data Protection Service can levy modest fines (GEL 1,000–10,000) for data protection violations that may incidentally touch AI-driven processing, but no AI-focused audits or monitoring programs exist.
Specific Laws: Law on Personal Data Protection No. 3144/2023 (in force March 2024), Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence CETS No. 225 (signed September 2024, not ratified)

Regulation Status: Germany adopted the KI-MIG (KI-Marktüberwachungs- und Innovationsförderungsgesetz) as a cabinet draft on 10–11 February 2026, but it remains in parliamentary procedure (Bundestag and Bundesrat) and has not yet been enacted; the directly applicable EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) already carries binding force across Germany since August 2024/2026 phases. The framework covers AI broadly across sectors (horizontal EU AI Act) with sector-specific carve-outs for finance (BaFin) and healthcare, giving wide scope, but full national implementation rules are still transitional pending KI-MIG enactment. Germany's instrument is AI-specific (the KI-MIG is a dedicated AI market-surveillance and innovation law), earning a high specificity score, though it is not yet fully in force.
Policy Lever: Germany relies on the directly applicable EU AI Act as its primary binding instrument plus the draft KI-MIG; the Bundesnetzagentur AI Service Desk provides practical compliance guidance for SMEs, and regulatory sandboxes are planned under KI-MIG; federal guidelines for AI use in public administration were issued in March 2025, and sector-specific regulators (BaFin, BMG) issue supplementary guidance, but the full compliance infrastructure awaits KI-MIG enactment.
Governance Type: KI-MIG designates Bundesnetzagentur as the central AI authority and coordination/competence center (KoKIVO), while BaFin oversees financial-sector AI, BMG health-sector AI, and state data protection authorities handle DPA-related AI matters; formal cross-agency coordination mechanisms are provided for in the draft KI-MIG but not yet fully operational, and state-level (Länder) DPAs play a real but secondary implementation role.
Actor Involvement: Industry associations (Bitkom, German AI Association) and research institutes (DFKI, Fraunhofer) have formal standing in consultations and working groups on KI-MIG and EU AI Act implementation; civil society is consulted through standard legislative procedures but lacks standing formal roles; Germany is a leading voice in the EU AI Act, G7 Hiroshima AI Process, OECD.AI, and GPAI, representing frontier-level international engagement.
Enforcement Level: The penalty regime derives primarily from the EU AI Act itself plus supplementary KI-MIG fines of up to €50,000 for national violations, but KI-MIG has not been enacted and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken to date; the Bundesnetzagentur is designated but its dedicated AI enforcement resources and routine monitoring practice are still being established.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), KI-Marktüberwachungs- und Innovationsförderungsgesetz - KI-MIG (cabinet draft February 2026), Guidelines for the Use of AI in Federal Administration (2025), German AI Strategy (2023 update)

Regulation Status: Ghana launched its National AI Strategy on 24 April 2026 (covering 2023–2033) following Cabinet approval in February 2026, and is developing an Emerging Technologies Bill covering AI, blockchain, and robotics expected to be laid before Parliament in 2026, but as of June 2026 no AI-specific binding legislation is in force. The Data Protection Act (2012) and a proposed new Data Protection Bill remain the only operative instruments indirectly touching AI, with the strategy being a soft-law commitment rather than an enforceable instrument.
Policy Lever: Ghana's primary policy lever is the newly launched National AI Strategy (a non-binding roadmap) and consultations convened with UNESCO and the British High Commission, alongside a USD 270 million commitment and a mandate for government agencies to adopt AI by 2026; no binding AI-specific instruments are in force, and institutional capacity remains concentrated in the Ministry of Communications and KNUST's Responsible AI Lab rather than a dedicated regulatory body.
Governance Type: Ghana's AI governance is distributed across the Ministry of Communication, Digital Technology and Innovations, the Data Protection Commission, and investment bodies, with no dedicated AI regulatory authority, though a Responsible AI Authority modelled on Singapore's is proposed under the Emerging Technologies Bill; formal inter-agency coordination is ad hoc and subnational governments play no distinct role.
Actor Involvement: KNUST's Responsible AI Lab has played a central formal role in developing and coordinating the National AI Strategy, with multi-stakeholder consultations including industry, civil society, and government; the strategy was developed with UNESCO, Smart Africa, German development cooperation, and the EU, though domestic civil society and industry lack standing formal roles in ongoing governance.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement of AI-relevant rules remains limited to the Data Protection Commission's plans to strengthen compliance with the 2012 Data Protection Act from 2026, with no AI-specific sanctions framework, no dedicated AI enforcement authority, and no record of AI-specific enforcement actions.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act (2012), National AI Strategy (2026), Emerging Technologies Bill (proposed 2026), Data Protection Bill (proposed 2026)

Regulation Status: Greece is subject to the directly applicable EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, in force August 2024) which constitutes binding horizontal AI legislation, and also has national Law 4961/2022 explicitly addressing AI governance, algorithmic impact assessments, and AI coordination bodies. The November 2024 'Blueprint for Greece's AI Transformation' sets a national vision, while the EU AI Act's full obligations are phased in through 2026-2027, placing implementation in a substantial transition period rather than fully operational.
Policy Lever: Greece relies primarily on the EU AI Act as its single main binding instrument, supplemented by national Law 4961/2022; the Ministry of Digital Governance created a Special Secretariat for AI and Data Governance in 2025, though resources remain limited, and the HDPA has been designated for fundamental rights oversight while full market surveillance designation is still being finalized.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Digital Governance (with its new Special Secretariat for AI) leads national AI governance alongside the HDPA for data-related and fundamental rights oversight; a statutory Coordinating Committee for AI (Law 4961/2022, Art. 11) provides formal but limited cross-body coordination, and Greece is a unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI regulatory role.
Actor Involvement: Consultations on Law 4961/2022 and the Blueprint involved industry and some academic input; the Athena Research Center signed an MoU with the Special Secretariat signaling academic engagement; Greece participates in the EU AI Board and OECD AI policy frameworks, though domestic civil society is consulted only occasionally.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act provides a comprehensive penalty framework applicable in Greece, but domestic enforcement infrastructure is still being established with national competent authorities required by August 2025, and no published AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken to date.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Law 4961/2022 on Emerging ICT and Digital Governance (2022), Blueprint for Greece's AI Transformation (2024), Law 5188/2025 on Data Governance Act Implementation (2025)

Regulation Status: Grenada has no AI-specific legislation, binding regulations, or formal regulatory proposals as of mid-2026. No AI rules are in force or in any legislative pipeline, and general laws do not explicitly address AI systems.
Policy Lever: Grenada has not deployed any policy instruments targeting AI governance — no national AI strategy, published guidelines, sandboxes, funding programs, or designated institutional capacity exists.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure in Grenada; no government body holds an AI oversight mandate, and no coordination mechanisms, subnational activity, or independent review processes relating to AI have been established.
Actor Involvement: Domestic stakeholder engagement in AI policy is absent; however, Grenada is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI in the Americas, giving it minimal international participation credit without any active domestic process.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, penalties, monitoring, or dedicated authority for AI exists in Grenada.

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, Guatemala has no dedicated AI legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no AI-specific bill that has passed Congress. The country relies on existing general legal frameworks (intellectual property, the 2008 Law on Access to Public Information) to incidentally address AI-related issues, while a National AI Strategy is still being co-created with UNDP support and a data-protection bill (No. 6,105) remains pending in the legislature without AI-specific provisions.
Policy Lever: Guatemala has no binding AI instruments and no published AI guidelines or codes of practice; UNDP-supported AI Sprint and AI for Local Development Challenge programs constitute nascent economic/innovation activity, but no dedicated national AI institution or compliance infrastructure exists.
Governance Type: There is no formal AI governance structure; existing sectoral regulators (Superintendency of Banks, Superintendency of Telecommunications) apply general mandates without AI-specific remits, and no inter-institutional coordination mechanism for AI has been established.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academic engagement in AI governance is minimal and unstructured; Guatemala's main international engagement is as a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI under CELAC and participation in UNDP's AI Sprint initiative.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement actions, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanisms exist in Guatemala as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: Law on Access to Public Information, Decree 57-2008 (incidentally touches personal data); Data Protection Bill No. 6,105 (pending, introduced 2022)

Regulation Status: Guinea has no dedicated AI legislation, national AI strategy, or binding regulatory instrument addressing artificial intelligence as of mid-2026. The country lacks even a drafted AI bill or formal policy commitment, and general laws do not meaningfully address AI systems.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy mechanisms of any kind exist in Guinea — no guidelines, standards, codes of conduct, sandboxes, or dedicated public funding programs for AI. No institutional body with an AI mandate has been established.
Governance Type: Guinea has no AI governance architecture whatsoever; no single authority, let alone plural regulators, oversees AI, and there are no coordination mechanisms, subnational roles, or independent review bodies relevant to AI.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia are not involved in any AI policy process in Guinea; the country holds nominal African Union membership and is nominally subject to AU continental digital economy frameworks but has not actively engaged in AI-specific international governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice related to AI exists in Guinea.

Regulation Status: Guinea-Bissau has no AI-specific legislation, strategy, or binding instrument of any kind as of June 2026. The country has no recorded regulatory activity addressing artificial intelligence, and general laws do not meaningfully touch AI either.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any form—binding or non-binding—exist for AI governance in Guinea-Bissau. There are no dedicated bodies, funding programs, sandboxes, or published guidance related to AI.
Governance Type: Guinea-Bissau has no institutional framework for AI governance; no regulator, coordinating body, subnational authority, or independent review mechanism exists in this domain.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement of industry, civil society, academia, or international bodies in any domestic AI governance process has been recorded; Guinea-Bissau's nominal African Union membership has not translated into engagement with AU AI initiatives.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, no designated authority, no sanctions, and no monitoring activity related to AI in Guinea-Bissau.

Regulation Status: Guyana has no dedicated AI legislation, no adopted national AI strategy, and no AI-specific bill before Parliament as of mid-2026. The only enacted instruments with any relevance to AI are general laws: the Data Protection Act 2023, the Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 2023, and the Open Data Act 2024, none of which address AI specifically; senior officials have publicly acknowledged the governance gap but no binding AI rules are in force or formally proposed.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, codes of practice, or dedicated funding/sandbox programs exist; the newly appointed Data Protection Commissioner (January 2026) heads the only nascent body that could touch AI-adjacent data issues, but the Data Protection Office is still being established and has no explicit AI remit.
Governance Type: AI oversight in Guyana is wholly unstructured: there is no dedicated AI regulator, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism for AI, and no independent review body or court jurisprudence shaping AI rules; Guyana is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory activity.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic multi-stakeholder engagement on AI policy involving industry, civil society, or academia; internationally, Guyana is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI in the Americas, participates in CARICOM regional AI discussions (UNESCO Caribbean AI Policy Roadmap), and Minister McCoy represented Guyana at the IPU 2026 Assembly on AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement authority, or monitoring mechanism exists; the general Data Protection Act 2023 carries administrative penalties but the Data Protection Office is only beginning to be established and no enforcement actions of any AI-relevant kind have been reported.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act 2023 (No. 18 of 2023), Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 2023 (No. 12 of 2023), Open Data Act 2024

Regulation Status: Haiti has no AI-specific legislation, regulation, or formal proposal at any stage. The country remains in a severe humanitarian and political crisis — with gang violence, collapsed state institutions, and transitional governance — that has entirely precluded any AI policy development as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, guidance documents, national AI strategy, dedicated funding programs, sandboxes, or AI-focused institutional bodies exist in Haiti. The state lacks the basic administrative capacity to develop or sustain such mechanisms.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure of any kind in Haiti; fragmented and weak central government authority, ongoing insecurity, and the absence of functioning sectoral regulators mean no AI-relevant coordination or oversight architecture is present at any level.
Actor Involvement: Haiti has no structured domestic AI policy process involving industry, civil society, or academia, and has not signed major international AI declarations or participated meaningfully in global AI governance forums; international engagement on AI policy is negligible.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, designated authority, penalty structure, monitoring mechanism, or audit practice exists in Haiti.

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, Honduras has no AI-specific legislation enacted or formally proposed in its legislature. The country's engagement is limited to a general digital government plan (Plan Nacional de Gobierno Digital 2023–2026) that references AI challenges without creating binding rules, and trade.gov confirms 'no specific regulatory framework governs its use.' All commitments remain non-binding and aspirational.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments exist; Honduras relies on the Plan Nacional de Gobierno Digital 2023–2026 managed by DIGER as its only soft-law reference document touching AI. No dedicated AI funding programs, sandboxes, or institutional bodies with AI mandates have been established.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated AI governance body or formal inter-agency coordination mechanism for AI in Honduras; fragmented general-purpose agencies (DIGER, sector ministries) handle digital matters without any explicit AI remit, and no subnational or independent oversight role exists.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia have no structured formal role in AI governance; Honduras participates at a basic level in regional Latin American AI discussions and is referenced as a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI, representing the extent of its international engagement.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no designated authority, no sanctions regime, no monitoring practice, and no known enforcement actions related to AI in Honduras.
Specific Laws: Plan Nacional de Gobierno Digital 2023–2026

Regulation Status: Hungary implements the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) as directly applicable EU law, and enacted Act LXXV of 2025 plus Government Decree 344/2025 to designate national competent authorities (the Minister for National Economy as market surveillance authority, and the National Accreditation Authority as notifying authority), with most provisions in force from 1 December 2025. Scope is horizontal and cross-sectoral per the EU AI Act's design, but certain provisions (Section 3(2) and Section 10, including regulatory sandboxes) only enter into force on 2 August 2026, keeping implementation partially in transition. The national instruments are primarily authority-designation and procedural rules rather than a freestanding dedicated AI statute, hence ai_specificity is 4 rather than 5.
Policy Lever: Hungary has one binding instrument (Act LXXV of 2025 plus its implementing decree) designating authorities under the EU AI Act framework, while the National AI Strategy 2025–2030 is soft-law; the AI Coalition provides an industry-academia-government consultation platform, but no mature national guidance suite, dedicated sandbox program, or substantially resourced standalone AI agency exists yet beyond the designated ministerial function.
Governance Type: The Minister for National Economy leads as primary market surveillance authority, with the National Accreditation Authority handling notifying functions, the Hungarian National Bank as sectoral authority for financial AI, and an advisory Hungarian AI Council; coordination is defined in the legislation but still nascent, and Hungary is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role. Judicial review of administrative decisions is available through the Metropolitan Court of Budapest.
Actor Involvement: The AI Coalition (400 members from business, science, and government, formed in 2018) provided input into the 2025–2030 AI Strategy, giving industry and academia some structured but not standing formal policy roles; civil society engagement is limited and not institutionalized; Hungary participates in the EU AI Board and is bound by EU-level international AI governance frameworks.
Enforcement Level: The sanctions framework follows EU AI Act fines (up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover), translated into Hungarian currency by the implementing decree, with the Minister for National Economy designated as enforcement authority, but the authority is newly constituted with no published enforcement actions or routine monitoring as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Act LXXV of 2025 on the Implementation in Hungary of the European Union Regulation on Artificial Intelligence (2025), Government Decree 344/2025 (X.31.) on the Implementation of Act LXXV of 2025 (2025), National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025–2030 (2025)

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) remains 'under scrutiny' for EEA incorporation and has not been formally adopted into the EEA Agreement for Iceland; it is therefore not yet binding in Iceland.
