Iceland's primary regulatory instruments are the GDPR (via Act No. 90/2018) and a non-binding AI Action Plan 2025–2027, meaning binding AI-specific rules are in legislative pipeline but not yet in force.
Policy Lever: Iceland relies on a published AI Action Plan 2025–2027 (soft law) and Persónuvernd's GDPR-based enforcement as its main levers; Persónuvernd has published AI-and-data-protection guidance and a focused 2026 strategy, but dedicated binding AI instruments are still pending EEA incorporation, and economic tools such as an AI regulatory sandbox remain under discussion rather than operational.
Governance Type: Iceland has a compact institutional structure: Persónuvernd leads AI-related oversight alongside the Ministry of Culture, Innovation and Higher Education as strategy owner, with limited additional sectoral regulators; as a unitary state there is no subnational AI role, and coordination across bodies is ad hoc rather than formalised.
Actor Involvement: Iceland conducted stakeholder consultations in developing its AI Action Plans and draws on Nordic DPA cooperation and OECD AI discussions; it participates as an observer in the EU AI Board and is active within EEA/EFTA governance, but domestic civil society, industry, and academic involvement is consultative rather than institutionalised.
Enforcement Level: Persónuvernd applies GDPR-based sanctions (up to 4% of global turnover) to AI-related data processing violations and has issued fines in practice, but there is no AI-specific sanctions framework yet, and routine AI-dedicated monitoring is limited to occasional reviews informed by its annual report and 2026 strategy priorities.
Specific Laws: Act on Data Protection and Processing of Personal Data No. 90/2018, AI Action Plan 2024–2026 (2025), AI Action Plan 2025–2027 (2025), AI Policy 2021

Regulation Status: India has deliberately avoided comprehensive AI legislation, relying instead on non-binding AI Governance Guidelines (November 2025) built around seven 'Sutras' principles. The Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (and 2025 Rules) provides the closest binding instrument but is a general data-protection law, not AI-specific. Binding AI rules are not yet in force; the regulatory approach remains voluntary and principles-based at the AI level.
Policy Lever: The DPDP Act 2023 is the primary binding instrument but is not AI-specific; the seven Sutras guidelines are non-binding soft law supplemented by sector-specific guidance from RBI, SEBI, and IRDAI. The IndiaAI Mission provides compute infrastructure (10,000+ GPUs), an AI marketplace, and startup funding programs, while the IndiaAI Safety Institute operates as a designated coordinating body, though it remains thinly resourced relative to mature counterparts.
Governance Type: MeitY leads national AI policy and coordinates through the IndiaAI Safety Institute's hub-and-spoke model with sectoral regulators (RBI, SEBI, IRDAI, healthcare regulators), providing a lead-body-plus-sectoral-regulator structure with ad hoc rather than fully formalized coordination mechanisms. Subnational governments play no independent AI regulatory role, and while Indian courts can hear data/AI-related cases, there is no active independent review body systematically shaping AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academic stakeholders have been consulted through MeitY and IndiaAI processes, with working groups informing the Sutras guidelines, though formal standing roles are limited; civil society engagement remains occasional and not institutionalized. India is highly active internationally—hosting the G20 AI presidency in 2023, participating in GPAI, BRICS AI dialogue, and the International Network of AI Safety Institutes—placing it near the frontier on international engagement.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement of AI-specific obligations is essentially absent: the DPDP Act 2023 defines penalties for data breaches (up to ₹250 crore) but AI-specific sanctions frameworks do not exist; the IndiaAI Safety Institute is a coordinating, not an enforcement, body; and no published AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken, with monitoring limited to occasional policy reviews.
Specific Laws: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (2023), Digital Personal Data Protection Rules (2025), IndiaAI Mission (2024), AI Governance Guidelines – Seven Sutras (2025)

Regulation Status: As of June 13, 2026, Indonesia still lacks any enacted binding AI-specific instrument: two Presidential Regulations (Perpres) — one on AI Ethics and one on an AI National Roadmap — were drafted in 2025, declared presidential priorities, and as recently as June 11, 2026 the Minister was still publicly emphasizing their urgency at the BRAVO 500 Summit, indicating neither had yet been signed. Existing AI governance relies on the non-binding 2020 AI National Strategy (Stranas KA 2020–2045), a 2023 AI ethics guideline (policy paper only), and general laws such as GR 71/2019 on electronic systems and Law No. 27/2022 on personal data protection that touch AI only incidentally.
Policy Lever: Indonesia's policy toolkit consists primarily of soft instruments — a national AI strategy document and an ethics guideline — while binding Perpres remain unsigned; Komdigi and BRIN provide institutional capacity but lack a dedicated AI regulatory unit, and one or two digital innovation sandbox/sandbox-adjacent programs exist under the ministry.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralized under the Ministry of Communication and Digital (Komdigi) as the lead body, with BRIN providing technical advisory input and sector ministries expected to issue derivative regulations once the Perpres is enacted; coordination is ad hoc rather than formalized, sub-national governments have no independent AI regulatory role, and judicial review under general administrative law exists but has not been applied specifically to AI.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates through consultations and events such as the BRAVO 500 Summit and working groups convened by Komdigi; civil society and academia have limited, occasional involvement with no standing formal roles; internationally, Indonesia is active in ASEAN AI governance frameworks and has bilateral AI partnerships with Japan, South Korea, and the UAE, and participates in global AI safety declarations.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists; enforcement relies entirely on general digital and data-protection law (Law 27/2022, ITE Law), no AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken, and no dedicated AI monitoring or audit regime is in place pending enactment of the Perpres.
Specific Laws: Indonesian Artificial Intelligence National Strategy 2020–2045 (Stranas KA, 2020), AI Ethics Guidelines (Komdigi policy paper, 2023), Government Regulation No. 71/2019 on Electronic Systems and Transactions, Law No. 27/2022 on Personal Data Protection, Draft Presidential Regulation on AI Ethics (pending 2026), Draft Presidential Regulation on AI National Roadmap (pending 2026)

Regulation Status: Iran established the National AI Organization (NAIO) via parliamentary legislation in October 2025 and published a National AI Roadmap in May 2025, making it one of the few countries with a legislatively-created AI body; however, comprehensive binding horizontal AI legislation remains in development rather than in force, and the NAIO's mandate is primarily strategic/coordinating rather than regulatory-restrictive. The regulatory posture is advancement-oriented, with binding rules limited to legacy sectoral instruments (e.g., cybersecurity and content filtering laws incidentally touching AI applications) rather than dedicated AI statutes.
Policy Lever: The primary lever is the National AI Roadmap with its $8 billion investment target and NAIO as a coordinating institution, supplemented by bilateral technology agreements with China and Russia; soft-law guidance remains thin and the NAIO is newly established with unclear staffing depth. Economic tools include state investment targets and research funding channeled through universities and defense-aligned institutions, but no sandbox or innovation-zone programs comparable to leading jurisdictions have been publicly announced.
Governance Type: AI governance is highly centralized under NAIO and the presidency, with the security apparatus playing a dominant role; there are no independent sectoral AI regulators, no meaningful subnational AI policymaking, and no independent judicial or ombudsman mechanisms that substantively check AI deployments. Coordination across agencies exists in principle through NAIO's inter-ministerial mandate but remains ad hoc and opaque in practice.
Actor Involvement: Policy formulation is state-led with limited structured input from private industry or academia, and civil society organizations are effectively excluded from domestic AI governance processes given Iran's restrictive political environment. Internationally, Iran participates in a narrow bilateral AI governance initiative with China (Global AI Governance Initiative, March 2024) and engages Russia, but is largely excluded from multilateral forums (OECD, G7, Bletchley-process) due to sanctions.
Enforcement Level: No publicly documented AI-specific sanctions framework or enforcement actions exist; the NAIO is designated as the coordinating authority but has no publicly known dedicated enforcement or audit function, and monitoring of AI systems is opaque and not publicly reported.
Specific Laws: National AI Roadmap (2025), National AI Organization (NAIO) Establishment Law (2025)

Regulation Status: Iraq has no enacted AI legislation as of June 2026; the National AI Strategy Initiative (INSAI) remains a non-binding draft and has not progressed through formal legislative channels. No binding AI rules, executive orders, or sectoral regulations are in force, leaving AI governance entirely in the realm of unimplemented aspirational planning.
Policy Lever: Iraq's only AI policy instrument is the unpublished/unenacted INSAI draft, with no formal guidance documents, codes of practice, sandboxes, procurement incentives, or dedicated institutional bodies established to implement any AI strategy.
Governance Type: AI oversight in Iraq is entirely fragmented across general ministries with no designated lead authority, no formal inter-ministerial coordination mechanism for AI, no subnational regulatory role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: Iraq has conducted no structured consultations with industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy, and participates minimally or not at all in international AI governance forums, declarations, or standard-setting bodies.
Enforcement Level: With no AI-specific laws in force, Iraq has no penalty framework, no dedicated enforcement authority, no recorded enforcement actions, and no monitoring or auditing practices related to AI systems.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy Initiative - INSAI (draft, unenacted)

Regulation Status: Ireland's AI regulatory framework rests on the directly applicable EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) plus S.I. No. 366/2025 designating initial competent authorities, while the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill 2026 (General Scheme published February 2026) is undergoing pre-legislative Oireachtas committee scrutiny as of June 2026 and has not yet been enacted. The EU AI Act provides horizontal cross-sector coverage and is partly in force (governance rules from August 2025, full enforcement powers from August 2026), making the regime substantively AI-specific but still in transition at the national implementation level. The national Bill is in the legislative pipeline as priority legislation, not yet law.
Policy Lever: S.I. 366/2025 is a binding statutory instrument designating competent authorities; the EU AI Act itself is directly binding. Guidance is emerging but not yet a mature suite; the National AI Office (to be operational by August 2026) and 15 sectoral authorities are designated but not yet fully resourced or operational, and a regulatory sandbox is planned but not yet running.
Governance Type: Ireland adopts a distributed model with 15 designated national competent authorities (Central Bank, DPC, CCPC, Health Products Regulatory Authority, Workplace Relations Commission, etc.) coordinated by a forthcoming National AI Office, giving high regulator plurality; formal coordination mechanisms are legislated but not yet operational. Ireland is a unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI role; judicial and DPC review provides some independent checks but routine AI-specific enforcement has not yet begun.
Actor Involvement: The General Scheme process included public consultation and Oireachtas committee engagement with industry, civil society, and academic stakeholders; Ireland is deeply embedded in EU AI Board, OECD AI Policy Observatory, and international enforcement cooperation networks given its role as EU headquarters for major tech platforms. Standing formal roles remain limited at the domestic level compared to consultation-based input.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act establishes a comprehensive penalty framework (up to €35 million or 7% global turnover) and Ireland's Bill proposes national enforcement tools including contravention and prohibition notices, but full enforcement powers only apply from August 2026 and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken yet; the National AI Office is designated but not yet operational as of June 2026.
Specific Laws: EU Artificial Intelligence Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Artificial Intelligence Act (Governance and Enforcement) Regulations 2025 / S.I. No. 366/2025 (2025), General Scheme of the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill 2026 (2026)

Regulation Status: Israel has no dedicated AI statute in force; regulation remains policy-led and sector-based anchored in the December 2023 AI Policy ('Responsible Innovation'). The Protection of Privacy Law 1981 as modernised by Amendment 13 (effective August 2025) is the most concrete binding instrument touching AI, applying cross-sectorally to AI that processes personal data, but it is not an AI-specific law. The National AI Directorate (established October 2025, work plan approved May 2026) adds institutional impetus but has not yet translated into binding AI-specific rules.
Policy Lever: The primary binding lever remains Amendment 13 to the Protection of Privacy Law (August 2025), supplemented by the PPA's May 2025 draft AI guidance and sector-level soft regulation; the Ministry of Justice published regulatory sandbox white papers in January 2025 and the National AI Directorate is rolling out compute and talent programmes. Institutional capacity has grown with the PPA's expanded enforcement powers and the new Directorate in the PM's Office, but resources and dedicated AI compliance infrastructure are still being built.
Governance Type: Governance sits between the PPA (data/privacy-AI lead with expanded Amendment 13 powers), the Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology, the new National AI Directorate in the PM's Office, and sector regulators (finance, health, labour), giving a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure. Formal cross-body coordination mechanisms are nascent—the proposed AI Policy Coordination Centre within MIST has not yet been fully established—and Israel is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates through published consultations (e.g., PPA draft guidance, MOJ sandbox white papers) and working groups, while civil society and academia have limited, occasional advisory input rather than standing formal roles. Israel is an active OECD AI Working Party participant, pursues bilateral AI partnerships, and the May 2026 strategy explicitly targets Tier-1 positioning in global AI alliances (Pax Silica/US frameworks).
Enforcement Level: The PPA has imposed monetary sanctions (e.g., on EY and PwC Israel in March 2025 for PPL violations) and Amendment 13 significantly raises the penalty ceiling effective August 2025; the PPA conducts proactive AI system audits but routine, publicly published AI-specific enforcement actions remain limited and the Directorate does not yet have an enforcement remit.
Specific Laws: Protection of Privacy Law 5741-1981 (Amendment 13, effective August 2025), National AI Policy (December 2023), National AI Directorate establishment (October 2025), Ministry of Justice Regulatory Sandbox Legislative Guidelines (January 2025)

Regulation Status: Italy has Law No. 132/2025 (in force October 10, 2025), the first dedicated national AI statute in the EU, complementing the directly applicable EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689). The law covers healthcare, employment, justice, IP, deepfakes, and minors' protection with dedicated criminal offenses, while two implementing decrees were approved by the Council of Ministers on June 10, 2026—making Italy the first EU member state to operationalize a comprehensive national framework—though additional decrees defining the full sanctioning and data/algorithm governance framework remain pending through October 2026.
Policy Lever: Italy deploys the EU AI Act and Law 132/2025 (with criminal and administrative penalties) as multiple binding instruments, supplemented by Ministry of Labour AI-in-employment guidelines (December 2025), AgID draft guidelines for public administration under public consultation, and a €1 billion state-backed venture fund alongside joint AgID-ACN AI sandboxes; ACN and AgID are formally designated authorities with explicit AI remits, though dedicated AI-specific staffing remains modest relative to the scope of their mandates.
Governance Type: Italy's AI governance involves multiple national bodies—ACN (market surveillance and EU single point of contact), AgID (digital/public sector), Garante (data protection), Ministry of Justice, Ministry of Labour, and an inter-ministerial coordination committee at the Presidency of the Council of Ministers—providing both plurality and formal coordination structures; Italy is a centralized state with negligible subnational independent AI regulation, while courts play an emerging but still occasional role (Court of Rome annulled Garante's €15M OpenAI fine in March 2026).
Actor Involvement: Italy held the G7 AI governance chair in 2024, played an active role in EU AI Act negotiations, and benefits from EU coverage under the Council of Europe AI Framework Convention (CETS 225, 2024); domestically, industry and civil society participate through AgID public consultations and ministerial working groups, and an expert committee of academics developed the Italian AI Strategy 2024–2026, though none of these actors hold standing formal co-governance roles.
Enforcement Level: Italy has a comprehensive penalty framework including criminal imprisonment (1–5 years for deepfakes), administrative fines under the EU AI Act, and ACN as designated market surveillance authority with inspection and sanctioning powers; enforcement actions include the Garante's landmark ChatGPT ban (2023) and €15M fine (2024, though annulled by the Court of Rome in March 2026), while routine auditing and public monitoring reporting infrastructure is still being established as implementing decrees are finalized.
Specific Laws: Law No. 132/2025 on Artificial Intelligence (2025), EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Italian Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2024-2026 (2024), Ministry of Labour AI Guidelines No. 180/2025 (2025), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI CETS No. 225 (2024)

Regulation Status: Jamaica has no dedicated AI legislation enacted or formally in legislative process as of mid-2026. The Data Protection Act (2020) provides the only indirectly applicable framework, but it contains no AI-specific provisions; there are no binding AI rules in force or formally proposed.
Policy Lever: No dedicated AI policy instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or AI-specific institutional bodies exist in Jamaica; the only relevant instrument remains the general Data Protection Act (2020), and no AI-specific capacity has been stood up.
Governance Type: AI oversight is not assigned to any specific authority; data protection responsibilities under the DPA sit with the Office of the Information Commissioner, but no multi-body coordination or independent AI review mechanism exists, and Jamaica's unitary structure leaves no sub-national regulatory role.
Actor Involvement: No domestic structured consultation processes involving industry, civil society, or academia for AI policy have been established; Jamaica participates at the international level through CARICOM regional discussions and is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration on AI ethics, giving it a limited international footprint.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Jamaica; the DPA's general data-protection sanctions are the only conceivably applicable mechanism but have not been applied to AI contexts.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act (2020)

Regulation Status: Japan's AI Promotion Act (enacted May 28, 2025; fully in force September 1, 2025) is a dedicated horizontal AI law covering all sectors, establishing the AI Strategy Headquarters, and requiring industry to 'endeavor to cooperate'; secondary Utilization Guidelines (December 19, 2025) and the Basic AI Plan (December 23, 2025) have been issued, with an APPI amendment bill entered the Diet in April 2026. The law is AI-specific and cross-sectoral, but private-sector obligations remain non-binding effort duties with no financial penalties, placing it below the EU AI Act standard on binding force.
Policy Lever: Japan relies primarily on a mature, maintained suite of soft-law instruments—AI Business Operator Guidelines v1.1 (METI/MIC, March 2025), AI Utilization Guidelines (December 2025), Basic AI Plan (December 2025), and Hiroshima AI Process Principles—complemented by R&D investment programs, semiconductor export controls, and data-center infrastructure support under the Act; the AI Strategy Headquarters and the operationally active Japan AI Safety Institute (J-AISI, ~30+ staff) provide meaningful institutional capacity, though the AI Promotion Act itself creates only weak binding obligations on industry.
Governance Type: Governance is led by the AI Strategy Headquarters under the Prime Minister, formally coordinating across METI, MIC, Cabinet Office, and the PPC, with J-AISI handling safety evaluation; the National AI Strategy Council provides a multi-stakeholder forum including government, industry, academia, and civil society. No subnational AI regulation exists, and while general judicial review is available, no AI-specific independent review body or ombudsman has been established.
Actor Involvement: Industry holds formal roles through standing working groups and the AI Institutional Study Group; civil society and academia participate in the National AI Strategy Council and stakeholder hearings but without formal co-governance standing; internationally, Japan leads the G7 Hiroshima AI Process (66 countries, 38 organizations), participates in GPAI, the International Network of AI Safety Institutes, and agreed a 2026 HAIP Action Plan in Tokyo.
Enforcement Level: The AI Promotion Act contains no financial penalties; enforcement relies solely on a 'name-and-shame' public disclosure mechanism and backstop enforcement under existing sectoral laws (APPI, competition, product safety), with no AI-specific enforcement actions documented since the law's entry into force, while J-AISI publishes annual AI safety status reports and the PPC conducts ongoing APPI compliance monitoring.
Specific Laws: Act on Promotion of Research and Development and Utilization of AI-Related Technology (AI Promotion Act, 2025), Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI, 2003, amended 2022, amendment bill 2026), AI Business Operator Guidelines v1.1 (2025), AI Utilization Guidelines (2025), Basic AI Plan (2025), Hiroshima AI Process International Guiding Principles (2023)

Regulation Status: Jordan's AI regulatory framework remains non-binding as of mid-2026. The National AI Strategy and Implementation Plan (2023–2027) and the National Charter of Ethics for AI (published 2022) are both voluntary instruments; no binding AI-specific legislation has been enacted or is in active legislative process. The Personal Data Protection Law No. 24 of 2023 (in force since March 2024) incidentally touches AI use of personal data but is not an AI-specific instrument.
Policy Lever: Policy is driven by soft-law instruments—the AI Strategy (2023–2027), the AI Ethics Charter, and a Digital Transformation Strategy (2026–2028)—with no binding regulatory instruments. MoDEE hosts a dedicated AI division with limited resources, and some capacity-building programs exist through partnerships with UNIDO, UN ESCWA, and bilateral partners, but no formal sandbox or broad funding program is operational.
Governance Type: AI governance is centralized under MoDEE's AI division as the single lead body, with no independent sectoral AI regulators and no dedicated AI enforcement authority. Coordination across government entities exists informally through strategy implementation structures; Jordan is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance role.
Actor Involvement: The AI Ethics Charter was developed with input from public, private sector, civil society, and academia in a consultation process, but no standing formal roles exist; Jordan participates in regional AI forums and has bilateral cooperation with UAE and EU partners, and has engaged with UNIDO, UN ESCWA, and ITU, qualifying it as a signatory-level international participant.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists; the MoDEE AI division is the closest body to a designated authority but lacks an explicit enforcement remit or dedicated compliance resources, and no AI enforcement actions or systematic monitoring have been publicly reported.
Specific Laws: Jordanian AI Policy (2020), National Charter of Ethics for Artificial Intelligence (2022), Jordanian Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023-2027, Personal Data Protection Law No. 24 of 2023, National Digital Transformation Strategy 2026-2028

Regulation Status: Kazakhstan's Law No. 230-VIII 'On Artificial Intelligence' (signed November 17, 2025, in force January 18, 2026) is the first comprehensive binding AI statute in Central Asia, covering developer, owner, and user obligations with seven guiding principles and a risk-based structure across most sectors. The Digital Code, which will consolidate and expand the digital/AI framework further, is set to take effect July 11, 2026. While the primary law is in force, secondary regulations and full implementation guidance are still being developed, keeping implementation at a partial stage.
Policy Lever: Kazakhstan has one dedicated binding AI instrument (Law No. 230-VIII) supplemented by the forthcoming Digital Code; soft-law guidance remains limited with a unified Digital Qazaqstan conceptual document in preparation. Economic tools include the $1B Qazaqstan Venture Group, Astana Hub Ventures for startups, and the Alem.ai international center targeting $5B in AI solution exports, while the newly established Ministry of AI and Digital Development is operational but still building full compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: AI governance is strongly centralized under the Ministry of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Development (established September 2025), with limited formal coordination mechanisms across other sectoral bodies still being established under the Digital Code framework. Kazakhstan is a unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI regulatory role, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters is nascent at best.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academia have some advisory input through bodies like Alem.ai and government-linked university partnerships, but structured civil society consultation in Kazakhstan's AI governance process is not formally established given the country's political context. Internationally, Kazakhstan leads Central Asian AI governance, cooperates with CIS on standards, holds an International AI Advisory Council under the President, and participates in Gulf state and Eurasian partnerships.
Enforcement Level: Administrative fines (15–200 MCI) for violations of AI obligations are defined in the Code of Administrative Offenses, with potential suspension or prohibition of AI systems, and the Digital Code (effective July 2026) is expected to expand sanctions; however, no public enforcement actions have been reported since the law entered force in January 2026, and routine auditing practices are not yet operational.
Specific Laws: Law No. 230-VIII 'On Artificial Intelligence' (2025), Digital Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan (2026), Presidential Decree 'Year of Digitalization and Artificial Intelligence' (2026), Digital Qazaqstan Strategy

Regulation Status: Kenya's Artificial Intelligence Bill, 2026 (Senate Bills No. 4 of 2025), published in the Kenya Gazette on 19 February 2026 and read a First Time in the Senate on 2 April 2026, is squarely in the legislative process but has not been enacted — it must pass both chambers before presidential assent. The Bill establishes a dedicated, risk-based horizontal framework modelled in part on the EU AI Act, covering high-risk systems, mandatory registration, and criminal penalties, making it AI-specific in design; however, nothing binding is yet in force and only the Data Protection Act (2019) currently applies to AI-adjacent conduct.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instrument is in force; the Data Protection Act (2019) and its ODPC-issued guidelines provide the only operational regulatory floor. Kenya's National AI Strategy (2025–2030) allocates KES 152 billion over five years and articulates sector-specific guidelines for media and finance, and the ODPC has issued draft compliance-audit and data-sharing codes, but dedicated AI institutions remain unestablished pending the Bill's enactment.
Governance Type: Currently the Ministry of Information, Communications and the Digital Economy (MICDE) leads AI policy coordination and the ODPC handles data-related AI enforcement, constituting a modest two-body arrangement with ad hoc coordination rather than a formalised multi-regulator structure. The proposed AI Commissioner and AI Authority would significantly expand regulator plurality, but they do not yet exist; sub-national (county) involvement in AI governance is absent, and judicial or independent review of AI decisions remains occasional and untested.
Actor Involvement: Senate committee stakeholder retreats (May 2026, Naivasha) brought together industry, regulators, and civil society organisations including KICTANet and Amnesty International Kenya, which submitted formal memoranda on the Bill, indicating meaningful if not yet institutionalised consultation. Kenya is internationally active as AU Champion on Institutional Reform under President Ruto, participates in Smart Africa Alliance, co-hosted the Africa Forward Summit 2026, and engages with OECD AI governance work, though formal treaty-level AI obligations remain limited.
Enforcement Level: The AI Bill defines criminal penalties and fines for its proposed regime but is not law; the ODPC has imposed data-protection fines (e.g., against Brainstorm Insurance Brokers and Nairobi Hospital) that incidentally touch AI-related personal-data processing, and PwC notes Kenya entering a more structured enforcement phase in 2026, but no dedicated AI enforcement authority or routine AI-specific auditing exists yet.
Specific Laws: Artificial Intelligence Bill, 2026 (Senate Bills No. 4 of 2025), Data Protection Act (2019), Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act (2018), National AI Strategy (2025–2030)

Regulation Status: Kiribati has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules, and no known draft AI legislation as of June 2026. The government's digital policy focus remains on basic ICT connectivity, e-government foundations, and climate resilience, with no AI governance activity recorded.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, guidance documents, AI-specific funding programs, or dedicated AI institutional bodies exist in Kiribati; the country lacks the administrative infrastructure and resources to implement such mechanisms.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure in Kiribati; no regulatory authority, coordination mechanism, or independent review body has been established for AI, and the country's small, highly centralized government means no subnational AI role exists.
Actor Involvement: No structured industry, civil society, or academic consultation on AI policy exists domestically, and Kiribati has not been identified as a signatory to any international AI governance declarations or standards processes as of 2026.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Kiribati, consistent with the complete absence of any AI regulatory instrument.

Regulation Status: Kosovo has no dedicated AI legislation, no binding AI-specific rules, and no national AI strategy formally adopted as of mid-2026. The country relies exclusively on general data protection law aligned with GDPR through the Information and Privacy Agency, which incidentally touches AI only insofar as personal data processing is involved.
Policy Lever: Kosovo has no AI-specific policy instruments of any kind—no binding rules, no published AI guidance or codes of practice, no AI-targeted funding or sandbox programs, and no dedicated AI institutional body; the Information and Privacy Agency handles data protection with no explicit AI mandate or resources.
Governance Type: AI governance in Kosovo is effectively non-existent as a structured system; the Information and Privacy Agency is the sole body with any tangential relevance to AI via data protection, there are no formal inter-agency coordination mechanisms for AI, no subnational AI regulatory role, and no established independent review or ombudsman function for AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic engagement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy processes in Kosovo; internationally, Kosovo participates in some Western Balkan and EU accession-related dialogues that touch on digital governance, providing a minimal foothold in broader European AI policy conversations but without formal treaty or standards commitments.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no designated authority with an AI remit, no sanctions defined for AI-specific violations, and no monitoring or auditing practice related to AI systems in Kosovo.
Specific Laws: Law No. 06/L-082 on Personal Data Protection (2019)

Regulation Status: Kuwait has a publicly available draft National AI Strategy (2025–2028) and participates in regional AI declarations, but no AI-specific legislation has been enacted and no binding AI rules are in force; the proposed Public Authority for AI has not been formally established by parliament. Existing coverage of AI relies entirely on general ICT, cybersecurity (CITRA, National Cybersecurity Center Decision No. 37/2022), and data-governance instruments that only incidentally touch AI.
Policy Lever: Kuwait's only operative policy levers are soft commitments: the draft National AI Strategy (2025–2028), CAIT's digital-transformation mandate, and nascent pilot programmes including a planned AI Centre of Excellence; no binding AI-specific instruments exist, and dedicated institutional capacity for AI oversight remains embryonic.
Governance Type: AI-related responsibilities are fragmented across CAIT, CITRA, and the National Cybersecurity Center without a formal coordination mechanism; Kuwait is a unitary state with no sub-national regulatory role, and there is no active judicial or independent body shaping AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry engagement is limited to ad-hoc partnerships (e.g. Microsoft/CAIT collaboration) and Kuwait Chamber of Commerce validation sessions; civil society is essentially absent from domestic AI policy processes; Kuwait hosted the DCO 5th General Assembly in February 2026 and adopted the Kuwait Declaration on Responsible AI, reflecting active regional/international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanism exists; general ICT fines apply incidentally but no AI-targeted enforcement action has been recorded.
Specific Laws: Kuwait National AI Strategy 2025-2028 (draft), Kuwait Vision 2035 (2017), National Cybersecurity Center (Decision No. 37 of 2022), CITRA Data Governance Framework

Regulation Status: Kyrgyzstan has no binding AI-specific legislation in force or formally tabled in its parliament as of mid-2026. The Digital Code (2023) provides a general digital governance framework that may incidentally apply to some AI use, but no dedicated AI statute exists and no draft AI law is confirmed to be in the legislative process.
Policy Lever: The main policy levers remain soft: a National Council on AI was established to coordinate strategy development, and a national AI strategy is reportedly under preparation, but no binding instruments, sandbox programs, or substantial dedicated funding mechanisms have been publicly confirmed.
Governance Type: AI governance is centralized around the National Council on AI under executive authority with limited multi-body coordination; sub-national entities play no independent AI governance role, and there is no established judicial or independent-body review mechanism specifically for AI matters.
Actor Involvement: Stakeholder engagement is nascent: some consultations are reported in the strategy preparation process, but civil society participation is not institutionalized; Kyrgyzstan participates in CIS and Central Asian regional digital cooperation forums but has not signed dedicated multilateral AI governance instruments.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI-specific enforcement framework, no recorded enforcement actions, no authority with an explicit AI compliance remit, and no AI monitoring or audit regime in place.
Specific Laws: Digital Code (2023)

Regulation Status: Laos has no binding AI-specific legislation, no AI strategy, and no draft AI laws as of mid-2026. General ICT frameworks such as the Law on Telecommunications and the Law on Electronic Transactions exist but do not address AI. No AI governance instruments have been initiated at any stage of a legislative process.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments of any kind exist in Laos; the country relies entirely on pre-AI general ICT regulations administered by the Ministry of Technology and Communications, with no dedicated AI funding programs, sandboxes, guidance documents, or institutional bodies.
Governance Type: Any AI-adjacent oversight would default to a single central body, the Ministry of Technology and Communications, with no multi-regulator framework, no formal coordination mechanisms, no subnational regulatory role, and no independent judicial or civil oversight active in the AI domain.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia have no structured role in AI policy; Laos participates passively in ASEAN digital economy initiatives and has nominal exposure to ASEAN AI governance declarations but has not signed or engaged with any substantive international AI-specific commitments.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no designated authority with an AI remit, no sanctions structure, and no monitoring or audit activity related to AI systems in Laos.
Specific Laws: Law on Telecommunications (2011), Law on Electronic Transactions (2012)

Regulation Status: Latvia directly applies the EU AI Act (in force from August 2024, with major obligations from August 2025 and February 2025 for prohibited AI), which constitutes binding horizontal AI legislation. Latvia additionally enacted the Law on the Latvian Artificial Intelligence Centre (Mākslīgā intelekta centra likums, adopted 6 March 2025, in force 20 March 2025), creating a national AI coordination and sandbox institution. Implementation is partially in force: core EU AI Act obligations apply from August 2025, national competent authority designations were completed per the VARAM plan (PTAC as market surveillance lead, Ombudsman for fundamental rights), but the AI Centre was still building operational capacity through 2025–2026, and secondary sandbox regulations were pending finalization.
Policy Lever: Latvia's primary binding instrument is the EU AI Act supplemented by the national AI Centre Law; soft law guidance is limited to EU AI Office materials and early national guidance rather than a mature national suite. Economic tools include EU-funded AI initiatives and the planned regulatory sandbox for high-risk AI projects. The AI Centre (NMIC) was expected operational by 2025–2026 but remains in build-out phase, so institutional capacity is designated and partially resourced rather than fully mature.
Governance Type: Latvia uses a lead-body plus sectoral regulator model: PTAC leads market surveillance for AI Act, with the Health Inspectorate, Civil Aviation Agency, Maritime Administration, and other agencies covering their sectors; the Ombudsman handles fundamental rights; formal coordination between authorities is mandated by the VARAM implementation plan but still formalizing. Latvia is a unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI role, and courts and the Ombudsman provide some independent checks.
Actor Involvement: Industry participated in the AI Centre Law drafting and public consultation processes; academia is represented in the NMIC structure linking universities and the public sector; civil society engagement is limited to periodic consultation rather than standing roles. Latvia participates in the EU AI Board, Baltic digital cooperation frameworks, and the OECD EU Coordinated Plan on AI implementation process.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's comprehensive penalty framework (up to €35M or 7% global turnover) applies in Latvia, with PTAC and sector inspectorates designated as market surveillance authorities, but as of mid-2026 there are no known published enforcement actions under the AI Act and systematic AI-specific monitoring is not yet routinely practiced.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, 2024), Law on the Latvian Artificial Intelligence Centre (Mākslīgā intelekta centra likums, 2025), Digital Transformation Guidelines 2021-2027 (2020), Latvia National AI Strategy (2020, considered outdated)

Regulation Status: Lebanon has no binding AI legislation, no draft AI law in any legislative process, and no AI-specific regulatory instrument of any kind. The country's prolonged economic collapse, governmental dysfunction, and political paralysis have entirely precluded any formal AI governance activity, leaving all sub-indicators at the floor.
Policy Lever: No policy mechanisms of any type — binding or non-binding — exist for AI in Lebanon. There are no dedicated AI funding programs, regulatory sandboxes, published guidance, codes of practice, or institutional bodies with any AI remit, as the broader state apparatus lacks the capacity to establish them.
Governance Type: Lebanon possesses no AI governance structure whatsoever; there is no lead authority, no sectoral regulator with an AI remit, no formal coordination mechanism, and no independent review body engaged with AI. Sub-national entities play no role in AI policy, and courts have not engaged with AI-specific legal questions in any structured way.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in any domestic AI policy process in Lebanon, as no such process exists. Lebanon has not meaningfully participated in international AI governance forums, declarations, or standards bodies, reflecting its broader diplomatic and economic isolation.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, monitoring regime, or dedicated enforcement authority exists in Lebanon; there have been zero AI-related enforcement actions of any kind.

Regulation Status: Lesotho has no dedicated AI legislation, strategy, or binding regulatory framework as of June 2026. The country relies solely on general ICT and telecommunications law that predates AI considerations, with no AI-specific instrument drafted or proposed in any legislative process.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidance documents, funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies exist; Lesotho's digital governance is handled by the Lesotho Communications Authority under general ICT mandates without any AI-specific capacity.
Governance Type: All ICT-adjacent governance is centralised under a single national regulator (Lesotho Communications Authority) with no multi-body coordination, no subnational AI role, and no independent review mechanism addressing AI systems.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic engagement of industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy; Lesotho's only indirect international connection is through SADC digital economy frameworks, but the country has not signed AI-specific international declarations or participated actively in global AI governance fora.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanisms exist in Lesotho.
Specific Laws: Lesotho Communications Authority Act (2000), Electronic Transactions and Commerce Act (2008)

Regulation Status: Liberia has no dedicated AI legislation, national AI strategy, or any binding AI-specific regulatory instrument as of mid-2026. General telecommunications and ICT frameworks exist but do not address AI systems in any targeted way.
Policy Lever: No policy mechanisms—binding or non-binding—have been developed for AI governance in Liberia; there are no sandboxes, funding programs, or designated AI bodies of any kind.
Governance Type: Any AI-adjacent oversight would default to the Liberia Telecommunications Authority under general ICT law; there is no multi-body coordination, no subnational AI role, and no independent review mechanism specific to AI.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia have no structured role in AI governance; Liberia's only relevant international linkage is nominal African Union membership, where continental AI policy discussions (the AU Continental AI Strategy) are underway but Liberia has not actively participated.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, designated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Liberia.

Regulation Status: Libya has no binding AI legislation, no draft AI law in any legislative process, and no sector-specific AI rules. The country's ongoing political fragmentation between rival authorities in Tripoli and the east precludes any coordinated regulatory development. All sub-indicators remain at the floor.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any kind exist for AI governance in Libya; there are no published strategies, guidance documents, sandbox programs, or dedicated institutional bodies with AI mandates. State capacity for AI policy development is effectively absent.
Governance Type: Libya has no AI regulatory authority of any kind and no coordination mechanisms; competing parallel governments (GNU in Tripoli and HoR/LNA-aligned administration in the east) mean there is no unified governance structure, no subnational AI role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI systems.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder engagement on AI policy exists; civil society, industry, and academia have no formal or informal role in AI governance, and while Libya holds nominal AU membership where AI frameworks have been discussed, there is no meaningful domestic operationalization of any international AI commitment.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in Libya.

Regulation Status: Liechtenstein has no AI-specific binding legislation in force as of June 2026: the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) is marked EEA-relevant but remains 'under scrutiny' for EEA Agreement incorporation and has not yet been formally adopted, meaning its obligations do not automatically apply. The only domestic AI-specific instrument is the non-binding 'KI-Strategie der Liechtensteinischen Landesverwaltung' (April 2026), which guides internal government use only; AI is otherwise addressed incidentally through the GDPR-implementing Data Protection Act and sector-specific financial rules.
Policy Lever: There are no AI-specific binding instruments in force domestically; soft law consists of the Datenschutzstelle's 2023/2024 GDPR-based guidance on AI chatbots and the April 2026 public-administration AI strategy. The FMA and Datenschutzstelle serve as de facto AI-relevant bodies within existing mandates, but neither has dedicated AI-specific resources or a formal AI remit yet.
Governance Type: Governance is split between the Datenschutzstelle (general AI/data protection) and the FMA (financial sector), with coordination occurring informally given Liechtenstein's compact administrative structure and no formal multi-body AI coordination mechanism established. As a city-state-scale jurisdiction there is no subnational layer, and judicial/independent review of AI is nascent, relying on ordinary courts and the DPA's supervisory role.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society engagement is limited and informal, reflecting the country's small size; there is no standing formal consultation structure for AI policy beyond EEA/EFTA channels. Internationally, Liechtenstein is active: it signed the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (February 2025), participates as an observer in EU AI Board meetings, and engages through EFTA/EEA scrutiny processes for the EU AI Act.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists; the Datenschutzstelle monitors AI chatbot compliance under the GDPR and has issued guidance signalling intent to monitor, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been reported, and no authority yet holds an explicit dedicated AI enforcement remit.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024, pending EEA incorporation), KI-Strategie der Liechtensteinischen Landesverwaltung (2026, non-binding), Datenschutzgesetz / Data Protection Act (2019), Cyber-Security Act / Cyber-Sicherheitsgesetz LGBl. 2025/111 (2025), Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence CETS 225 (signed 2025)

Regulation Status: Lithuania implements the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), which entered into force on 2 August 2024, with horizontal cross-sector coverage; on 14 January 2025, the Seimas enacted amendments to the Law on Technology and Innovation and the Law on Information Society Services designating national competent authorities. Prohibited-AI provisions have applied since February 2025, GPAI obligations since August 2025, and high-risk system obligations are set to apply from 2 August 2026, placing Lithuania in an advanced transition with authorities operational but full enforcement framework not yet complete.
Policy Lever: Lithuania relies on the EU AI Act as its primary binding instrument, supplemented by national implementing amendments and a 2023–2026 AI Action Plan; economic tools include a €3 million EU-funded AI regulatory sandbox (Innovation Agency), over €42 million for Lithuanian-language AI resources (2021–2026), a €7.5 million SME AI adoption programme (2026–2028), and a €65 million LitAI innovation hub secured in 2025. The Communications Regulatory Authority (RRT) and Innovation Agency have been formally designated but are still building dedicated AI compliance capacity.
Governance Type: Lithuania has a lead body (RRT as market surveillance authority) plus the Innovation Agency as notifying authority, with sectoral distribution of functions still being finalised; formal coordination mechanisms include the Seimas Working Group on AI and the National AI Governance Forum, though sub-national governance is absent in this unitary state. Judicial review and the Seimas Working Group provide nongovernmental checks, but no fully independent AI oversight body exists.
Actor Involvement: The National AI Governance Forum convened five times in 2024 with over 200 stakeholders including business and academia, providing structured but non-standing consultative input; civil society does not hold formal standing roles in AI policy-making. Lithuania participates actively in the EU AI Board, the OECD Coordinated Plan on AI, and Baltic regional cooperation, reflecting strong international engagement.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's comprehensive penalty framework (up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover) applies in Lithuania, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly documented as of June 2026, with RRT designated as market surveillance authority without evidence of a fully resourced dedicated AI enforcement team or routine audit programme in place.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Law on Technology and Innovation amendments (2025), Law on Information Society Services amendments (2025), Lithuanian Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2019), AI Strategy Action Plan 2023-2026

Regulation Status: Luxembourg is directly subject to the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), which is horizontally applicable; Chapter I and II obligations have applied since February 2025, with high-risk system requirements phasing in through August 2026–2027. National implementing Draft Bill No. 8476, which designates competent authorities and sets penalties, was submitted to Parliament in December 2024 and remained under review as of June 2026, leaving implementation partially in transition.
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act constitutes the primary binding instrument, supplemented by directly applicable EU regulations (DSA Law of 4 April 2025, GPSR Law of 28 February 2025, NIS2 draft bill 8364); the CNPD has published AI literacy guidance and launched the ReMI (Regulation Meets Innovation) regulatory sandbox with the AI Factory, while the MeluXina supercomputer and a Mistral AI strategic partnership (June 2025) underpin economic tools. CNPD and CSSF are explicitly designated with operational AI remits, though the national bill formalizing this is still pending adoption.
Governance Type: Luxembourg has a multi-authority structure: CNPD as the lead horizontal supervisory authority and single contact point, CSSF as market surveillance authority for financial-sector AI, and additional sectoral bodies (OLAS, ALMPS, CGPD) under Draft Bill 8476; the January 2026 'AI Act in Action' conference brought all designated regulators together for coordination, but formal standing coordination mechanisms are still being established. Luxembourg is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance role; judicial and CNPD opinion-based review provides moderate non-governmental oversight.
Actor Involvement: Industry engages through consultations and formal submissions (e.g., FEDIL's position on Bill 8476); civil society participation in domestic AI policy processes is limited; academia contributes via LIST and university involvement in the AI Factory and ReMI sandbox; internationally, Luxembourg actively participates in the EU AI Board, cooperates with the EU AI Office, contributes to OECD AI policy reviews, and pursued a strategic AI partnership with Mistral AI in June 2025.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's penalty framework is directly applicable, and Draft Bill 8476 proposes additional national sanctions, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been reported; CNPD is operationally active in publishing guidance, running awareness campaigns, and operating a regulatory sandbox, though routine formal audits and published enforcement records are not yet established.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Draft Bill No. 8476 (2024, pending), Accelerating Digital Sovereignty 2030 – National AI Strategy (2025), Law of 4 April 2025 (DSA implementation), Law of 28 February 2025 (GPSR implementation), Draft Bill 8364 (NIS2 transposition)

Regulation Status: North Macedonia has no dedicated AI statute, no adopted national AI strategy, and no binding AI-specific rules as of June 2026. AI is governed only indirectly through the 2020 Law on Personal Data Protection (ZZLP), which mirrors GDPR; the SMART/MK 2030 ICT strategy names AI as a pillar but is a non-binding planning document. Signing the Council of Europe's AI Convention (CETS 225) in May 2026 is a commitment to future alignment, not yet binding domestic law.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instrument exists; the sole policy lever is the general ZZLP data-protection framework applied incidentally to AI. The SMART/MK 2030 strategy and the Ministry of Digital Transformation's AI pillar constitute nascent soft-law signalling, but no operational AI guidance, sandbox, or dedicated AI body has been established.
Governance Type: AI oversight in practice rests almost entirely with the Personal Data Protection Agency (AZLP/DZLP), a single authority enforcing the ZZLP; the Ministry of Digital Transformation holds a strategic role but no enforcement remit, yielding very limited plurality. A government working group on a national AI strategy was established in 2021 but has met only once, indicating no meaningful formal coordination; subnational units play no AI-governance role.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic industry or formal civil-society role in AI policy-making; think-tank analysis (e.g., EPI) and academic commentary exist but carry no formal advisory standing. International engagement is notable: North Macedonia signed the CoE AI Convention (CETS 225) in May 2026 and continues EU accession alignment, placing it at signatory-to-declarations level internationally.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement actions, dedicated AI authority, or systematic AI monitoring exist; enforcement is limited to PDPA/ZZLP data-protection cases that incidentally touch AI systems, and no published AI-specific enforcement actions are on record.
Specific Laws: Law on Personal Data Protection / ZZLP (2020), ICT Development Strategy SMART/MK 2030 (2025), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI and Human Rights Democracy and Rule of Law CETS No. 225 (signed 2026)

Regulation Status: Madagascar has no binding AI legislation, no AI-specific regulatory framework, and no AI provisions in general law as of mid-2026. General ICT and telecommunications laws (e.g., Loi n°2014-006 on electronic communications) and a rudimentary data protection framework may incidentally touch AI systems but were not designed for AI governance and do not constitute AI-specific instruments.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments of any kind exist in Madagascar — no national AI strategy, no guidelines, no sandboxes, and no dedicated institutional body with an AI mandate. The Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Digital Development handles broad ICT matters but has not produced AI-specific capacity or programs.
Governance Type: AI oversight, if it were to arise, would default to the general ICT ministry with no plural regulatory structure, no formal inter-agency coordination, no subnational role, and no independent or judicial body with an AI remit. The governance landscape is entirely undifferentiated with respect to AI.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder consultation on AI has taken place involving industry, civil society, or academia in Madagascar. Madagascar is a member of the African Union and is nominally exposed to AU AI governance frameworks (including the AU AI Continental Strategy), but has not translated that into active participation or domestic policy processes.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no designated enforcement authority, no recorded enforcement actions, and no monitoring or audit mechanism related to AI in Madagascar.
Specific Laws: Loi n°2014-006 sur les communications électroniques et la poste (2014), Loi n°2014-038 sur la protection des données à caractère personnel (2014)

Regulation Status: Malawi has no dedicated AI legislation, strategy, or binding regulatory framework as of mid-2026. General ICT law and a nascent data protection regime exist but contain no AI-specific provisions, leaving AI entirely outside any formal regulatory perimeter.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist at the national level; Malawi's policy toolkit for AI remains entirely absent.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure in Malawi: no designated lead authority, no inter-agency coordination mechanism, no sub-national regulatory role, and no independent or judicial review body with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic consultations involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy have been recorded; Malawi's only relevant engagement is passive participation in AU and SADC digital transformation initiatives, which have not produced national AI commitments.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Malawi.
Specific Laws: Electronic Transactions and Cyber Security Act (2016), Data Protection Act (2024)

Regulation Status: Malaysia has no binding AI-specific legislation in force as of June 2026; the AI Governance Bill was drafted through NAIO and presented to Cabinet in June 2026 but has not been tabled in Parliament. The only binding instruments with any AI relevance are the Cybersecurity Act (2024) and amended PDPA (2024), both of which are general laws that touch AI only incidentally, supplemented by the non-binding National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE, 2024) and the MY-AI Standards platform (launched March 2026).
Policy Lever: Malaysia relies on soft-law instruments — the AIGE guidelines, AI-Rmap 2021–2025, the AI Technology Action Plan 2026–2030, and the MY-AI Standards platform — with no binding AI-specific instrument yet; the NAIO and MDEC provide some institutional capacity and there are sandbox and investment programs, but resources remain modest for AI-specific compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: AI governance is distributed across NAIO (Ministry of Digital), MOSTI, MDEC, CyberSecurity Malaysia, and the Department of Personal Data Protection, with ad hoc inter-agency coordination through NAIO; subnational entities play no independent role and judicial or independent review of AI-specific matters remains occasional and undeveloped.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academia are engaged through multi-stakeholder working groups convened by NAIO during preparation of the AI Technology Action Plan 2026–2030, while civil society participation is limited and largely informal; internationally, Malaysia is active through ASEAN's Working Group on AI, hosts the ASEAN AI Safety Network secretariat, and maintains bilateral AI partnerships with the US, China, and UAE.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework or enforcement authority exists; the NAIO coordinates governance but lacks an explicit enforcement remit, and monitoring of AI systems is voluntary and limited to occasional self-assessment against the non-binding AIGE guidelines.
Specific Laws: National AI Roadmap (AI-Rmap 2021-2025), National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE 2024), Cybersecurity Act (2024), Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act (2024), MY-AI Standards Platform (2026), AI Technology Action Plan 2026-2030

Regulation Status: As of mid-2026, Maldives has no binding AI-specific legislation in force; the National AI Masterplan (2025–2035) was still being drafted/finalized following the July 2025 UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment, and no dedicated AI law has been enacted. Existing general laws such as the ICT Act (2017) and a draft Personal Data Protection Bill (consulted 2023, target enactment uncertain) touch digital governance incidentally but are not AI-specific instruments.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments exist; the only notable soft-law output is the draft AI Masterplan aligned with UNESCO's Recommendation on the Ethics of AI, but no mature guidance suite, dedicated funding programs, sandboxes, or staffed AI governance body has been established yet.
Governance Type: AI governance in Maldives is centralized under the Ministry responsible for digital affairs, with no multi-regulator structure, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism for AI, no subnational role (unitary state), and no independent review body or ombudsman with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: Structured domestic stakeholder engagement (industry, civil society) is absent; academic input was involved in the UNESCO-led AI Readiness Assessment process (2025), and Maldives participates as a signatory to UNESCO's AI Ethics Recommendation and engaged with UNESCO/World Bank on digital governance, providing limited international-level engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement actions, dedicated AI authority, or monitoring/audit regime exists; the Communications Authority of Maldives holds general telecoms enforcement powers but has no explicit AI remit.
Specific Laws: National AI Masterplan 2025–2035 (draft, 2024), ICT Act (2017), Privacy and Personal Data Protection Bill (draft, 2023)

Regulation Status: Mali has no AI-specific legislation, binding frameworks, or formal regulatory proposals as of mid-2026. The military government (junta in power since 2021) has not initiated any AI governance process, and no general laws have been adapted to address AI systems.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any kind—binding or soft—related to AI exist in Mali. There are no dedicated bodies, sandbox programs, or funding mechanisms targeting AI development or oversight.
Governance Type: No AI governance structure exists; no authority has been designated for AI oversight at any level of government. Subnational and non-governmental actors play no formal role in any AI-related process.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement from industry, civil society, or academia exists in any AI policy process domestically. Mali's military government has also disengaged from key multilateral bodies (ECOWAS suspended Mali in 2022), limiting meaningful international AI governance participation.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework of any kind for AI in Mali—no penalties, no designated authority, no monitoring, and no enforcement actions have ever occurred.

Regulation Status: Malta is bound by the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), which entered into force on 1 August 2024 and whose main provisions apply from 2 August 2026; some obligations (prohibited practices, GPAI, AI literacy) already apply. Nationally, Legal Notice 226 of 2025 (Artificial Intelligence Regulations, 2025) and L.N. 227 of 2025 formally designate competent authorities and transpose national implementation duties, making Malta's binding framework operational ahead of the August 2026 full-application date. Scope is horizontal and cross-sectoral via the EU Act, but full operationalisation is still in a transition period as of June 2026.
Policy Lever: Malta relies on the EU AI Act as its single binding horizontal instrument, supplemented by L.N. 226 and L.N. 227 of 2025; MDIA publishes voluntary certification frameworks and has begun establishing a national AI regulatory sandbox, while the Malta Digital Innovation Authority (Amendment) Act XIX of 2024 expanded MDIA powers. Soft law and economic tools remain limited but developing—MDIA guidance is published and partial EU-funded AI projects exist, though dedicated resources are modest for a small state.
Governance Type: Governance is led by the MDIA as primary market surveillance authority and single point of contact, with the IDPC (Information and Data Protection Commissioner) assigned concurrent oversight for data-sensitive and high-risk AI systems, constituting a dual-authority model with some formal coordination. Malta is a unitary state with no sub-national regulatory role; judicial and ombudsman review provides standard independent checks but AI-specific active shaping by courts is not yet established.
Actor Involvement: Industry is engaged through MDIA consultation processes and voluntary certification schemes; academia has input via the University of Malta and its AI specialisations, referenced in EU co-ordinated plan progress reports. Civil society is consulted occasionally but has no standing formal role, while internationally Malta is active in the EU AI Board and participates in EU-level AI governance mechanisms as an EU member state.
Enforcement Level: L.N. 226 of 2025 establishes a defined penalty framework—MDIA can impose administrative fines up to €350,000 or 1% of global turnover for AI Act operator infringements and daily penalties up to €12,000—but the main enforcement regime does not become fully operational until 2 August 2026, meaning no published enforcement actions have been taken yet and monitoring practices are still being set up.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Malta Digital Innovation Authority (Amendment) Act XIX (2024), Artificial Intelligence Regulations – Legal Notice 226 of 2025, Artificial Intelligence – Designation of the IDPC – Legal Notice 227 of 2025, Malta Digital Innovation Authority Act (Cap. 592, 2018)

Regulation Status: The Marshall Islands has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules in force or in draft, and no AI governance framework of any kind. The country's policy priorities remain centered on climate resilience, maritime sovereignty, and basic infrastructure needs, with no AI regulatory activity identified as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, guidance documents, AI-focused funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies exist in the Marshall Islands; there is no known government capacity allocated to AI policy.
Governance Type: The Marshall Islands has a highly centralized small-state government with no AI regulatory structure whatsoever; there are no sectoral AI regulators, no coordination mechanisms, no subnational AI roles, and no independent review bodies addressing AI.
Actor Involvement: No structured industry, civil society, or academic involvement in AI governance exists domestically; the Marshall Islands has not been identified as a signatory to major international AI declarations or as a participant in multilateral AI standards processes.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, designated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in the Marshall Islands.

Regulation Status: Mauritania has no AI-specific legislation, national AI strategy, or binding regulatory instrument of any kind as of mid-2026. No AI-relevant bills are known to be in the legislative pipeline, and general laws do not substantively address AI systems.
Policy Lever: No policy mechanisms of any kind—binding instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies—exist for AI governance in Mauritania.
Governance Type: There is no designated regulatory authority, interagency coordination mechanism, subnational governance role, or independent review body for AI in Mauritania.
Actor Involvement: Domestic stakeholder engagement on AI policy is absent; Mauritania holds nominal African Union membership and is a signatory to broad AU digital frameworks but has not actively participated in AI-specific multilateral governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice for AI exists in Mauritania.

Regulation Status: Mauritius has no horizontal binding AI legislation; the FAIR Guidelines (April 2026) and the National AI Strategy 2025–2029 are currently non-binding soft-law instruments. The only binding AI-specific rule remains the FSC's Financial Services (Robotic and Artificial Intelligence Enabled Advisory Services) Rules 2021, which covers one sector (financial services). The Data Protection Act 2017 applies incidentally to AI, and a series of new digital regulations were announced in 2025 but not yet enacted.
Policy Lever: One binding sector-specific instrument exists (FSC AI Rules 2021) alongside several soft-law documents: the National AI Strategy 2025–2029 and FAIR Guidelines (April 2026), developed with UNDP support. The 2025–2026 budget allocated Rs 25 million for a Public Sector AI Programme, established an AI Innovation Start-Up Programme with tax deductions, and created a dedicated AI Unit at MITCI, though the unit is newly established and thinly resourced relative to advanced jurisdictions.
Governance Type: Governance is spread across the MITCI AI Unit (strategy), the National Computer Board (implementation), the Data Protection Office (data aspects), and the FSC (financial sector AI), representing a lead body plus sectoral regulators structure. Mauritius is a small unitary island state with no sub-national governance role; coordination across bodies appears mostly ad hoc rather than via formal standing mechanisms, and judicial or independent review of AI has not been activated in practice.
Actor Involvement: The National AI Strategy 2025–2029 and FAIR Guidelines were developed through a multi-stakeholder consultation that included the private sector, startups, academia, and civil society (confirmed by UNDP), though these remain one-off consultations rather than standing formal roles. Mauritius participates in the African Union, Smart Africa Alliance, and collaborated with UNDP on the strategy launch, reflecting active international engagement beyond mere declarations.
Enforcement Level: The only enforceable AI-specific sanctions framework is within the FSC's 2021 AI advisory services rules (financial sector); the broader FAIR Guidelines are explicitly non-binding with no penalties defined, the newly established AI Unit at MITCI has no enforcement remit as yet, and no public AI-specific enforcement actions or routine monitoring audits have been recorded.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy (2018), Data Protection Act (2017), Financial Services (Robotic and Artificial Intelligence Enabled Advisory Services) Rules (2021), Digital Transformation Blueprint 2025-2029, National AI Strategy 2025-2029, FAIR Guidelines (2026)

Regulation Status: Mexico has no enacted AI-specific law as of June 2026; the Ley Nacional para Regular el Uso de la Inteligencia Artificial (February 11, 2026) remains in committee stage in the Senate. The 2025 LFPDPPP (enacted March 2025) provides binding rules on automated decision-making and AI-driven data processing, and the Principios Chapultepec (January 2026) are a non-binding soft-law declaration; together these represent limited AI coverage within adapted general law rather than a dedicated horizontal framework.
Policy Lever: The 2025 LFPDPPP is the sole enacted binding instrument with AI-relevant provisions; the draft AI law and Principios Chapultepec add soft-law guidance but no mature standards suite. The ATDT operates a public AI training school and the Senate hosted six thematic roundtables (2024–2025), but no AI-specific compliance infrastructure or dedicated AI body yet exists.
Governance Type: Multiple bodies now share AI-adjacent oversight—the Ministry of Anticorruption and Good Governance (data protection, replacing dissolved INAI), the Telecommunications Regulatory Commission and ATDT (replacing dissolved IFT), and PROFECO and CNBV in sectoral roles—but coordination is ad hoc rather than formalised and CONAIA is only proposed. Subnational governments play no independent AI regulatory role, and judicial or ombudsman review of AI decisions is occasional at best.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academia participated in the Senate's six thematic roundtables (2024–2025) and contributed to the multi-stakeholder National AI Agenda 2024–2030, while civil society engagement has been limited and occasional. Mexico is a signatory to the Santiago Declaration, participates in OECD AI policy forums, and maintains bilateral digital cooperation with the United States.
Enforcement Level: The 2025 LFPDPPP defines administrative and criminal sanctions for data/AI violations enforced by the Ministry of Anticorruption, but no dedicated AI enforcement authority exists, no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly documented, and there is no systematic AI auditing or public reporting regime.
Specific Laws: Ley Federal de Protección de Datos Personales en Posesión de los Particulares (LFPDPPP, 2025), Ley Federal de Telecomunicaciones y Radiodifusión (2025), Ley Nacional para Regular el Uso de la Inteligencia Artificial (draft, 2026), Principios Chapultepec (2026), Propuesta de Agenda Nacional de Inteligencia Artificial 2024-2030

Regulation Status: The Federated States of Micronesia has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules, and no formal AI governance framework in force or under active legislative consideration as of mid-2026. The country remains focused on foundational connectivity and digital infrastructure challenges rather than AI regulation.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, soft-law guidance, economic tools such as sandboxes or AI-focused funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies for AI policy exist in Micronesia.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure of any kind; no regulatory body, no inter-agency coordination mechanism, and no subnational or nongovernmental role in AI oversight has been established.
Actor Involvement: No structured engagement with industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy exists domestically; Micronesia has limited participation in international AI governance forums, though it is a UN member and party to some broader Pacific regional dialogues, placing it marginally above complete absence on the international dimension.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in Micronesia.

Regulation Status: Moldova's AI governance rests primarily on the 2024 White Paper on Data Management and Artificial Intelligence (a non-binding vision document) and its signature (not ratification) of the Council of Europe AI Framework Convention. Law No. 195/2024 on Personal Data Protection (GDPR-aligned, entering force August 2026) is the only enacted binding instrument with indirect AI relevance, while no dedicated binding AI statute exists or is in the legislative pipeline. The country's posture is pre-legislative: strong policy ambition driven by EU candidacy but no binding AI rules yet in force.
Policy Lever: The primary lever is the 2024 White Paper (soft-law vision document) and the April 2026 Sub-Council for Artificial Intelligence and Applied Research (SCIA), a multi-stakeholder consultative body. Moldova Innovation Technology Park provides some economic infrastructure for the tech ecosystem, but there is no sandbox or AI-specific funding program; institutional capacity remains thin and nascent.
