Governance Type: Governance is led primarily by the Ministry of Economic Development and Digitalization, with the National Centre for Personal Data Protection holding a secondary role on data issues; the newly launched SCIA platform adds some cross-ministerial and multi-stakeholder coordination. Moldova is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance role, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters has not been documented.
Actor Involvement: The April 2026 SCIA platform formally convenes public authorities, academia, private sector, and civil society organizations in a consultative structure, representing an early step toward structured multi-stakeholder input; Moldova's EU candidacy, Council of Europe AI Convention signature, and Eastern Partnership participation place it at a moderate level of international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, dedicated enforcement authority, or systematic monitoring mechanism exists; data protection enforcement under the existing law is limited, and the new GDPR-aligned data protection law does not enter force until August 2026.
Specific Laws: White Paper on Data Management and Artificial Intelligence (2024), Law No. 195 on Personal Data Protection (2024, entering force 2026), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law – signature (2024)

Regulation Status: As of June 2026 Monaco has enacted no standalone AI legislation, no AI risk-classification framework, and no formal proposal for one has been introduced in the National Council. AI governance relies entirely on the GDPR-aligned Law No. 1.565 of 3 December 2024 on personal data protection—which applies to AI systems only insofar as they process personal data—and the Extended Monaco digital transformation programme, neither of which constitutes binding AI-specific law.
Policy Lever: Monaco has no binding AI instruments and no published AI-specific guidelines or codes of practice; the Extended Monaco Enterprises (EME) programme and its Digital FlashUp initiative offer government-funded digital-skills and AI-awareness support to businesses (scoring slightly above baseline on economic tools), while the newly established APDP provides a functioning but narrowly data-protection-focused supervisory body with limited AI remit.
Governance Type: Monaco is a small unitary city-state with a single data protection authority (APDP) handling all data-related AI oversight and no sectoral regulators with distinct AI remits; there is no formal AI coordination mechanism, no sub-national governance, and judicial review of administrative decisions provides a minimal independent check.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates informally through the Extended Monaco Enterprises programme and the ADIM IT directors association, but there are no formal standing roles for industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy-making; Monaco's international engagement is limited to Council of Europe Convention 108+ participation and EU-adjacent alignment, without signing the CoE AI Framework Convention (CETS 225).
Enforcement Level: Law No. 1.565 equips the APDP with administrative fines up to €10 million applicable to data-processing violations including those involving AI, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been published and routine AI auditing or monitoring practice does not yet exist.
Specific Laws: Law No. 1.565 on Protection of Personal Data (2024), Act No. 1.566 approving ratification of the Protocol to Convention 108+ (2024), Extended Monaco digital transformation programme (2019)

Regulation Status: Mongolia has no binding AI-specific legislation as of mid-2026. The National Strategy on Big Data and Artificial Intelligence was only in draft/consultation stage as of May 2025, with no evidence it has been formally enacted by parliament; all current AI references remain within the non-binding Digital Nation and Digital First policy frameworks, which carry no mandatory compliance obligations.
Policy Lever: Mongolia relies entirely on non-binding strategic documents and a draft national AI strategy developed in collaboration with UNDP, with no binding instruments enacted. Some soft-law activity is emerging (draft strategy, stakeholder consultations, published readiness assessments), and limited economic tools exist (an Oracle skills training partnership, plans for a GPU cluster AI Center), but no dedicated AI body or compliance infrastructure is operational yet.
Governance Type: AI policy in Mongolia is concentrated entirely within the Ministry of Digital Development, Innovation and Communications (MDDIC), with no dedicated AI regulator, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism, no sub-national AI governance role, and no independent review or ombudsman function for AI matters.
Actor Involvement: Mongolia's AI strategy development has involved some structured consultation with private sector, civil society, and academic stakeholders through UNDP-facilitated workshops and the February 2025 national conference, but these remain ad hoc rather than standing formal roles; internationally, Mongolia is engaging with UNDP, Oracle, and GCSCC on AI readiness and strategy, placing it as a signatory-level participant in international AI governance dialogue.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no dedicated authority, no penalties defined for AI-related obligations, and no monitoring or audit practice in place, as no binding AI rules exist to enforce.
Specific Laws: National Strategy on Big Data and Artificial Intelligence (draft, 2025), Digital First Policy, Digital Nation Strategy, Law of Mongolia on Personal Data Protection (2021)

Regulation Status: Montenegro has no dedicated AI legislation in force, and no national AI strategy has yet been formally adopted as of June 2026; the AI Strategy 2026–2030 Working Group was constituted in October 2025 and is still drafting. General laws such as the Personal Data Protection Law (GDPR-aligned since 2023) and Copyright Act apply only incidentally to AI, offering no AI-specific binding obligations or enforcement mechanisms.
Policy Lever: Montenegro relies entirely on soft instruments: the UNDP AI Readiness Assessment (May 2025) and the ongoing AI strategy drafting process provide some published guidance, while MontEDIH (launched January 2025 under Digital Europe) offers one economic/innovation programme; however, there are no binding AI instruments and the relevant bodies—Ministry of Public Administration, Data Protection Agency—have thin resources and no dedicated AI compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralised around the Ministry of Public Administration with the Data Protection Agency playing a secondary data-governance role; no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanism exists, the country is a small unitary state with no subnational AI role, and judicial or independent review of AI matters is only occasional via general administrative and data protection channels.
Actor Involvement: The AI Strategy Working Group (constituted October 2025) brings together representatives from public administration, academia, industry, and civil society in consultative sessions, though these are not standing formal roles; internationally, Montenegro participates in EU Digital Europe and Western Balkans digital cooperation frameworks and engages with UNDP, marking it as a signatory-level participant rather than an active treaty shaper.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists, no enforcement actions have been taken on AI matters, no dedicated AI enforcement authority has been established, and no AI auditing or monitoring practice is in place; the Data Protection Agency has limited enforcement capacity even under the general data protection law.
Specific Laws: Personal Data Protection Law (2008, amended 2024, Official Gazette Nos. 79/2008 and 77/2024), New Personal Data Protection Act (2023, entered into force 1 July 2023), Digital Transformation Strategy 2022–2026, Copyright and Related Rights Act

Regulation Status: Morocco has a draft Digital X.0 Framework Law under review by the General Secretariat of the Government as of mid-2026 — it has not yet been enacted — while the Maroc IA 2030 ('AI Made in Morocco') roadmap was launched in January 2026 as a strategic non-binding instrument. The only binding instruments touching AI remain the general data protection Law No. 09-08 (2009) applied via CNDP, not AI-specific legislation. A bill proposing a National Agency for AI Governance was submitted to the House of Councilors in 2024 but has not passed, meaning no binding, AI-specific statute is in force.
Policy Lever: Morocco's policy toolkit is predominantly soft law: the Maroc IA 2030 roadmap and Maroc Digital 2030 strategy provide strategic guidance, and the JAZARI ROOT Institute Centre of Excellence was created in January 2026 for coordination and skills. Economic programs include sandbox and innovation hub initiatives tied to the Digital Morocco 2030 strategy, but no dedicated AI-specific binding instruments exist yet. CNDP has enforcement capacity for data protection but is not resourced or mandated for AI-specific compliance.
Governance Type: AI governance is effectively centralized under the Ministry of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform, with the CNDP covering data-related aspects; no dedicated AI regulator exists yet, though a National AI Agency has been proposed. Coordination is ad hoc across ministries and there is no sub-national regulatory role, as Morocco is a unitary state with no regional AI rule-making authority.
Actor Involvement: The July 2025 National AI Conference and January 2026 Maroc IA 2030 launch involved industry and academic stakeholders in consultative roles, including MoUs with higher education institutions for the JAZARI ROOT Institute. Internationally, Morocco actively participates in African Union AI governance (AU Continental AI Strategy Phase 1) and maintains digital partnerships with the EU and Gulf states, but domestic civil society has only limited, occasional involvement in formal AI policy processes.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is limited to indirect data-protection channels through CNDP under Law 09-08, which stepped up formal notices and published some sanctions in 2025, but there is no AI-specific enforcement framework, no dedicated AI authority with explicit enforcement powers, and no systematic AI monitoring or public audit regime in place.
Specific Laws: Law No. 09-08 on the Protection of Individuals with Regard to the Processing of Personal Data (2009), Digital X.0 Framework Law (draft, 2025), Maroc IA 2030 National AI Roadmap (2026), Maroc Digital 2030 Strategy (2024), Bill for the Establishment of the National Agency for Artificial Intelligence (2024)

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, Mozambique has no binding AI legislation in force or formally in the legislative process. INTIC conducted a public consultation on the first draft of a National AI Strategy (ENIA) from May 4 to June 4, 2026, with the technical component expected to be finalized and submitted to the Council of Ministers in June 2026—but this remains a pre-adoption draft strategy, not an enacted instrument. Parliament approved a Cybersecurity Law and Cybercrime Law in April 2026 and a draft Personal Data Protection Law is before Parliament, but none of these contain AI-specific provisions.
Policy Lever: Mozambique has no binding AI instruments, but INTIC published a draft National AI Strategy (ENIA) open for public consultation in 2026, supported by UNESCO, ITU, EU, AU, World Bank, and German Cooperation; ITU's 'AI for Good Sandbox' is being piloted in Mozambique, providing a limited economic/sandbox mechanism. INTIC is designated as the lead digital governance body but lacks dedicated AI-specific staffing or compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: No AI-specific governance architecture exists; INTIC, under the Ministry of Communications and Digital Transformation, acts as a single central body for ICT and digital policy including the nascent AI strategy, with no designated AI regulator, coordination mechanism, or independent review body. Sub-national units have no AI governance role.
Actor Involvement: The public consultation on the draft ENIA (May–June 2026) and provincial workshops involved stakeholders from government, private sector, civil society, and academia in an ad hoc, time-limited process. Mozambique presented its strategy at the ITU AI for Good Workshop and is supported by multiple international partners (UNESCO, ITU, EU, AU, World Bank, IRCAI), and has ratified the African Union Convention on Cyber Security and Personal Data Protection.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanism exists; AI-relevant obligations remain nonexistent pending adoption of the National AI Strategy.
Specific Laws: Estratégia Nacional de Inteligência Artificial / National AI Strategy - ENIA (draft, 2026), Cybersecurity Law (2026), Cybercrime Law (2026), Electronic Transactions Law No. 3/2017, Decree No. 71/2025 - Data Centers Regulations, Decree No. 70/2025 - Cloud Computing Regulations

Regulation Status: Myanmar has no binding AI-specific legislation in force as of mid-2026. The Cybersecurity Law No. 1/2025 (in force July 30, 2025) incidentally defines AI systems and regulates digital platforms but is not an AI governance instrument. A National AI Strategy and Policy was still in draft/coordination stage as of early 2025 and had not been published or enacted by mid-2026 according to regional assessments; no horizontal or sectoral AI rules exist.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated AI institutions exist in Myanmar. The Ministry of Science and Technology led drafting coordination meetings but no institutional AI body has been established or resourced.
Governance Type: Myanmar's AI governance space is effectively a vacuum: no lead AI regulator exists, no interagency coordination mechanism for AI has been formalized, and under military (State Administration Council) rule there is no meaningful subnational, judicial, or independent check on AI-related policy.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder engagement in AI policy exists; civil society, industry, and academia have no formal roles. Myanmar nominally follows the ASEAN AI Guide framework but has not actively participated in binding international AI governance instruments or treaty processes.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists; the Cybersecurity Law No. 1/2025 contains digital-platform penalties but no AI-specific enforcement has been observed.
Specific Laws: Cybersecurity Law No. 1/2025, Digital Economy Development Master Plan (2021-2025)

Regulation Status: Namibia has no binding AI-specific legislation in force or formally tabled in parliament as of mid-2026. AI references appear only within broader digital transformation and ICT strategy documents, none of which carry regulatory force. The country remains at the aspirational/strategic stage with no enacted AI rules.
Policy Lever: Namibia's sole policy instrument touching AI is the National Digital Economy Roadmap and related ICT strategies, which are non-binding. There are no dedicated AI funding programs, sandboxes, or procurement schemes, and no institution with a specific AI mandate or compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: AI-adjacent matters in Namibia are handled informally within the Ministry of Information and Communication Technology, with no dedicated AI regulator, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism, no sub-national AI governance role, and no independent body exercising oversight of AI systems.
Actor Involvement: Domestically, there is no structured consultation process involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy. Namibia's international engagement is modest but real through African Union AI policy processes, SADC frameworks, and the Smart Africa Alliance, justifying a mid-range international score.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Namibia; there has been no AI-specific regulatory action of any kind.
Specific Laws: National Digital Economy Roadmap (2023), ICT Policy for Namibia (2019)

Regulation Status: Nauru has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules in force or in any legislative process, and no national AI strategy. The country's extremely limited administrative capacity means no AI governance instruments of any kind exist.
Policy Lever: No binding instruments, no published guidance, no sandbox or funding programs, and no dedicated body with any AI remit exist in Nauru.
Governance Type: Nauru is a micro-state with a single, highly centralized government and no AI governance structure; there are no sectoral regulators, no subnational entities with AI roles, and no independent review mechanisms touching AI.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic involvement from industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy; Nauru participates minimally in international forums as a UN member state but has not signed AI-specific declarations or engaged in AI standard-setting.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice related to AI exists in Nauru.

Regulation Status: Nepal's National AI Policy 2082 (August 2025) is a cabinet-approved non-binding policy document, not enacted legislation; no binding AI-specific law or regulation has been passed by parliament as of June 2026. The policy proposes an AI Regulation Council and commits to enacting AI-related legislation, but these remain forward-looking commitments rather than operational rules. General laws (Individual Privacy Act 2018, E-commerce Act 2025) touch AI incidentally but were not designed as AI instruments.
Policy Lever: The sole instrument is the National AI Policy 2082, a non-binding strategy document with no secondary regulations or enforceable codes; the policy proposes regulatory sandboxes, AI incubation hubs, and provincial AI Excellence Centres, but these programs are aspirational and not yet operational. The proposed AI Regulation Council and National AI Centre have not been formally established with dedicated resources.
Governance Type: AI governance is currently centralized under the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, which led policy drafting; the proposed AI Regulation Council (chaired by the communications minister) and provincial AI Excellence Centres are not yet established, meaning formal multi-body coordination does not yet exist in practice. Nepal's unitary governance structure assigns no independent AI regulatory role to provinces or the judiciary at this stage.
Actor Involvement: The AI Association of Nepal and some industry stakeholders have engaged in policy discourse, and UNDP cooperated on the 2025 Human Development Report launch in Kathmandu, but structured consultative mechanisms with industry, civil society, and academia are not formalized. Nepal has not yet signed major international AI declarations or standards frameworks, though it participates in UN-affiliated digital dialogues.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework exists for AI-specific obligations; the AI Regulation Council proposed as the eventual enforcement body has not been established, no penalties have been defined, and no AI-related monitoring or audit activity has been reported.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy 2082 (2025), Individual Privacy Act 2075 (2018), E-commerce Act 2081 (2025)

Regulation Status: The EU AI Act (2024) applies directly in the Netherlands and is fully in force, with prohibited practices rules applying from February 2025 and high-risk requirements progressively operative through 2026. A national Dutch AI Implementation Act was published for public consultation on 20 April 2026 (expected to go to Parliament in Q4 2026), designating a hybrid supervisory model with ten market surveillance authorities—meaning national binding instruments are drafted but not yet enacted. The AP's DCA already provides dedicated AI/algorithmic oversight under GDPR and national law, giving the Netherlands AI-specific instruments beyond general law.
Policy Lever: Binding instruments include the directly applicable EU AI Act and existing GDPR-based algorithmic oversight by the AP/DCA; the national AI Implementation Act is in consultation. Soft law is mature: the AP publishes biannual RAN reports, issued guidelines on meaningful human intervention (July 2025), a 'Vision on Generative AI' (February 2026), and maintains the national Algorithm Register; the Ministry of Economic Affairs announced a €300M+ AI R&D investment and the National AI Delta Plan (November 2025); the DCA is a staffed, €3.6M-budgeted institution with compliance infrastructure involving ten supervisory authorities.
Governance Type: The Netherlands employs a decentralised hybrid model: the AP/DCA coordinates, with eight to ten sectoral market surveillance authorities (ACM, AFM, RDI, etc.) holding their own AI remits—a high degree of regulator plurality with formally structured inter-agency coordination. Subnational (municipal) bodies implement national rules (e.g., algorithmic registers) but do not independently legislate AI. Courts and the AP's enforcement activity (including the landmark SyRI judgment) actively shape AI rules, and the national ombudsman and independent advisory bodies provide additional checks.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society are consulted through formal public consultation on the April 2026 Implementation Act draft and via the AP's seminar processes, but do not hold standing formal co-decision roles. Academia provides advisory input to the DCA and national AI Delta Plan process. The Netherlands is among the most internationally active AI governance actors: it leads in EU AI governance, participates in OECD AI Policy Observatory, UNESCO AI ethics processes, and Council of Europe AI Convention frameworks.
Enforcement Level: The AP enforces via a comprehensive GDPR/EU AI Act penalty framework (e.g., €30.5M fine against Clearview AI in 2024); the DCA is a dedicated, resourced algorithmic oversight authority; and the AP publishes biannual RAN monitoring reports, routine enforcement priorities, and public enforcement decisions—placing the Netherlands at the global frontier of AI monitoring and enforcement practice.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), General Data Protection Regulation/GDPR (2018), Dutch GDPR Implementation Act/UAVG (2018), Dutch AI Implementation Act draft (2026, in consultation), National AI Delta Plan (2025), Government Algorithm Register policy (2022)

Regulation Status: New Zealand has explicitly rejected a standalone AI Act; the July 2025 AI Strategy 'Investing with Confidence' and the Algorithm Charter are both voluntary instruments. The Privacy Act 2020 (with IPP 3A amendments effective May 2026) and the Biometrics Processing Privacy Code (in force November 2025) provide the only binding rules touching AI, but they are general data-protection instruments applied incidentally to AI rather than horizontal AI legislation. No binding AI-specific law is in force or in the legislative pipeline.
Policy Lever: Binding instruments are limited to the Privacy Act 2020, its Biometrics Privacy Code, and the Customer and Product Data Act 2025—none AI-specific. Soft law is moderately developed: the Algorithm Charter, Public Service AI Framework, Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses, and GenAI guidance for public service constitute a published but non-binding suite. The R&D Tax Incentive and the FMA's 2025 regulatory sandbox pilot offer limited economic tools, while institutional capacity (Privacy Commissioner, MBIE, GCDO) exists but remains thinly resourced for AI oversight.
Governance Type: Governance is spread across the Privacy Commissioner, MBIE, GCDO, Commerce Commission, FMA, and sectoral regulators, with no single AI authority—placing New Zealand in a mid-range plurality. Coordination is ad hoc via Cabinet direction and the AI Strategy rather than through formal statutory mechanisms. As a unitary state, there is no meaningful subnational role. Courts and the Human Rights Review Tribunal provide some independent review of Privacy Act matters, but proactive independent checks on AI specifically are limited.
Actor Involvement: Industry is engaged through published consultations, the AI Forum NZ, and voluntary guidance development, while civil society and academia have only occasional advisory input with no standing formal roles. Internationally, New Zealand is a GPAI member, has adopted OECD AI Principles, and cooperates through Five Eyes and AUKUS-adjacent arrangements, reflecting active but non-treaty-level participation.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is anchored in Privacy Act complaints handled by the Privacy Commissioner (over 1,000 per year) and referred to the Human Rights Review Tribunal, but financial penalties remain minimal and no large AI-specific fines have been levied; the Biometrics Privacy Code adds a targeted code-based enforcement mechanism from November 2025, but the Algorithm Charter carries no sanctions, and there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority or systematic AI audit regime.
Specific Laws: Privacy Act 2020, Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand (2020), Public Service AI Framework (2024), New Zealand AI Strategy: Investing with Confidence (2025), Privacy Amendment Act 2025, Biometrics Processing Privacy Code (2025), Customer and Product Data Act 2025, Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI (2025)

Regulation Status: As of mid-2026, Nicaragua has no binding AI-specific legislation, no draft AI bill under formal legislative consideration, and no AI-focused executive orders in force. The only AI-relevant activity was a June 2023 National Assembly task force agreement to develop proposals, which produced no enacted instrument; existing laws such as the Special Cybercrime Law (No. 1042, 2020) and the National Cybersecurity Strategy 2020–2025 touch digital activity incidentally but contain no AI provisions.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, published AI guidance, sandboxes, funding programs, or dedicated AI institutional bodies exist; Nicaragua relies entirely on pre-existing general ICT and cybercrime frameworks with no AI-specific policy infrastructure.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated AI regulator or multi-body coordination structure; AI oversight would theoretically fall to general telecommunications and cybercrime authorities under a highly centralized state, with no subnational AI role and no active independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academic actors have no structured role in AI governance; Nicaragua's only international AI engagement is marginal participation in regional forums (e.g., CELAC), and it signed the Santiago Declaration, placing it at the lowest tier of international involvement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions, enforcement actions, dedicated enforcement authority, or monitoring mechanisms exist in Nicaragua.
Specific Laws: Special Law on Cybercrimes No. 1042 (2020), National Cybersecurity Strategy 2020–2025 (Presidential Decree No. 24-2020)

Regulation Status: Niger has no binding AI legislation, no draft AI laws in the legislative pipeline, and no AI-specific regulatory instruments of any kind. The country remains under military governance following the 2023 coup, and no AI governance framework has been developed or proposed as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments — binding or soft — exist for AI in Niger. There is no national AI strategy, no AI-related guidelines, no sandbox programs, and no dedicated institutional body with any AI remit.
Governance Type: There is no governance structure for AI in Niger: no designated regulator, no inter-agency coordination mechanism, no sub-national AI role, and no independent or judicial review function related to AI.
Actor Involvement: No structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy has been documented; Niger's suspension from the African Union following the 2023 coup further limits meaningful international AI governance participation.
Enforcement Level: There are no AI-related enforcement mechanisms, no penalties, no designated enforcement authority, and no monitoring or auditing activity of any kind.

Regulation Status: Nigeria has enacted the Nigeria Data Protection Act (2023) and published its National AI Strategy (NAIS, September 2025), but as of June 2026 the National Digital Economy and E-Governance Bill — which would introduce binding, risk-tiered AI rules and a National AI Commission — remains in the legislative process (expected Q2 2026 enactment) and has not yet been signed into law. The NITDA Code of Practice for AI (2025) provides soft governance but no dedicated binding AI statute is yet in force, keeping binding force and specificity at nascent levels.
Policy Lever: Nigeria's binding instruments remain general (NDPA 2023, cybercrime law) with no dedicated binding AI instrument yet; soft-law consists of NITDA's AI Code of Practice and sectoral guidelines, plus the published NAIS strategy. Sandbox provisions are proposed in the pending bill, and NITDA and the NDPC have designated but thinly resourced roles on AI matters.
Governance Type: Nigeria operates a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure: NITDA coordinates digital/AI policy, NDPC handles data protection, and NCC governs telecoms, with ad hoc rather than formally institutionalized coordination across them. Subnational governments play no independent AI regulatory role, and judicial or independent review of AI governance is occasional at best.
Actor Involvement: Industry has participated in consultations on the bill and NAIS development; civil society and academia have had limited advisory input (e.g., Tunaní policy brief critiques March 2026). Internationally, Nigeria is active in the AU AI governance process, the Smart Africa Alliance, and has aligned with the Bletchley Declaration and other multilateral AI safety commitments, reflecting strong international engagement.
Enforcement Level: The pending bill proposes fines up to NGN 10 million or 2% of annual Nigerian revenue for non-compliance, but no dedicated AI enforcement authority yet exists with explicit remit or resources; enforcement of AI-specific rules has not occurred, and routine AI-specific auditing or public monitoring reporting is absent.
Specific Laws: Nigeria Data Protection Act (2023), National Artificial Intelligence Strategy — NAIS (2025), NITDA Code of Practice for Artificial Intelligence (2025), National Digital Economy and E-Governance Bill (2024, pending)

Regulation Status: North Korea has no publicly documented AI-specific legislation, regulation, or formal policy framework. All technology, including any AI development, falls under the opaque general state security and party apparatus with no evidence of dedicated AI governance instruments.
Policy Lever: No AI policy instruments of any kind are publicly documented. The state monopoly on technology and information provides de facto control, but there are no known published strategies, guidance documents, sandboxes, or designated AI institutions.
Governance Type: All governance is concentrated in the Korean Workers' Party and state security apparatus; there are no independent regulators, no sub-national AI roles, and no non-governmental checks or judicial review mechanisms of any kind.
Actor Involvement: North Korea is fully isolated from international AI governance frameworks, participates in no multilateral AI agreements or standards bodies, and permits no independent domestic industry, civil society, or academic input into technology policy.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists; technology control is exercised through general state security mechanisms with no publicly observable AI-specific compliance or enforcement activity.

Regulation Status: Norway published a draft dedicated AI Act (KI-loven) for public consultation in June 2025, mirroring the EU AI Act's risk-based approach, with entry into force targeted for August 2026; as of June 2026 the act has not yet been formally enacted by the Stortinget and the EU AI Act has not yet been incorporated into the EEA Agreement, leaving binding force still at draft/legislative-process stage. A new Public Administration Act adopted June 2025 (not yet in force) introduces AI rules for public-sector automated decision-making, adding a second AI-specific instrument in progress. General law (GDPR/Personal Data Act) provides background coverage but dedicated AI-specific instruments are clearly the primary framing.
Policy Lever: No binding AI-specific instrument is yet in force (draft KI-loven pending); however Datatilsynet operates a mature AI sandbox and publishes extensive AI-specific guidance, Nkom is designated as the coordinating supervisory authority, and KI-Norge has been established as a national AI innovation arena, giving Norway a solid but not yet fully operational compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: Norway operates a lead-body-plus-sectoral model: Nkom is the proposed coordinating market surveillance authority, Datatilsynet covers data-protection dimensions, and sectoral authorities are being designated; coordination mechanisms exist but are still being formalised around the forthcoming KI-loven. Norway is a unitary state with no sub-national AI regulatory role; courts and the parliamentary ombudsman provide independent review of administrative AI use.
Actor Involvement: The draft KI-loven underwent a public consultation (June–September 2025) open to industry, civil society, and academia; Norway participates actively as an EEA/EFTA observer in the EU AI Board, contributes to OECD AI Policy Observatory, and is engaged in Nordic DPA cooperation and International Network of AI Safety Institutes discussions, reflecting strong international but structured domestic involvement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework is yet in force; Datatilsynet has taken GDPR-based enforcement actions touching AI (e.g. Grindr fine upheld 2025) and operates an AI sandbox for monitoring, but dedicated AI enforcement authority and routine AI-specific audit/reporting mechanisms await the entry into force of the KI-loven.
Specific Laws: Draft KI-loven / Norwegian AI Act (2025, consultation draft), Public Administration Act amendment for AI automated decisions (2025, adopted but not in force), Personal Data Act (2018, implementing GDPR), EU AI Act Regulation (EU) 2024/1968 (EEA incorporation pending)

Regulation Status: Oman has published the General Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (2025) through the MTCIT, which is a policy framework rather than binding legislation. No AI-specific law has been enacted; the 2025 policy and the National Programme for AI and Advanced Digital Technologies (2024–2026) remain non-binding instruments covering public and private entities without legislative force.
Policy Lever: Policy tools are limited to non-binding national strategy frameworks and the 2025 AI policy document providing ethical guidelines and governance principles; a WEF Centre for the Fourth Industrial Revolution partnership was signed in January 2025 and public-private partnerships exist, but no binding regulations or comprehensive enforcement infrastructure are in place.
Governance Type: The MTCIT serves as the single lead coordinator for AI governance through the National Programme, with sectoral ministries expected to implement guidance in their domains, but there is no formal multi-regulator coordination mechanism, no meaningful subnational role given Oman's unitary structure, and no independent oversight or judicial review body specific to AI.
Actor Involvement: Industry and academia were consulted during the draft AI policy process, but civil society engagement in the domestic process is minimal; internationally, Oman participates in GCC cooperation, signed a WEF 4IR Centre agreement in January 2025, and is assessed under UNESCO's AI Ethics Observatory.
Enforcement Level: No penalties or sanctions framework exists for AI-specific obligations; the MTCIT is designated to monitor government entity compliance and report to the Council of Ministers, but no dedicated AI enforcement authority with resources exists and no enforcement actions have been taken.
Specific Laws: National Programme for Artificial Intelligence and Advanced Digital Technologies (2024), General Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (2025)

Regulation Status: Pakistan has no binding AI legislation in force as of June 2026. The National AI Policy (July 2025) is a cabinet-approved strategic document, and the Islamabad AI Declaration (February 2026) is a non-binding political commitment. The Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Act 2024 was introduced in the Senate but not passed, and the Personal Data Protection Bill remains unenacted. AI-related activity is governed only incidentally through PECA 2016, giving minimal AI-specific statutory coverage and no implemented regulatory framework.
Policy Lever: Pakistan relies entirely on soft-law instruments: the National AI Policy 2025, the Islamabad AI Declaration 2026, and the proposed AI Council and AI Supervisory Framework under the Pakistan Digital Authority. A USD 1 billion AI investment commitment and AI innovation/venture funds have been announced for 2030, but no binding instruments are in force. The Pakistan Digital Authority has been designated as the lead AI governance body but lacks operational regulatory infrastructure.
Governance Type: Pakistan Digital Authority serves as the designated national AI governance and supervisory body, with PTA (telecom) and SBP (banking) providing sectoral coverage and provincial governments nominally included in the AI Council. Coordination between MOITT and PDA is emerging but largely ad hoc; no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism is operational. Subnational governments are referenced in the AI Council structure but exercise no independent AI regulatory role, and judicial or independent review of AI decisions remains incidental rather than systematic.
Actor Involvement: The National AI Policy 2025 was developed through consultations with industry, academia, civil society, and the public sector, and the Indus AI Week 2026 engaged startups, students, and global industry leaders. Civil society was consulted during policy drafting but has no standing formal role in ongoing governance. Pakistan has adopted the Islamabad AI Declaration, participates in bilateral AI partnerships (China, Gulf states), and hosted the Indus AI Summit 2026 with international participants, reflecting growing but mostly declaratory international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No sanctions framework for AI exists; penalties are deferred to future sector-specific rulemaking under the National AI Policy. The Pakistan Digital Authority has been tasked with operationalizing a supervisory framework but has not done so, no enforcement actions have been taken, and no AI audit or monitoring mechanisms are in place.
Specific Laws: National AI Policy (2025), Islamabad AI Declaration on Sovereign, Responsible, and Capability-Driven Artificial Intelligence (2026), Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill (2024, Senate, not enacted), Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act (2016)

Regulation Status: Palau has no AI-specific legislation, executive orders, or binding regulatory instruments of any kind as of mid-2026. The country's policy focus remains on foundational digital infrastructure and connectivity rather than AI governance.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, published guidance, dedicated funding programs, or institutional bodies with an AI remit exist in Palau; the government lacks the administrative capacity to operate such mechanisms.
Governance Type: Palau has no AI regulatory structure whatsoever; there is no designated authority, no coordination mechanism, and no sub-national or non-governmental body involved in AI governance.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academic actors have no structured role in AI policy; Palau participates minimally in international digital governance through its UN membership and Pacific regional forums but has not signed AI-specific multilateral declarations.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, designated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Palau.

Regulation Status: Panama has no binding AI-specific legislation in force as of June 2026. Draft Bill No. 14 (August 2024) and Draft Bill No. 0016 (July 2025) are both pending approval in the National Assembly, keeping the country in a pre-legislative stage. Existing coverage relies entirely on general instruments — Law 81 of 2019 (data protection) and Law 144 of 2020 (digital government) — that incidentally touch AI without addressing it explicitly.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to general digital-governance laws and soft guidance: the AIG's 2021 National AI Strategy (ENIA) provides non-binding principles, and SENACYT is co-developing a new national AI strategy with Georgia Tech (workshops July–September 2025, not yet finalized). The IDB-funded quinquennial plan includes AI components and SENACYT launched a public AI survey, but no dedicated binding AI instrument or fully staffed AI institution exists.
Governance Type: AI oversight is fragmented across AIG (digital government innovation), SENACYT (R&D strategy), and ANTAI (data-protection enforcement), with no formal domestic AI coordination mechanism; coordination occurs ad hoc and mainly through international/regional forums. Panama is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulation, and ANTAI's mandate under Law 81 provides occasional quasi-judicial review for data-related AI complaints.
Actor Involvement: SENACYT's July 2025 multi-stakeholder workshops convened government, academia, civil society (including IPANDETEC), and private sector in a consultative co-design process for the new AI strategy, but no standing formal roles exist. Panama is a signatory to the UNESCO Recommendation on AI Ethics and the Santiago Declaration and participates in the Ibero-American Network of Data Protection authorities.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, or dedicated authority exists; ANTAI can enforce data-protection rules under Law 81 that may incidentally apply to AI systems, but there are no documented AI-specific enforcement actions, audits, or public monitoring reports.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy / ENIA (2021), Law 81 on Personal Data Protection (2019), Executive Decree 285 (2021), Law 144 on Government Digital Transformation (2020), Law 478 on Cybercrime Amendments (2025), Draft Bill No. 14 on AI Regulation (2024, pending), Draft Bill No. 0016 on AI Regulation (2025, pending)

Regulation Status: Papua New Guinea has no dedicated AI legislation, no AI-specific binding instruments proposed or enacted as of June 2026. The only relevant instrument is the Digital Government Act (2022), which provides general digital governance provisions with no AI-specific provisions, leaving binding_force, scope, implementation, and ai_specificity all at the minimum level.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, soft-law guidance, dedicated AI funding programs, sandboxes, or institutional bodies with AI mandates exist; the Digital Government Act (2022) is the sole operative digital governance instrument and does not establish any AI-specific policy levers.
Governance Type: AI governance responsibilities are unassigned; there is no lead AI regulator, no inter-agency coordination mechanism, no subnational AI regulatory role, and no independent review body or court practice shaping AI rules in Papua New Guinea.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia are not engaged in any structured AI policy process; Papua New Guinea participates marginally in regional digital cooperation through the Pacific Islands Forum, which provides the only minimal international AI governance connection.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists, no enforcement actions have been taken, no authority holds an AI enforcement mandate, and no monitoring or audit practice related to AI has been documented.
Specific Laws: Digital Government Act (2022)

Regulation Status: Paraguay has no dedicated AI legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no AI-specific bill in active legislative process as of mid-2026. The country relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks that only incidentally touch AI systems, with no horizontal or sectoral AI coverage.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, soft-law guidance, AI-specific funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies have been established; Paraguay remains entirely without AI-specific policy infrastructure.
Governance Type: There is no designated AI regulator, no inter-agency coordination mechanism for AI, no sub-national AI governance role, and no independent review body with an AI mandate; governance of AI-adjacent issues falls to general technology regulators on an ad hoc basis.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic consultations involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy have been recorded; Paraguay's only visible AI governance activity is participation in regional declarations such as the Santiago Declaration on AI in Latin America through ECLAC/CEPAL processes.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Paraguay.

Regulation Status: Peru enacted Law No. 31814 (July 2023), the first horizontal AI statute in Latin America, covering public and private sectors with a risk-based framework (prohibited, high-risk, acceptable). Supreme Decree No. 115-2025-PCM (September 2025) issued comprehensive implementing regulations, which entered into force on January 13, 2026, with staggered compliance timelines of 1–3 years for public entities and some sectors, so the framework is binding and in force but not yet fully operational across all domains.
Policy Lever: The main binding instruments are Law 31814 and Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM; Emergency Decree 007-2020 on digital ethics also applies. The SGTD is designated as the normative authority and the regulations reference sandboxes (CNIDIA) and a National AI Strategy (ENIA) to 2030, but these programs are nascent and the SGTD remains thinly resourced relative to the scope of its mandate.
Governance Type: The SGTD/PCM serves as lead AI authority with sectoral regulators (ANPD for data protection, sectoral ministries) sharing oversight, but there is no standalone AI regulator and coordination is emerging rather than fully formalized. Subnational governments are subject to national rules with phased compliance deadlines but have no independent AI regulatory role; judicial and independent review is available but not actively shaping AI rules yet.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society were consulted during drafting of the implementing regulations (public consultation in 2024), but there are no standing formal roles; academia has limited advisory input. Peru is active internationally—aligning its framework with OECD AI Principles, UNESCO Recommendation, and the EU AI Act model, and participating in Santiago/Cartagena Declarations.
Enforcement Level: The regulations rely on existing sectoral and data-protection frameworks for sanctions rather than creating new AI-specific penalties; the SGTD is designated for preventive monitoring but lacks dedicated resources, and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly reported as of early 2026.
Specific Laws: Law No. 31814 - Ley que promueve el uso de la inteligencia artificial en favor del desarrollo económico y social del país (2023), Supreme Decree No. 115-2025-PCM - Reglamento de la Ley N° 31814 (2025), Emergency Decree No. 007-2020 (2020)

Regulation Status: The Philippines has no enacted horizontal AI legislation as of mid-2026; multiple draft bills (House Bill 7396/Artificial Intelligence Development Act, Senate Bill 25/AIRA, and related measures) remain in the legislative process. The only operative AI-specific binding instruments are narrow sector rules, notably COMELEC's AI-disclosure requirements for the 2025 elections, while the National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 (NAISR 2.0, 2024) and related DTI/DICT/DOST roadmaps provide strategic direction without legal force.
Policy Lever: Binding instruments are limited to COMELEC's election-campaign AI disclosure rules; soft-law coverage includes NAISR 2.0, DICT's people-centric AI guidelines, and NPC privacy-by-design guidance, forming a partial but not yet mature suite. Institutional capacity is fragmented across DTI, DICT, DOST, and NPC with no dedicated AI authority (AIDA) yet established, and economic tools are limited to a digital-investment masterplan and HPC infrastructure commitments rather than operational sandboxes.
Governance Type: AI governance is distributed across DICT (coordination lead), DTI (industry/economic), DOST (R&D), and NPC (data privacy/algorithmic accountability), plus COMELEC for elections, making for a multi-body landscape with only ad hoc inter-agency coordination and no formal AI governance council yet. Sub-national governments play no independent AI regulatory role, and judicial or ombudsman review of AI systems remains incidental rather than systematic.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates through ASEAN-BAC consultations and DTI working groups; civil society and academia have some input into legislative hearings and UNESCO engagement but lack formal standing roles. The Philippines' 2026 ASEAN chairmanship and active push for binding regional AI governance, plus participation in ASEAN Digital Economy Framework Agreement and UNESCO AI Ethics processes, places it among the more internationally engaged developing economies.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is primarily through NPC (privacy-related AI complaints) and COMELEC (election AI disclosure), with defined penalties under existing data-privacy law applied incidentally to AI; no dedicated AI enforcement authority or routine AI-specific audit/monitoring regime exists pending enactment of pending bills.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 / NAISR 2.0 (2024), COMELEC AI Campaign Guidelines (2025), Data Privacy Act (Republic Act 10173, 2012), House Bill 7396 – Artificial Intelligence Development Act of the Philippines (pending, 2023), Senate Bill 25 – Artificial Intelligence Regulation Act (pending, 2025)

Regulation Status: Poland is subject to the directly applicable EU AI Act (in force since August 2024, with prohibitions since February 2025 and GPAI rules since August 2025), making binding horizontal AI rules operative. Poland's Council of Ministers adopted a draft Act on AI Systems on 31 March 2026 to transpose and complement the EU AI Act domestically, but as of June 2026 the bill is still in parliamentary proceedings (committee stage in the Sejm), so the national implementing layer is not yet enacted. The EU AI Act itself provides full cross-sector horizontal coverage, but Poland's own dedicated national AI statute remains a draft, placing it at a partially-implemented transition stage.
Policy Lever: The binding EU AI Act is directly applicable; Poland's domestic implementing act is pending enactment. UODO has issued guidance on AI and DPIA, and Poland published an AI Development Policy 2025–2030. Economic tools include a PLN 1 billion (~EUR 235 million) AI Fund announced in late 2024, an AI Council, and regulatory sandboxes proposed in the draft bill. KRiBSI is designated in the draft bill but not yet operational; interim oversight sits with UODO and the Ministry of Digital Affairs.
Governance Type: Poland's draft law creates a single centralized AI authority (KRiBSI) as the sole national market surveillance authority—an outlier among EU Member States—with operational support inside the Ministry of Digital Affairs, indicating low regulator plurality. An AI Council and a multistakeholder PL/AI advisory board provide cross-governmental and expert coordination, and UODO's parliamentary opinion represents independent checks; courts and the UODO can and do review AI-related cases under GDPR.
Actor Involvement: Public consultations were held on successive drafts of the national AI bill, and the PL/AI multistakeholder advisory board includes industry and academic representatives. Civil society and academia were consulted but lack standing formal roles. Internationally, Poland participated actively in the EU AI Board, the OECD AI Policy Observatory, aligned with the EU Coordinated Plan on AI, and wielded significant influence during its H1 2025 EU Council Presidency—including proposing the Digital Omnibus GPAI postponement measures.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's penalty framework (fines up to €35 million or 7% of turnover) applies in Poland, and the draft national act codifies administrative sanctions and inspection powers for KRiBSI, but as the domestic bill has not yet been enacted and KRiBSI is not operational, no dedicated authority with explicit AI resources yet exists and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been taken; UODO provides interim oversight through GDPR-based monitoring.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, 2024); Act on Artificial Intelligence Systems – Draft adopted by Council of Ministers (March 2026, pending Parliament); Policy for AI Development in Poland until 2030 (2024); AI Act – Digital Omnibus Amendment Proposal (2025)

Regulation Status: Portugal applies the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) directly, which entered into force August 2024 with phased application through 2026; prohibitions and transparency rules applied from early 2025, and full high-risk AI obligations entered force August 2026. Portugal also has existing binding instruments including Law No. 58/2019 (GDPR implementation) and consumer/data laws incidentally covering AI. The AI Act is dedicated AI-specific legislation with horizontal cross-sector scope, but national implementation measures (penalty rules, implementing act designating ANACOM) were only formally in place by September 2025, placing implementation in partial/transition status as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: Portugal relies on the directly applicable EU AI Act as its primary binding instrument plus GDPR-based rules; soft law includes EU-level codes of practice and national guidance from ANACOM and CNPD; economic tools include FCT-managed AI Portugal 2030 funding programs, participation in AI Factory initiatives, and the national LLM (Amália) project. ANACOM was designated as market surveillance authority in September 2025 and coordinates with sectoral regulators, though dedicated AI enforcement resources remain developing.
Governance Type: Portugal has a lead AI authority (ANACOM as market surveillance and single point of contact) coordinating with sectoral regulators (Banco de Portugal, CMVM, CNPD), creating a lead-plus-sectoral structure; coordination is formalized through the EU AI Act framework but domestic inter-agency mechanisms are still being established. Portugal is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulation, and judicial review exists but has not yet produced AI-specific case law.
Actor Involvement: The National AI Agenda (Agenda Nacional de IA, presented for public consultation in January 2025) included a Committee of Accompaniment with industry, academia, and civil society representatives; Portugal participates actively in the EU AI Board and EU Coordinated Plan on AI, and maintains Lusophone digital cooperation, reflecting meaningful but not institutionally entrenched multi-stakeholder engagement.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act provides a comprehensive penalty framework (fines up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover) directly applicable in Portugal, and ANACOM was formally designated as the national market surveillance authority in September 2025, but as of June 2026 no published enforcement actions against AI systems have been recorded and routine monitoring infrastructure is still being built.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Law No. 58/2019 - GDPR Implementation (2019), Law No. 24/96 - Consumer Protection Law (1996, applicable to AI), Decree-Law No. 57/2008 - Unfair Commercial Practices (2008, applicable to AI), AI Portugal 2030 National Strategy (2019), Estratégia Digital Nacional (2024), Agenda Nacional de Inteligência Artificial (2025)

Regulation Status: Qatar has no comprehensive horizontal AI law in force as of mid-2026; binding instruments are sector-specific: the Qatar Central Bank AI Guideline (September 2024) is enforceable on licensed financial institutions, the QFC Practice Direction No. 1 of 2026 governs AI use in judicial proceedings before QFC courts, and QFMA draft AI regulations for capital markets were consulted in May 2025 but not yet enacted. The overarching National AI Strategy (2019) and MCIT's Ethical Principles and Guidelines for AI (2025) remain non-binding soft-law instruments, leaving coverage patchy and implementation partial.
Policy Lever: Binding instruments are limited to the QCB AI Guideline (September 2024) and the QFC Practice Direction No. 1 of 2026; soft-law output has grown with MCIT's AI ethical principles (2025) and NCSA's Secure AI Guidelines (2024), forming a modest but incomplete suite; economic tools include QFC sandboxes and the Fanar platform investment, but no broad AI funding programme; MCIT and the QCB have designated AI-related responsibilities without deeply resourced dedicated enforcement units.
Governance Type: Qatar is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance; MCIT leads national AI strategy coordination while QCB, QFMA, NCSA, and the QFC each address AI within their sectoral remits, constituting a lead-plus-sectoral model, but formal cross-body coordination mechanisms are ad hoc rather than institutionalised; the NDPO provides independent data-protection review and QFC courts have begun shaping AI conduct rules, offering limited but growing independent checks.
Actor Involvement: Industry engages through QCB and QFMA consultation processes and QFC sandbox arrangements; domestic civil society has no structured role in Qatar's AI governance process; academia contributes through QCRI and university partnerships but lacks formal standing advisory roles; internationally Qatar participates in GCC AI cooperation, UNESCO AI ethics initiatives, and hosted the World Summit AI 2025 where 14 strategic agreements were signed, placing it as a regional participant in multilateral AI governance dialogues.
Enforcement Level: The NDPO has issued binding enforcement decisions under the PDPPL in 2025 (covering data-related AI risks), and the QCB AI Guideline carries supervisory authority over licensed entities, but there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority, no published AI-specific enforcement actions, and monitoring is sector-siloed and ad hoc rather than routine or publicly reported across domains.
Specific Laws: Qatar National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2019), Personal Data Privacy Protection Law / Law No. 13 of 2016, Qatar Central Bank AI Guideline (2024), NCSA Guidelines for Secure Usage and Adoption of AI (2024), MCIT Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of AI (2025), QFC Practice Direction No. 1 of 2026 on the Use of Artificial Intelligence

Regulation Status: Romania, as an EU member state, is subject to the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), which entered into force in August 2024 and is directly binding law. The Act provides horizontal cross-sector coverage, though most operational obligations (for high-risk systems etc.) are phasing in through 2026–2027; Romania has been slower than western EU peers in designating its national competent authority and issuing supplementary national guidance, placing implementation in transition.
Policy Lever: The primary binding instrument is the EU AI Act itself; Romania has not enacted standalone domestic AI legislation. National soft-law output is limited — no mature domestic guidance suite exists — and economic tools are largely limited to participation in EU-level programs (e.g., Digital Europe Programme, AI TEF networks) rather than dedicated national initiatives. ANSPDCP has been designated as the likely market surveillance / supervisory authority but remains thinly resourced for AI-specific tasks.
Governance Type: Romania's AI governance centres on ANSPDCP (data protection authority) as the expected lead supervisory body under the EU AI Act, with sectoral regulators (financial, health) potentially carrying responsibilities in their domains; formal cross-body coordination mechanisms are still ad hoc and not yet institutionalised. Romania is a unitary state so sub-national entities play no independent role, and courts can in principle review AI-related decisions under existing administrative and GDPR frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Domestic consultation processes on AI are limited and largely reactive to EU-level processes; industry, civil society and academia participate mainly through EU-level channels (e.g., AI Office stakeholder consultations, GPAI) rather than structured national forums. Romania participates in EU AI governance structures including the EU AI Board and has signed relevant EU-level declarations, placing it solidly in international engagement.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's penalty framework (up to €35 million or 7% of turnover) is directly applicable in Romania, but as of mid-2026 no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly recorded domestically; ANSPDCP is designated but lacks a dedicated, fully resourced AI enforcement unit, and systematic monitoring/audit practice under the AI Act has not yet been publicly reported for Romania.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Romania National AI Strategy (2020), GDPR as implemented by Law No. 190/2018

Regulation Status: Russia has no comprehensive binding AI law in force as of June 2026; a dedicated draft law 'On the Fundamentals of State Regulation of the Application of Artificial Intelligence,' released by the Ministry of Digital Development on 18 March 2026 with public consultation closing 15 April 2026, is now in the legislative pipeline targeting State Duma consideration but may not come into force until September 2027 at the earliest. The existing normative base consists of the National AI Strategy (Presidential Decree No. 490, 2019, updated 2024), Federal Law No. 123-FZ (experimental legal regime for Moscow), and Federal Law No. 258-FZ (national sandbox framework), providing AI-specific language but only within limited experimental and sectoral contexts rather than a horizontal binding statute.
Policy Lever: Policy levers are led by strategic instruments (National AI Strategy 2030, updated 2024) and sandbox legislation (FZ-123, FZ-258), with mandatory civil liability insurance for sandbox operators under the 2024 update to FZ-123; soft guidance includes AI ethics principles and sector-specific guidance from Mintsifry; economic tools include state-funded compute infrastructure and sandbox programs, though no comprehensive multi-domain binding instrument yet exists. Institutional capacity exists across multiple agencies (Mintsifry, Roskomnadzor, a new Presidential Commission on AI established by Decree No. 116 of 26 February 2026) but remains fragmented and without a single resourced enforcement authority.
Governance Type: Governance is highly state-centric: AI policy authority is concentrated at the federal level across the Ministry of Digital Development, Roskomnadzor, and since February 2026 the Presidential Commission on AI Technologies, with a centralized AI headquarters also directed by Putin in November 2025; sub-national roles are limited to implementing experimental regimes in designated regions such as Moscow and Tatarstan. Independent judicial or civil-society oversight of AI is essentially absent; courts have not been observed to shape AI rules and no ombudsman or independent review body exists.
Actor Involvement: Industry players (Sberbank, Yandex, Delovaya Rossiya) have participated in State Duma working groups and public consultations on the 2026 draft law, giving them a structured but non-standing role; civil society is effectively excluded from domestic AI governance processes; academia provided some input during the 2019/2024 strategy revision but holds no formal standing advisory role; internationally, Russia is geopolitically isolated from Western AI governance forums but promotes AI cooperation within BRICS and CIS and has established a BRICS AI Study Group on common safety and ethics standards.
Enforcement Level: No comprehensive AI-specific sanctions framework is yet in force; the 2026 draft law proposes liability for developers and mandatory insurance for high-risk operators, but until enacted penalties remain defined only for narrow sandbox breaches; the new Presidential Commission and AI headquarters provide coordination but are not resourced enforcement authorities, and no pattern of published AI-specific enforcement actions has been observed.
Specific Laws: National Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence until 2030, Presidential Decree No. 490 (2019, updated 2024); Federal Law No. 123-FZ on experimental legal regime in Moscow (2019, updated 2024); Federal Law No. 258-FZ on experimental legal regimes for digital innovation (2020); Presidential Decree No. 116 establishing the Presidential Commission on AI Technologies (2026); Draft Law On the Fundamentals of State Regulation of the Application of Artificial Intelligence (2026, in legislative process)

Regulation Status: Rwanda adopted Africa's first dedicated National AI Policy in April 2023, a non-binding Cabinet-approved document; no binding AI legislation has been enacted as of mid-2026. The policy covers strategic AI priorities broadly but creates no enforceable obligations, relying on general ICT and data-protection law (Law No. 058/2021) for any legal effect. AI specificity is high for the continent given the dedicated standalone policy instrument, but binding force and full implementation remain absent.
Policy Lever: Rwanda has no binding AI-specific instruments; soft-law output consists of the 2023 National AI Policy and ethical AI guidelines being developed by RURA. Economic tools include C4IR Rwanda's AI Scaling Hub backed by a $17.5M Gates Foundation commitment and the WEF C4IR partnership. The Responsible AI Office called for in the policy is recommended under MINICT but has not been confirmed as fully staffed and operational.
Governance Type: AI governance is centralized in MINICT as lead ministry, with RURA developing ethical guidelines and NCSA enforcing data protection; formal inter-agency coordination mechanisms for AI are nascent rather than institutionalized. Rwanda is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI specifically has not been established.
Actor Involvement: Over 120 private sector, civil society, and academic actors participated in workshops during the 2023 policy drafting, but ongoing formal roles in implementation are limited. Internationally, Rwanda is highly active: hosting the inaugural Global AI Summit on Africa (Kigali, April 2025), leading the Smart Africa Alliance, co-sponsoring the Africa AI Council, engaging with AU AI governance frameworks, and participating in WEF, ISO, and G20 AI processes.
Enforcement Level: There is no sanctions framework specific to AI; the NCSA enforces data-protection law with defined penalties that can incidentally cover AI data processing, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been recorded, and monitoring of the National AI Policy relies on self-reported stakeholder feedback and international benchmarking indices rather than formal audits.
Specific Laws: National AI Policy (2023), Law No. 058/2021 Relating to the Protection of Personal Data and Privacy

Regulation Status: South Sudan has no AI governance framework of any kind as of June 2026. No binding AI legislation exists or has been proposed; the country remains in a state of conflict and institutional fragility that precludes policy development. General laws do not incidentally address AI in any meaningful way.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies for AI exist. The country lacks the basic digital governance infrastructure needed to develop or implement any AI-specific policy mechanisms.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure whatsoever; no regulatory authority, no coordination mechanism, no subnational role, and no independent oversight body has any AI remit. State fragility and ongoing conflict prevent any such structures from forming.
Actor Involvement: No structured engagement from industry, civil society, or academia on AI regulation exists domestically. South Sudan does not meaningfully participate in international AI governance forums, though international organizations provide limited humanitarian digital support unrelated to AI policy.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, no designated authority, no penalties, and no monitoring related to AI, consistent with the complete absence of any AI regulatory instrument.

Regulation Status: Saint Kitts and Nevis has no enacted AI legislation, no draft AI bills in the legislative process, and no published national AI strategy as of June 2026. General technology and data protection laws touch digital activity incidentally but contain no AI-specific provisions.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, soft-law guidance, AI-focused funding programs, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies have been established; AI governance remains entirely absent from the policy toolkit.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI regulatory authority exists at any level of government; as a unitary microstate Saint Kitts and Nevis has no subnational AI governance, no inter-agency coordination mechanisms, and no documented independent or judicial review of AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: No structured consultations with industry, civil society, or academia on AI governance have been documented, and Saint Kitts and Nevis has not been identified as a signatory to any international AI governance declaration or framework as of mid-2026.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, designated authority, or monitoring mechanisms of any kind exist in Saint Kitts and Nevis.

Regulation Status: Saint Lucia has enacted no dedicated AI legislation and no binding AI-specific rules are in force or formally proposed as of mid-2026. The country relies on existing general laws and participates in CARICOM regional coordination, but no domestically binding AI instrument exists at any stage of the legislative process.
Policy Lever: No national AI policy instruments—binding or soft—have been implemented at the domestic level; Saint Lucia has no dedicated AI funding programs, sandboxes, or institutional body with an AI mandate.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated AI regulator or formal multi-body coordination structure in Saint Lucia; governance remains entirely informal and channeled through regional CARICOM processes rather than any domestic institutional arrangement.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic consultation with industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy has been recorded; Saint Lucia's only AI governance engagement is at the international/regional level through CARICOM and adherence to the Santiago Declaration on AI.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanism exists in Saint Lucia.

Regulation Status: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines has no dedicated AI legislation enacted or formally proposed as of June 2026; the country relies entirely on general telecommunications, consumer protection, and electronic transactions laws that do not explicitly address AI systems. There is no binding AI-specific rule of any kind in force or in the legislative pipeline.
Policy Lever: No national AI strategy, guidelines, sandboxes, or dedicated funding programs have been established; the country participates informally in CARICOM technology discussions but has not translated any of this into domestic policy instruments or institutional capacity for AI governance.
Governance Type: AI-adjacent oversight defaults to the National Telecommunications Regulatory Commission and general consumer-protection bodies, which operate as a single de facto authority with no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism for AI; the country is a unitary small island state with no subnational regulatory tier and no independent ombudsman or court practice specifically addressing AI.
Actor Involvement: There are no structured domestic consultations involving industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy; internationally, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines participates in CARICOM regional technology-policy dialogue and has signed broad UN digital-cooperation declarations, providing minimal international engagement on AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanism exists; enforcement of technology-related harms relies entirely on general statutory powers that make no reference to AI.
Specific Laws: Electronic Transactions Act (2007), Telecommunications Act (2001)

Regulation Status: As of mid-2026, Samoa has no dedicated AI legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no AI-specific bill in the legislative pipeline. Digital transformation ambitions are captured in the draft National ICT Policy 2025–2030 and the Pathway for Development of Samoa 2025/26–2030/31, but these are non-binding policy documents without AI-specific legal provisions.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments exist; the only AI-relevant activity is a November 2025 UNDP-MCIT workshop on AI trust and safety and the draft ICT Policy 2025–2030 that mentions responsible data use and emerging digital risks, amounting to nascent soft-law framing without formal guidance or dedicated AI institutions.
Governance Type: AI matters fall entirely under the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology with no sectoral AI regulators, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanism, no subnational AI governance role, and no independent review body with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder processes for AI exist; the November 2025 MCIT-UNDP workshop brought in some regional and international partners, and Samoa participates in the Pacific Islands Forum digital policy dialogues and hosted the Pacific IGF 2025, providing minimal international-level engagement on AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in Samoa.
Specific Laws: National Communications & ICT Policy 2025–2030 (draft), National Cybersecurity Strategy 2025–2030, Pathway for Development of Samoa 2025/26–2030/31 (draft)

Regulation Status: San Marino has enacted no dedicated AI legislation and has no AI-specific binding instrument in force or formally in the legislative pipeline as of mid-2026. The country relies exclusively on proximity to EU data-protection norms (GDPR-aligned domestic law) and general legal principles, none of which explicitly address AI.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, published AI guidance, sandbox programs, or dedicated institutional bodies exist at the national level; AI oversight falls incidentally to the general data-protection authority (Garante) with no AI-specific mandate or resources.
Governance Type: San Marino is a very small unitary state with no AI-specific regulatory body; all relevant oversight defaults to a single general data-protection authority with no formal inter-agency AI coordination mechanism and no sub-national governance layer.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder consultations on AI policy have been conducted; San Marino's only relevant engagement is through Council of Europe multilateral frameworks (e.g., participation in the CAI Convention process) and general EU-adjacent dialogue, giving it a minimal international footprint on AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement actions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists; general GDPR-aligned data-protection enforcement applies only incidentally to AI-related processing.

Regulation Status: Sao Tome and Principe has no binding AI legislation, no draft AI law in any legislative process, and no sector-specific rules touching AI. The country lacks even general digital or data-protection law that would incidentally apply to AI systems.
Policy Lever: No policy mechanisms of any kind targeting AI exist; there are no guidelines, codes of practice, sandboxes, public funding programs, or designated institutional bodies with an AI mandate.
Governance Type: No AI governance structure of any kind exists at national or subnational level; there is a single unified government with no AI-specific regulatory authority, no coordination mechanism, and no independent review body with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: No domestic actors — industry, civil society, or academia — are involved in any structured AI policy process; the country holds nominal African Union membership, which provides passive exposure to continental AI policy discussions such as the AU Continental AI Strategy, but STP has not been an active or named participant in any international AI governance forum.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice related to AI exists in Sao Tome and Principe.

Regulation Status: Saudi Arabia has no dedicated AI-specific law in force as of June 2026. The PDPL (in force September 2023) covers automated data processing and profiling, and a new Copyright Law (published February 2026, entering force August 2026) includes AI training provisions, but SDAIA's core AI instruments—Ethics Principles (2023), Generative AI Guidelines (2024), AI Adoption Framework (2024)—remain non-binding guidance. A Draft Responsible AI Policy (public consultation closed May 2026) and a Draft AI Hub Law (2025) are not yet enacted, so binding AI-specific rules are essentially absent; binding_force scores 2 because a draft comprehensive AI policy just completed consultation. Scope and implementation reflect that binding coverage is limited to data protection with only partial AI relevance, while ai_specificity scores 2 because AI is explicitly addressed in soft-law instruments but not yet in enacted dedicated legislation.
