Policy Lever: Saudi Arabia relies primarily on SDAIA's well-developed suite of non-binding instruments (AI Ethics Principles 2023, GenAI Guidelines 2024, AI Adoption Framework 2024, Responsible AI Policy draft 2026) constituting a maturing soft-law toolkit (score 4), alongside the binding PDPL as the sole formal instrument with AI relevance (score 2 for binding instruments). SDAIA is a well-staffed, high-level authority with ISO 42001 certification, coordinating NCAI, NDMO, and sectoral regulators, with SDAIA accreditation increasingly tied to government contracts (institutional_capacity 4); economic tools include the SAMAI mass training programme and public-sector procurement leverage (score 3).
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralized under SDAIA (chaired by the Crown Prince), which sets strategy and policy; sectoral regulators (SAMA, SFDA, NCA, CST) apply AI provisions within their own domains but are subordinate in framing to SDAIA, giving limited plurality (score 2). Saudi Arabia is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance role. Formal cross-body coordination exists informally but lacks a published statutory mechanism (score 2). Independent checks are limited to PDPL violation committees, which issued 48 decisions in 2025, but no truly independent judicial or ombudsman-style AI review body exists (score 2).
Actor Involvement: Industry and the general public are consulted via the Istitlaa Platform (the Responsible AI Policy consultation in April–May 2026 was open to government, private, and non-profit entities), but there are no standing formal industry roles in policy-making (score 3); civil society participation is structurally limited given the domestic associational landscape (score 2); academia has some advisory input through NCAI but no formal standing advisory role (score 2). Internationally, Saudi Arabia is active at the UN, G20, and bilateral AI governance forums and hosted major international AI events, though it is not a treaty signatory on AI (score 4).
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is anchored in the PDPL framework (fines up to SAR 5 million, 48 decisions issued against violators in 2025), but there is no AI-specific sanctions framework yet; SDAIA is designated as the authority for both data and AI oversight but AI-specific enforcement resources and remit remain nascent, with no routine published AI enforcement actions or audit programme beyond data-protection compliance.
Specific Laws: National Strategy for Data and AI – NSDAI (2020), Personal Data Protection Law – PDPL, Royal Decree M/19 (2021, in force 2023), AI Ethics Principles – SDAIA (2023), Generative AI Guidelines – SDAIA (2024), AI Adoption Framework – SDAIA (2024), Copyright Law (2026, in force August 2026), Draft Responsible AI Policy – SDAIA (consultation closed May 2026), Draft Global AI Hub Law (2025)

Regulation Status: Senegal has no binding AI-specific legislation in force; its AI governance rests on the 2023 National Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (SNDIA) and the February 2025 New Technological Deal, both non-binding policy documents. The Data Protection Law No. 2008-12 and the Malabo Convention provide incidental data-governance coverage but no dedicated AI rules exist or are formally in legislative process.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to non-binding strategies (SNDIA 2023, New Technological Deal 2025) and soft guidance aligned with UNESCO AI Ethics; the SNDIA plans dedicated public funding for AI research and startups and proposes a Cluster Sénégal IA, but these remain largely aspirational as of mid-2026. The CDP oversees data-protection aspects applicable to AI systems but no dedicated AI institution with compliance infrastructure exists.
Governance Type: AI governance is centralized under the Ministry of Communication, Telecommunications and Digital Economy (MCTEN), with the CDP handling data-protection dimensions and ARTP overseeing telecoms infrastructure; no formal multi-regulator coordination mechanism for AI exists. Sub-national governments play no independent AI governance role, and judicial or independent review of AI decisions is occasional at best.
Actor Involvement: Industry and civil society were engaged in the SNDIA development process and in events like Afrik'IA (September 2024), but there are no standing formal roles in ongoing AI policy-making. Senegal participates in the African Union AI continental framework, Smart Africa Alliance, UNESCO RAM process, and aligns with the OECD AI Principles, giving it moderate international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework or enforcement actions exist; the CDP can impose administrative fines under data-protection law but no AI-dedicated authority with explicit enforcement remit or routine monitoring of AI systems has been established.
Specific Laws: National Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (SNDIA) (2023), New Technological Deal Horizon 2034 (2025), Data Protection Law No. 2008-12 (2008), African Union Convention on Cyber Security and Personal Data Protection (Malabo Convention) (ratified 2016)

Regulation Status: As of June 2026, Serbia has no enacted AI law; the AI Strategy 2025-2030 (Official Gazette RS, No. 5/2025) is the primary AI-specific instrument, and a working group formed in March 2025 has held only one meeting toward drafting a binding AI Law. AI-related activities remain governed by general laws (data protection, e-commerce, cybersecurity) with no binding AI-specific obligations in force.
Policy Lever: Serbia relies on its AI Strategy 2025-2030 and strategic action plans as the main policy tools, with no binding AI instruments yet. A Council for Artificial Intelligence provides coordination, a regulatory sandbox for AI testing is envisaged, and GPAI co-chairmanship elevates strategic capacity, but dedicated compliance infrastructure and mature guidance suites are absent.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Science, Technological Development and Innovation leads AI governance with a cross-ministerial Council for Artificial Intelligence providing ad hoc coordination; no independent AI regulator exists and subnational entities play no role. Judicial and ombudsman review of AI-related matters is occasional and not structured around AI specifically.
Actor Involvement: Industry (via IT sector bodies), civil society (NALED, Partners Serbia), and academia participate in the AI law drafting working group on an invited basis without standing formal roles. Serbia's GPAI co-chairmanship, the Belgrade Ministerial Declaration, and active EU candidate alignment represent a high level of international engagement.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists, no enforcement actions have been taken, no dedicated AI enforcement authority has been established, and no structured AI monitoring or audit regime is operational; the Data Protection Commissioner has issued no AI-specific guidance and lacks enforcement capacity even for general data protection cases.
Specific Laws: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia 2025-2030 (Official Gazette RS No. 5/2025), Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia 2020-2025 (Official Gazette RS No. 73/2019), Law on Personal Data Protection (2018)

Regulation Status: Seychelles has no dedicated AI legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no AI-specific regulatory instrument of any kind as of mid-2026. The country relies solely on general ICT and data protection frameworks that only incidentally touch AI.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies for AI policy exist in Seychelles; AI governance capacity remains entirely absent at the national level.
Governance Type: There is no multi-regulator or coordinated AI governance structure; responsibility is de facto absorbed by general ICT bodies with no formal AI coordination, no subnational AI role, and no independent review mechanism for AI issues.
Actor Involvement: No structured domestic stakeholder engagement in AI policy exists; Seychelles participates in AU and SADC regional forums and has endorsed broad African declarations on digital governance, providing minimal but non-zero international linkage.
Enforcement Level: With no AI-specific legal framework, there is no sanctions regime, no designated enforcement authority, no enforcement actions, and no monitoring or audit practice related to AI.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act (2003)

Regulation Status: Sierra Leone has no enacted AI-specific legislation or binding AI regulatory instruments as of June 2026. The Ministry of Communication, Technology and Innovation, in partnership with the World Bank, launched a national AI Readiness Assessment in October 2025 and has announced plans to develop a first-ever National AI Strategy, but neither instrument has been enacted. A Data Protection and Right to Access Information Bill (2025) is in validation but is not yet law and is not AI-specific.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments exist; the only AI-relevant output is a Draft AI Readiness Diagnostic Report produced in 2025-2026 under a $50M World Bank-funded Sierra Leone Digital Transformation Project, which constitutes nascent soft-law groundwork rather than operational guidance or standards.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated AI governance structure; the Ministry of Communication, Technology and Innovation acts as the sole focal point without a formal multi-body coordination mechanism, and no subnational or independent review body plays a role in AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia have no structured formal role in AI policy development; international engagement is limited to participation in the World Bank-supported AI readiness process and nominal membership in the African Union, which endorsed a Continental AI Strategy in 2024.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists; without any enacted AI rules there is nothing to enforce.
Specific Laws: Data Protection and Right to Access Information Bill (2025) [draft, not enacted]; Sierra Leone Digital Transformation Project AI Readiness Diagnostic Report (2026) [draft, non-binding]

Regulation Status: Singapore has no binding horizontal AI legislation as of June 2026; the Model AI Governance Framework (updated for Agentic AI in January 2026), AI Verify testing toolkit, and the May 2026 NAIS update remain voluntary. The MAS Consultation Paper on AI Risk Management Guidelines (November 2025) closed in January 2026 and has not yet been formally issued, so no binding AI-specific instrument is in force. The PDPA provides an indirect legal foundation, and the Online Safety Act covers some AI-generated harmful content. Singapore scores high on ai_specificity because it has published dedicated, AI-explicit instruments, but binding_force and scope remain low due to the voluntary, non-legislative nature of the core frameworks.
Policy Lever: Singapore relies almost entirely on a mature, well-maintained suite of voluntary soft-law instruments—Model AI Governance Frameworks (traditional, GenAI, Agentic), AI Verify toolkit, ISAGO 2.0, and sectoral MAS guidance—with no binding AI instruments yet in force; economic tools include the FSTI/AIDA Grant, AI Singapore research programme, and sandbox facilities, while IMDA, AI Verify Foundation, AISG, CAIDG, and MAS constitute a well-staffed multi-institutional governance infrastructure.
Governance Type: IMDA leads AI governance with AI Verify Foundation, MAS covers finance, PDPC handles data, and AISG drives research—a lead body plus several sectoral actors—while coordination occurs through the newly established National AI Council (NAIC) under NAIS 2026 update; Singapore is a city-state with no subnational layer, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI decisions is minimal and ad hoc.
Actor Involvement: Industry is formally embedded in AI Verify Foundation and participates in IMDA consultations and working groups; civil society is occasionally consulted but lacks standing formal roles; academia contributes through AISG and advisory channels; Singapore leads ASEAN AI governance, co-leads the WEF Agentic AI framework, participates in the International AI Safety Institute Network, OECD, GPAI, and bilateral partnerships globally.
Enforcement Level: No comprehensive AI-specific sanctions framework exists; PDPA penalties apply incidentally to AI-related data breaches but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly recorded; IMDA and PDPC are designated authorities but lack explicit AI enforcement mandates with dedicated resources, and monitoring is primarily through voluntary AI Verify audits rather than routine mandatory reporting.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy (2019), National AI Strategy 2.0 (2023), NAIS Update (2026), Model AI Governance Framework (2019, updated 2024 for GenAI, 2026 for Agentic AI), Personal Data Protection Act (2012, amended 2020), Online Safety Act (2022), MAS Consultation Paper on AI Risk Management Guidelines (2025)

Regulation Status: Slovakia, as an EU member state, is bound by the directly applicable EU AI Act (Reg. 2024/1689), giving it a fully binding horizontal AI framework. National implementation is partial: Act No. 318/2025 amended the conformity assessment statute to embed EU AI Act obligations with effect from 1 January 2026, while Draft Acts on State/Public Administration Organisation in the AI field were still undergoing interdepartmental procedures as of mid-2026 and a comprehensive national implementing statute had not yet been enacted. AI is explicitly addressed through the EU AI Act and national adaptations of existing sector laws, but no standalone Slovak AI Act exists.
Policy Lever: Binding instruments consist of the directly applicable EU AI Act supplemented by Act No. 318/2025 on conformity assessment; soft-law tools include the CERAI advisory body (est. 2020) and EU AI Act codes of practice, but a mature domestic guidance suite has not yet materialised. MIRRI oversees EDIHs and a sandbox framework is being developed, and the Government Plenipotentiary for AI (2025 decree) anchors coordination, though resources remain limited.
Governance Type: Governance involves MIRRI as the lead coordinator and expected general market-surveillance authority, the Office for Personal Data Protection for data-related AI, the National Security Authority for cybersecurity, and the Government Plenipotentiary for AI providing cross-cutting coordination, constituting a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure. Slovakia is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role; courts and independent bodies such as CERAI provide occasional review but have not yet actively shaped AI-specific rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry participates through consultations and programmes like InnovAIte Slovakia; academia contributes via CERAI and 22 university AI programmes; civil society is mentioned in CERAI's mandate but lacks a standing formal domestic role. Internationally, Slovakia participates in the EU AI Board, GPAI, OECD AI policy work, and Visegrad regional cooperation.
Enforcement Level: Act No. 318/2025 references EU AI Act penalty provisions and GDPR penalties apply to AI data processing, providing a partial sanctions framework, but no documented AI-specific enforcement actions have occurred; MIRRI is designated as the expected market surveillance authority without yet being fully resourced, and monitoring practice consists only of the UOOU's 2026 control plan referencing new technologies, with no routine dedicated AI auditing.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act – Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Act No. 18/2018 Coll. on Personal Data Protection (2018), Act No. 69/2018 Coll. on Cybersecurity (2018), Act No. 56/2018 Coll. on Conformity Assessment of Products as amended by Act No. 318/2025 Coll. (2025), Government Plenipotentiary for Artificial Intelligence Decree (2025), Action Plan for Digital Transformation of Slovakia 2023–2026 (2023), CERAI Standing Commission (2020)

Regulation Status: Slovenia has enacted the ZIUDHPUI (Act on the Implementation of the EU Regulation on Harmonised Rules on Artificial Intelligence), which became applicable and enforceable in November 2025, designating national authorities and establishing a market-surveillance framework directly linked to the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689). The EU AI Act itself is a directly applicable EU regulation with horizontal cross-sector scope covering the full AI lifecycle; Slovenia's national act operationalises it domestically, though enforcement activity is still nascent and secondary guidance is still developing.
Policy Lever: Slovenia relies on the EU AI Act as its primary binding instrument, supplemented by the ZIUDHPUI; soft law includes the Digital Slovenia 2030 strategy, the NpUI (2021–2025) national programme, and a draft National AI Programme 2030 out for public consultation in November 2025. AKOS has been designated as the lead authority and national single point of contact, with a regulatory sandbox framework planned, and an expert council advising the Ministry for Digital Transformation.
Governance Type: Governance is led by the Ministry for Digital Transformation, with AKOS as the primary market-surveillance authority, the Information Commissioner retaining jurisdiction over AI and personal data, and the Market Inspectorate also named; coordination is ad hoc inter-ministerial rather than through a formal standing mechanism. Slovenia is a centralised unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI regulatory role; courts and the Information Commissioner provide occasional independent review.
Actor Involvement: Slovenia engaged industry, academia, and civil society in public consultations on the NpUI and the draft National AI Programme 2030; the Ministry for Digital Transformation hosts working-group input from an expert council. As an EU member state Slovenia is fully embedded in the EU AI Board, GPAI, and relevant Council of Europe processes, placing it at the global frontier for international AI governance participation.
Enforcement Level: The ZIUDHPUI establishes penalties aligned with the EU AI Act's tiered fines framework, but as the law only became enforceable in November 2025 no enforcement actions have yet been recorded; AKOS is designated as the lead authority but is not yet fully resourced for AI-specific monitoring, and systematic public reporting on compliance is not yet in place.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Act on the Implementation of the EU Regulation on Harmonised Rules on Artificial Intelligence – ZIUDHPUI (2025), Digital Slovenia 2030 Strategy (2023), National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of AI in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 – NpUI (2021)

Regulation Status: Solomon Islands has no dedicated AI legislation, strategy, or binding regulatory framework as of June 2026. The country's policy attention remains focused on basic digital connectivity, telecommunications infrastructure, and general ICT development under legacy frameworks, with no AI-specific instruments drafted or proposed.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific policy instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist. The Ministry of Communication and Aviation oversees general ICT matters without any AI governance mandate or capacity.
Governance Type: AI governance responsibilities are entirely absent as a distinct function; general ICT oversight is centralized under a single ministry with no cross-agency coordination mechanisms, no sub-national role, and no independent or judicial review bodies addressing AI.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy domestically; Solomon Islands participates in the Pacific Islands Forum and related regional digital development dialogues, providing minimal international engagement on AI topics but without formal AI-specific commitments or treaty participation.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, designated authority, or monitoring mechanisms exist at any level of government.

Regulation Status: Somalia has no AI-specific legislation, binding rules, or even a drafted AI regulatory framework as of mid-2026. The country's ongoing fragility, institutional weakness, and conflict prevent the development or implementation of any AI governance instruments.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any kind exist for AI in Somalia — no guidelines, standards, sandboxes, funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies addressing artificial intelligence.
Governance Type: There is no AI governance structure whatsoever in Somalia; the absence of functioning regulatory institutions and persistent state fragility mean no authority, coordination mechanism, subnational role, or independent check exists in relation to AI.
Actor Involvement: No structured engagement by industry, civil society, academia, or international bodies on AI governance has been identified in Somalia; the country does not participate in international AI governance forums in any documented capacity.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, no designated authority, no sanctions, and no monitoring related to AI in Somalia.

Regulation Status: South Africa's Draft National AI Policy was gazetted on 10 April 2026 and subsequently withdrawn on 27 April 2026 after hallucinated citations were discovered; a revised draft is now targeted for cabinet approval by November 2026 and public comment in January 2027. No AI-specific binding legislation exists; POPIA (2013, fully effective 2021) provides indirect governance over automated decision-making and data processing but is not AI-specific legislation. The country therefore remains in a pre-binding-law state with a work-in-progress draft policy that does not yet create legal obligations.
Policy Lever: The only operative binding instrument is POPIA, which indirectly touches AI; there is no binding AI-specific instrument. The National AI Policy Framework and AI Government Summit Discussion Document constitute early soft-law guidance, but the formal draft policy has been withdrawn. The 4IR Commission provides advisory capacity and some sandbox/innovation-hub programs exist, but dedicated AI enforcement infrastructure remains nascent and thinly resourced.
Governance Type: Currently the Information Regulator (enforcing POPIA) is the de facto closest body to an AI regulator, with the Presidential 4IR Commission providing advisory input; the withdrawn draft proposed multiple new bodies (National AI Commission, AI Ethics Board, AI Regulatory Authority, AI Ombudsperson) but none exist yet. Coordination between the Department of Communications and Digital Technologies, 4IR Commission, and Information Regulator is ad hoc; sub-national provinces play no independent AI governance role.
Actor Involvement: The April 2026 draft policy opened a 60-day public comment window explicitly inviting inputs from industry, civil society, academia, and the public, though that process was cut short by the withdrawal; prior consultations were limited and episodic. South Africa participates in AU AI governance discussions, was among the original OECD AI Principles adopters (2019), and engages in G20 and BRICS AI fora, reflecting moderate international engagement.
Enforcement Level: POPIA provides a penalty framework that covers some AI-adjacent conduct (automated processing, profiling) and the Information Regulator has issued enforcement notices for data breaches, but there is no AI-specific sanctions framework, no dedicated AI enforcement authority, and no systematic AI-focused monitoring or audit regime as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) No. 4 of 2013 (fully effective 2021), National AI Policy Framework (2024), Draft South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy (gazetted April 2026, withdrawn April 2026)

Regulation Status: South Korea's AI Basic Act (Framework Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and the Establishment of a Foundation for Trustworthiness) entered into force on January 22, 2026—a comprehensive, binding, dedicated AI statute with an accompanying Enforcement Decree also effective January 22, 2026. It covers horizontal cross-sector obligations including high-impact AI (healthcare, biometrics, employment) and high-performance frontier AI (≥10²⁶ FLOPs), plus extraterritorial application. Full implementation is partial: primary rules and the Enforcement Decree are operative, but MSIT has granted a one-year grace period deferring administrative fines (up to KRW 30 million) except for serious harms, and secondary guidance is still being iteratively developed via a public-private task force.
Policy Lever: The AI Basic Act and its Enforcement Decree constitute one binding instrument, supported by PIPC's generative AI guidelines and MSIT policy briefings as soft law; economic tools include AI R&D programmes, AI infrastructure (data centres, clusters), semiconductor strategy, and a regulatory sandbox/support desk; KAISI and MSIT are designated institutions but secondary rules and dedicated compliance infrastructure are still being built during the grace period.
Governance Type: MSIT leads as primary enforcement authority with PIPC overseeing data-related AI issues and KCC regulating AI in communications/media—a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure; coordination mechanisms exist via the AI Basic Act's institutional framework but are still maturing; South Korea is a unitary state with no meaningful sub-national AI regulatory role; judicial and independent review channels exist but no active AI-specific enforcement actions have yet been published.
Actor Involvement: A public-private task force of 40+ experts from industry, academia, and civil society was convened within months of the Act taking effect, and an industry support desk operates for consultations; civil society participated in the enforcement decree consultation (with critical feedback) and MSIT ran a 40-day public comment period; South Korea co-hosted the Seoul AI Safety Summit, leads APEC's AI initiative, is active in OECD, GPAI, and the International Network of AI Safety Institutes—placing it at the global frontier of international AI governance participation.
Enforcement Level: Penalties up to KRW 30 million are defined in the Act but deferred during the one-year grace period except for serious harm cases; MSIT is designated as the primary enforcement authority and KAISI supports safety monitoring, but dedicated compliance infrastructure is still being established; no published routine enforcement actions have occurred yet, with monitoring limited to guidance and consultations during the transition period.
Specific Laws: Framework Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and the Establishment of a Foundation for Trustworthiness / AI Basic Act (2025, effective 2026), AI Basic Act Enforcement Decree (2026), Personal Information Protection Act as amended, National AI Strategy (2019, updated)

Regulation Status: Spain benefits from the directly applicable EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), which imposes binding obligations phased from February 2025 through August 2027, and AESIA has held full sanctioning powers since August 2025. A draft Organic Law for the proper use and governance of AI—advancing the national framework—was approved by the Council of Ministers on 26 May 2026 and sent to the Congress of Deputies, so a second dedicated AI-specific instrument is in active parliamentary processing but not yet enacted. Coverage is horizontal and cross-sector under the EU AI Act, with the national bill adding a domestic sanctioning regime and governance architecture; implementation is partially in force with secondary guidance issued but high-risk AI obligations only fully kicking in August 2026.
Policy Lever: The EU AI Act is the primary binding instrument, with a domestic Organic Law bill progressing through Parliament; AESIA published a 16-document compliance guidance pack in December 2025 covering risk-management templates and technical documentation. Economic tools include an active regulatory sandbox (12 projects selected April 2025), Cátedras ENIA university-industry AI chairs, and Digital Spain 2026 funding streams; AESIA is operational with approximately 80 staff, a management team, and a dedicated AI Advisory Council.
Governance Type: AESIA leads AI oversight as the national market-surveillance authority, complemented by AEPD (data protection) and CNMC (competition), with an interministerial working group and an AI Advisory Council providing structured coordination. Autonomous communities participate in implementation and linguistic/digital transformation working groups but do not independently regulate AI; courts provide ordinary judicial review but have not yet actively shaped AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry, academia, and civil society participated in the public consultation on the draft AI law (March 2025) and contributed to sandbox guidance development, with university-industry Cátedras ENIA providing academic-policy linkages and the AI Advisory Council offering broader multi-stakeholder input. Spain is exceptionally active internationally, participating in the EU AI Board, OECD, G20, UNESCO, Ibero-American cooperation frameworks, and hosting the Council of Europe AI Convention first anniversary conference in Madrid in September 2025.
Enforcement Level: AESIA holds explicit sanctioning powers under the EU AI Act since August 2025, with a comprehensive three-tier penalty framework in the draft national Organic Law (very serious, serious, minor), but no published AI Act enforcement actions by AESIA have been recorded to date; the AEPD remains active on data/biometric enforcement, and AESIA has begun monitoring through its regulatory sandbox and the December 2025 guidance cycle.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act – Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Royal Decree 729/2023 on AESIA Statute (2023), Proyecto de Ley Orgánica para el buen uso y la gobernanza de la Inteligencia Artificial (2026, in Parliament), Estrategia Nacional de Inteligencia Artificial – ENIA (2020, updated), Digital Spain 2026 Agenda (2021), Order ETD/1180/2022 on Cátedras ENIA (2022), Order TDF/304/2025 (2025), Order ETD/670/2020 (2020)

Regulation Status: Sri Lanka's AI governance remains anchored in the non-binding National AI Strategy 2024–2028 ('AI Sri Lanka 2028'), with no AI-specific legislation enacted or in the legislative pipeline as of mid-2026. The Personal Data Protection Act (No. 9 of 2022, amended by No. 22 of 2025) provides binding data-protection rules that incidentally touch AI but is not AI-specific and its substantive enforcement was still being phased in as of 2026. All AI commitments remain aspirational with no binding AI rules in force.
Policy Lever: The sole AI-related policy instrument is the voluntary National AI Strategy, supplemented by the PDPA which is general data law; a LKR 1.5 billion budget allocation was announced for 2024 AI initiatives, and the National AI Center and Data Protection Authority are being established but remain thinly resourced and not fully operational. No binding AI-specific regulatory instruments exist.
Governance Type: AI-related functions are dispersed across the Ministry of Digital Economy, the Presidential Secretariat, and the nascent Data Protection Authority, with no dedicated AI regulator and no formal coordination mechanism among these bodies. Sri Lanka is a unitary state with no subnational AI governance role, and judicial or independent review of AI practices remains occasional and incidental.
Actor Involvement: The 2023–2024 strategy formulation involved a multi-stakeholder CFSAI committee including private sector, academia, and civil society (with UNDP support and LIRNEasia participation), but these were one-off consultative processes without standing formal roles. Internationally, Sri Lanka endorsed the UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation (2021) and participated in the AI Impact Summit 2026, reflecting bilateral engagement without treaty-level or standards-body participation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions, penalties, monitoring, or enforcement mechanisms exist; the Data Protection Authority is being established but lacks AI-specific remit or operational enforcement capacity as of June 2026.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy 2024–2028 (AI Sri Lanka 2028), Personal Data Protection Act No. 9 of 2022, Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act No. 22 of 2025

Regulation Status: Sudan has no AI-specific legislation, binding regulation, or formal proposal under consideration as of June 2026. The country's ongoing civil war (erupted April 2023) and severe political fragmentation have entirely prevented the development of any AI governance framework; only general telecommunications and ICT laws exist and do not address AI.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, guidelines, codes of practice, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist. State capacity to develop or implement AI policy has effectively collapsed under conditions of active armed conflict.
Governance Type: There is no designated AI regulator or oversight body of any kind; fragmented and competing governmental structures resulting from the civil conflict mean no single or plural regulatory architecture for AI exists, and no subnational or independent-review mechanisms are operative.
Actor Involvement: Ongoing conflict has displaced institutions and suppressed civil society; there is no structured input from industry, academia, or civil society on AI governance, and Sudan's international engagement on AI governance forums is essentially non-existent.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, designated authority, sanctions, or monitoring practices related to AI exist in Sudan.

Regulation Status: Suriname has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules, and no dedicated regulatory framework as of mid-2026. The country relies exclusively on general ICT and older data-protection frameworks that predate AI considerations, with no AI instruments in any stage of the legislative process.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, published AI guidance, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies exist; Suriname has not implemented any policy mechanism specifically targeting AI governance or development.
Governance Type: There is no designated AI regulatory authority, no interagency coordination mechanism for AI, and no subnational or independent body with any AI oversight remit in Suriname.
Actor Involvement: Suriname's only recorded international AI engagement is its participation in the Santiago Declaration on AI in the Americas, placing it marginally above a score of 1 on international engagement; domestic industry, civil society, and academia have no structured role in AI policy discussions.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice for AI exists in Suriname.

Regulation Status: Eswatini has no AI-specific binding legislation as of June 2026; the Data Protection Act 2022 and the Eswatini Digitalisation Strategy 2024–2028 provide general digital/data governance but contain no dedicated AI provisions. Plans for a National AI Strategy were announced for the 2024/25 fiscal year but no published instrument has been confirmed.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated AI institutions exist; the Eswatini Communications Commission (ESCCOM) exercises data-protection powers under the 2022 Act but has no explicit AI remit or resources.
Governance Type: AI governance in Eswatini is entirely undefined; ESCCOM is the sole relevant regulatory body by virtue of its data-protection mandate, there is no multi-body coordination, no subnational role, and no independent or judicial AI oversight mechanism in place.
Actor Involvement: Eswatini hosted an inaugural AI Indaba in 2024 involving some academic and development-partner input, and as an African Union member has passive exposure to the AU Continental AI Strategy; structured domestic consultations with industry or civil society on AI policy have not been documented.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, actions, dedicated authority, or monitoring regime exists; ESCCOM's 2025 data-protection enforcement actions relate to data-controller registration under the 2022 Act and do not extend to AI-specific obligations.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act 5 of 2022, Eswatini Digitalisation Strategy (2024–2028)

Regulation Status: Sweden is directly bound by the EU AI Act (Regulation EU 2024/1689), a horizontal binding instrument now in force; the AI Office's prohibited-practices rules applied from February 2025 and GPAI rules from August 2025, with major high-risk provisions targeting August 2026. SOU 2025:101 (October 2025) proposes a complementary national law and ordinance designating PTS and 11 sectoral authorities, but this national implementing legislation was still in remiss (consultation) as of mid-June 2026 and had not yet been formally enacted, placing implementation in transition. The framework is AI-specific through the EU AI Act and the dedicated national inquiry process.
Policy Lever: Sweden relies on the EU AI Act as its primary binding instrument, supplemented by GDPR enforcement via IMY and sectoral overlaps (NIS2, DORA); soft law includes AI Commission roadmap (SOU 2025:12), procurement guidance and Vinnova-backed programmes totalling hundreds of millions of SEK. IMY operates a regulatory sandbox for data-intensive AI pilots; however, the dedicated national AI market surveillance authority (PTS) had not yet been formally resourced with an explicit AI enforcement mandate as of June 2026.
Governance Type: SOU 2025:101 proposes a highly plural structure: PTS as lead coordinator plus 11 sectoral market surveillance authorities and 2 notifying authorities, alongside IMY and Finansinspektionen with overlapping remits; inter-agency coordination is formalised in the proposal though still being legislated. Swedish courts and the Parliamentary Ombudsman provide independent checks, and IMY has exercised supervisory powers on AI use (Försäkringskassan case, November 2025). Sub-national entities implement but do not independently regulate AI.
Actor Involvement: The Swedish AI Commission (SOU 2025:12) included industry leaders (e.g. AI Sweden with 140 industry-academia-government partners) and researchers in a formal advisory capacity; the standard remiss process systematically consults civil society, academia and industry on both SOU 2025:12 and SOU 2025:101. Sweden is deeply embedded internationally: active in the EU AI Board, GPAI, OECD.AI, and Nordic AI cooperation initiatives.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act's penalty framework (up to €35m or 7% of turnover) is legally applicable but national implementing legislation to operationalise sanctions domestically was still in legislative process as of June 2026; IMY has conducted at least one AI-specific investigation (Försäkringskassan, closed November 2025) under GDPR, demonstrating active if isolated enforcement, while PTS has been designated as lead authority in proposed—but not yet enacted—legislation.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (2024), Swedish Data Protection Act (2018:218), SOU 2025:101 – Anpassningar till AI-förordningen (2025, proposed), SOU 2025:12 – AI Commission Roadmap for Sweden (2025), National Approach to Artificial Intelligence (2018)

Regulation Status: Switzerland has no AI-specific binding legislation in force; it signed (but has not ratified) the Council of Europe AI Convention in March 2025, and a consultation draft for implementing legislation is not expected until end-2026, with ratification/enactment likely after 2029. The FADP (2023) is the primary operative instrument and applies incidentally to AI-driven data processing, but no dedicated AI statute or binding AI rule is yet on the books. Coverage remains sector-specific through FINMA (finance) and Swissmedic (health) guidance rather than any enacted AI law.
Policy Lever: The sole operative binding instrument is the FADP (2023), applied indirectly to AI; the FDPIC publishes AI/data-protection guidance and FINMA issues supervisory expectations, constituting a modest suite of soft-law outputs, but no comprehensive standards framework. Economic tools are limited to isolated research-funding channels and no dedicated AI sandbox; institutional capacity sits with the FDPIC and sectoral regulators without dedicated AI units or compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: A lead data-protection authority (FDPIC) coexists with sectoral regulators (FINMA, Swissmedic, BAKOM) each handling AI in their own domain, but formal cross-agency AI coordination mechanisms are absent; inter-departmental work on the AI Convention draft involves FDJP, DETEC and FDFA ad hoc. Cantons implement national data-protection norms but do not regulate AI independently, and courts exercise ordinary judicial review under existing law without AI-specific mandates.
Actor Involvement: Industry, civil society, and academia participate through standard Swiss consultation (Vernehmlassung) processes and bodies like the Digital Switzerland advisory board, with no standing formal roles specific to AI. Switzerland punches above its weight internationally—signing the Council of Europe AI Convention, actively engaging in OECD AI policy, and hosting Geneva-based global AI governance centres (WEF, Geneva Centre for AI)—justifying a higher international score.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is channelled through existing sectoral authorities (FDPIC for data protection, FINMA for finance) using pre-AI-era penalty frameworks; no AI-specific sanctions have been defined or publicly recorded enforcement actions taken, and no dedicated AI monitoring programme or routine audit cycle exists.
Specific Laws: Federal Act on Data Protection / FADP (2023), Digital Switzerland Strategy (2025), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI – signed by Switzerland (2025)

Regulation Status: Syria has no AI governance framework of any kind. The ongoing civil conflict, territorial fragmentation, and near-total collapse of central state institutions prevent any binding AI legislation from being drafted, let alone enacted or enforced. No AI-specific or AI-adjacent legal instruments exist.
Policy Lever: No policy instruments of any kind—binding, soft, or economic—exist for AI governance in Syria. The state lacks functional institutions capable of developing or implementing any such mechanisms.
Governance Type: Syria has no AI governance structure whatsoever; there is no regulatory body, no coordination mechanism, and no independent review process. Territorial control is fragmented among multiple armed actors, none of which have established AI governance institutions.
Actor Involvement: Domestic actors—industry, civil society, and academia—are entirely absent from any AI governance process. Syria's only documented international AI governance engagement is its nominal accession to China's Global AI Governance Initiative in March 2024, which has produced no domestic implementation or capacity-building.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement framework, authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists for AI regulation in Syria.

Regulation Status: Taiwan's AI Basic Act entered into force on January 14, 2026—a dedicated, binding AI statute establishing seven core principles and imposing government-side obligations (risk assessments within 6 months, AI use rules within 12 months). However, it is a principles-based framework that imposes no direct private-sector obligations; secondary rules and the MODA risk classification framework (targeted for Q1 2026) were still pending as of mid-2026, while a National AI Strategy Special Committee was only announced in May 2026. Scope is cross-sectoral in architecture but lacks operational sector-specific coverage absent the forthcoming secondary instruments.
Policy Lever: The AI Basic Act is the sole binding instrument and imposes principally government-facing duties with no private-sector enforcement mechanism yet; soft-law guidance (codes of conduct, evaluation tools) is mandated but not yet published. Taiwan's economic tools are comparatively strong—NT$30B+ (~$950M) in the 2026 budget and a multi-year NT$100B+ AI investment program—while institutional capacity remains thin pending the establishment of the AI Strategy Special Committee and the MODA risk framework office.
Governance Type: Governance is led by NSTC as primary competent authority, with MODA handling risk classification and sectoral regulators (finance, health, transport) retaining their existing AI-adjacent remits, giving moderate regulator plurality; formal coordination is currently ad hoc—the cross-ministerial National AI Strategy Special Committee was announced in May 2026 but not yet formally constituted. Taiwan is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI governance is incidental rather than structural.
Actor Involvement: The AI Basic Act mandates industry representation in the AI Strategy Special Committee (Clause 6) and encourages government-industry-academia cooperation (Clause 8); public consultation was conducted during the 2024–2025 drafting phase but there is no standing formal civil-society role in ongoing governance. Taiwan participates in international AI governance frameworks and supported the Pax Silica Declaration (January 2026), but its diplomatic constraints limit full treaty participation, placing it closer to declaration-signatory status.
Enforcement Level: The AI Basic Act contains no penalties or enforcement provisions against the private sector; enforcement for AI-related harms relies entirely on pre-existing non-AI legislation, MODA has been designated to develop a risk framework but lacks a staffed AI-specific enforcement office, and no AI-specific audits or published enforcement actions have occurred.
Specific Laws: Artificial Intelligence Basic Act (2026), Taiwan AI Action Plan 2.0 (2023-2026)

Regulation Status: Tajikistan has adopted a Strategy for AI Development until 2040, which is a non-binding strategic document. No dedicated AI legislation or binding regulatory instruments are in force; all domestic AI-related activity remains at the strategy and declaration level. The sole AI-specific instrument is the national AI strategy, which does not impose legal obligations.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to the non-binding AI Development Strategy until 2040 and international advocacy positions; no binding instruments, funded programs, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional capacity for AI governance exist domestically.
Governance Type: AI governance falls under the general ICT ministry with no dedicated AI regulatory body, no formal inter-agency coordination mechanisms, no meaningful subnational role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman activity specifically addressing AI.
Actor Involvement: There is no evidence of structured domestic industry, civil society, or academic involvement in AI policy formulation; however, Tajikistan has demonstrated notable international engagement by sponsoring the July 2025 UN General Assembly resolution on equitable AI access and participating actively in international AI governance forums.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists; Tajikistan's AI governance is entirely at the strategy and declaration stage with no operative compliance or enforcement infrastructure.
Specific Laws: Strategy for AI Development until 2040

Regulation Status: Tanzania has no enacted AI-specific legislation, no dedicated AI strategy in force, and no binding AI rules as of mid-2026. The country's engagement with AI governance remains at the level of regional participation and informal workshops, with no legislative process for an AI bill underway.
Policy Lever: No binding instruments, published AI guidance, dedicated funding programs, or AI-specific institutional bodies exist; AI-related matters fall loosely under the general ICT policy mandate of the Ministry of Information, Communication and Information Technology.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated AI regulator or multi-body coordination structure; AI matters are informally absorbed by the ICT ministry as a single focal point with no formal subnational or independent oversight role.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry and civil society have no structured role in AI policy processes; some academic engagement occurs through regional African Union and Smart Africa Alliance workshops; Tanzania participates in AU-level AI governance declarations but has no bilateral AI agreements.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring mechanism of any kind exists in Tanzania.
Specific Laws: Electronic and Postal Communications Act (2010), Personal Data Protection Act (2022)

Regulation Status: Thailand's AI Act entered into force on 1 March 2026 under the ETDA framework, making binding AI rules formally operative; however, secondary rules on high-risk AI classification are still under public consultation as of June 2026, meaning full implementation with comprehensive secondary guidance is not yet complete. The law adopts a horizontal, risk-based approach (prohibited-risk and high-risk tiers) drawn from the EU AI Act model, covering AI across sectors, though sectoral guidance from financial, insurance and digital-asset regulators runs in parallel. Baker McKenzie (March 2026) still describes the Draft AI Principles as expecting enactment 'within two to three years', creating some uncertainty about whether the formal act-in-force claim from a secondary source reflects the full legislative picture, which warrants caution on the implementation score.
Policy Lever: Thailand now has one binding horizontal AI instrument (the AI Act, if in force March 2026) plus sector-specific guidelines from the Bank of Thailand, SEC, OIC and digital asset regulators; ETDA published Generative AI governance guidelines in late 2024 and operates an AI sandbox programme; an AI Governance Center (AIGC) under ETDA is mandated but appears to be in early operational build-out rather than fully staffed and resourced.
Governance Type: ETDA/MDES serves as the lead national AI authority while sectoral regulators (BOT, SEC, OIC, TCCT) retain their own AI guidance mandates, constituting a lead-body-plus-sectoral structure; coordination is ad hoc rather than through a permanent cross-agency council; Thailand is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulatory role; judicial and independent oversight is nascent with no established pattern of AI-specific court or ombudsman review.
Actor Involvement: Industry and law firms participated in the public hearing consultations of mid-2025 and ongoing secondary rulemaking, but these are episodic rather than standing formal roles; civil society and academia have limited formalised domestic input; Thailand is active internationally through ASEAN AI governance, UNESCO AI Forum engagement, bilateral AI partnerships, and alignment with OECD AI Principles, scoring higher on the international dimension.
Enforcement Level: The AI Act defines enforcement mechanisms including administrative orders to cease deployment, platform-blocking powers, and potential criminalisation of prohibited-risk AI, but the AI Governance Center is still being established and no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly reported as of June 2026.
Specific Laws: Draft Principles of the Artificial Intelligence Law (2025/2026), National AI Strategy (2022-2027), Draft Royal Decree on Business Operations Using AI Systems (2022), Draft Act on the Promotion and Support for National AI Innovations (2023), ETDA Guidelines for Application of Generative AI with Good Governance for Organizations (2024)

Regulation Status: Togo has no dedicated AI legislation, no AI-specific draft bills in the legislative pipeline, and no binding AI rules in force as of mid-2026. Governance relies entirely on general ICT and data protection frameworks such as the Loi n°2019-014 portant protection des données à caractère personnel, which do not address artificial intelligence explicitly.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, codes of practice, or dedicated funding programs exist; Togo has not established any institutional body with an AI mandate, leaving the policy landscape wholly undeveloped for AI.
Governance Type: AI oversight is entirely absent as a distinct function; the ARCEP (Autorité de Régulation des Communications Électroniques et des Postes) handles general telecoms and digital matters but has no AI remit, there is no coordination mechanism across agencies, no subnational AI role, and no independent or judicial body actively reviewing AI issues.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic engagement of industry, civil society, or academia on AI policy; Togo's only relevant international footprint is participation in African Union and ECOWAS digital economy frameworks, which include some AI-adjacent provisions but do not constitute active AI governance commitments.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Togo; the general data protection authority (ARMP/CNIL equivalent functions) has not taken any AI-related enforcement action.
Specific Laws: Loi n°2019-014 portant protection des données à caractère personnel (2019), Loi n°2012-018 portant Code des Postes et des Communications Électroniques (2012)

Regulation Status: Tonga has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no known AI regulatory proposals as of mid-2026. The country's policy priorities remain focused on basic digital connectivity, climate resilience, and sustainable development rather than AI governance.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, published AI guidance, dedicated AI funding programs, or institutional bodies with an AI remit exist in Tonga; digital policy capacity remains very limited and generalist.
Governance Type: Tonga has a highly centralized government with no AI-specific regulatory body or coordination mechanism; there is no subnational governance structure relevant to AI and no independent review body with an AI remit.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic involvement of industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy; Tonga's only relevant engagement is as a member of the Pacific Islands Forum, which has discussed digital and data governance at the regional level.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI enforcement framework, no designated authority, no sanctions, and no monitoring or auditing activity of any kind related to AI in Tonga.

Regulation Status: Trinidad and Tobago has no dedicated AI legislation in force, no AI-specific bill in the formal legislative pipeline, and no national AI strategy published as of June 2026. The Data Protection Act (2011) provides minimal incidental governance of data processing in AI systems but is not AI-specific and remains largely unenforced; the country's activity has been limited to readiness assessments and stakeholder consultations concluded in early 2026.
Policy Lever: No binding AI instruments, formal guidance documents, or AI-specific funding or sandbox programs exist; the creation of the Ministry of Public Administration and Artificial Intelligence (MPAAI) in May 2025 provides a nascent institutional home for AI policy, but it remains thinly resourced with no compliance infrastructure and its work has been confined to assessment and consultation activities.
Governance Type: The MPAAI is the sole governmental actor with an AI mandate, and the Office of the Information Commissioner nominally oversees the DPA but no formal coordination mechanism between these bodies on AI exists; Trinidad and Tobago is a unitary state with no subnational AI role, and while a judge issued an informal warning about AI-generated citations, there is no independent body or systematic judicial review shaping AI governance.
Actor Involvement: The national AI readiness assessment (Nov 2025–Feb 2026) involved sector-based consultations with industry, civil society, and UWI, but these were one-off diagnostic exercises rather than standing formal roles; internationally, Trinidad and Tobago has engaged through UNESCO and UNDP partnerships, CARICOM, endorsed the Santiago Declaration on AI ethics, and participated in the AI Impact Summit 2026 in India.
Enforcement Level: There is no AI-specific enforcement framework, no dedicated AI enforcement authority, and no monitoring or auditing of AI systems; the Data Protection Act 2011 is itself largely unenforced due to the absence of a fully operational and resourced Information Commissioner's office.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act (2011)

Regulation Status: Tunisia has no dedicated AI legislation; AI governance relies on the Organic Act No. 2004-63 on data protection (with a modernizing 2025 Organic Bill on Personal Data Protection advancing in parliament but not yet enacted), Decree-Law No. 2022-54 on cybercrime, and a National AI Strategy and Roadmap (2021-2025) succeeded by a 2026-2030 AI strategy. These instruments are not AI-specific but touch AI incidentally; the 2025 data protection bill explicitly addresses profiling and automated decision-making, moving toward AI-adapted general law, but remains in legislative process.
Policy Lever: Tunisia's primary policy tools are soft-law: the 2021-2025 National AI Strategy and its 2026-2030 successor, plus sectoral guidance from INPDP and ANSI; a binding data protection bill is in parliament but not yet in force, and economic tools are limited to innovation hubs like Novation City and some GIZ-supported programs, while institutional capacity remains thin with no dedicated AI regulator—only INPDP and ANSI covering adjacent domains.
Governance Type: AI-relevant oversight is fragmented between INPDP (data protection) and ANSI (cybersecurity), with a newly established National AI Council under the 2026-2030 strategy serving as an advisory coordinating body chaired by the Head of Government; sub-national governance plays no role, and independent judicial review of AI decisions is limited to general courts applying general law.
Actor Involvement: The Future Society and GIZ co-led multi-stakeholder consultations with ministries, academia and industry for the national AI strategy, and the Tunisian AI Society (TAIS) links government and academia, but civil society engagement has been inconsistent (notably gaps in the data protection bill process); internationally, Tunisia ratified Convention 108 (2017), participates in African Union AI readiness initiatives, and maintains bilateral AI-relevant partnerships with the EU and Gulf states.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement relies on INPDP's limited mandate under the 2004 data protection law and Decree-Law 2022-54 criminal provisions; the 2025 data protection bill proposes a strengthened INPDP with quasi-judicial powers and higher fines but is not yet in force, and there is no record of AI-specific enforcement actions or systematic monitoring.
Specific Laws: Organic Act No. 2004-63 on Personal Data Protection (2004), Decree-Law No. 2022-54 on Cybercrime (2022), National AI Strategy and Roadmap (2021-2025), Organic Bill on Protection of Personal Data (2025, in parliament), National AI Strategy (2026-2030)

Regulation Status: Turkey has submitted multiple draft AI bills to parliament since June 2024 (most recently three separate proposals, none enacted as of mid-2026), while the National AI Strategy 2021–2025 and Presidential Decree No. 119 (December 2025) restructuring the renamed National Technology and AI General Directorate represent significant but non-legislative steps. KVKK (Law No. 6698) and internet law No. 5651 touch AI incidentally; a January 2026 DPL amendment bill adding AI-related data-processing fines is also in parliament. No binding horizontal AI-specific law is yet in force, placing Turkey at the drafting/strategy stage.
Policy Lever: The sole binding instrument touching AI is KVKK data-protection enforcement (with fines updated for 2026); the draft AI Bill and DPL amendment remain unenacted. The National AI Strategy 2021–2025 provides soft-law direction but no maintained standards suite. The newly renamed National Technology and AI General Directorate (Presidential Decree No. 119, December 2025) is a designated institutional body under the Ministry of Industry and Technology, though its AI-specific compliance infrastructure is nascent.
Governance Type: AI governance is centralised under the Ministry of Industry and Technology via the National Technology and AI General Directorate, with BTK (telecoms/internet) and KVKK (data protection) holding partial sectoral oversight—constituting a lead body plus limited sectoral regulators rather than many independent remits. Formal coordination mechanisms between these bodies are ad hoc, there is no subnational AI regulatory role in Turkey's unitary system, and judicial or independent review of AI-specific matters is occasional rather than routine.
Actor Involvement: Industry, civil society, and academia have had limited structured input into Turkey's AI legislative process, with consultation largely informal or through general legislative channels rather than standing formal roles. Turkey is an OECD member participating in AI governance discussions, was a negotiating party observer to the Council of Europe AI Framework Convention process, and has bilateral technology cooperation agreements, qualifying it for active declaration-level international participation.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains primarily through KVKK's data-protection penalty framework (fines up to TRY 17 million, raised 25.49% for 2026), with no AI-specific sanctions framework enacted; the National Technology and AI General Directorate has a mandate but no demonstrated AI-specific enforcement actions or routine monitoring and public reporting regime yet operational.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2021-2025, Draft Artificial Intelligence Law (submitted June 2024, 8 articles, under parliamentary review), Presidential Decree No. 119 (25 December 2025) – establishing National Technology and AI General Directorate, Law No. 6698 on Protection of Personal Data (KVKK) (2016), Law No. 5651 on Regulation of Publications on the Internet, Draft Data Protection Law Amendment Bill (submitted January 2026)

Regulation Status: Turkmenistan has no identified AI-specific legislation, regulatory frameworks, or binding instruments as of June 2026. The country has not drafted, proposed, or enacted any AI-specific laws, and no general laws have been adapted to address AI. The existing governance environment remains focused on state control of information and communications without any AI-specific dimension.
Policy Lever: No binding instruments, soft-law guidance, economic tools such as sandboxes or AI funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies for AI governance have been established. Turkmenistan's policy agenda contains no observable AI-specific components.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralized under presidential authority with no plurality of independent regulators, no coordination mechanisms for AI, no meaningful subnational governance role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of technology policy. Civil society and independent bodies operate under severe restrictions that preclude any checks on government technology decisions.
Actor Involvement: No structured input from industry, civil society, or academia in any AI policy process has been documented. Turkmenistan is not a signatory to major AI governance declarations and participates only minimally in international digital or AI governance forums, reflecting its broader pattern of international isolation.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, sanctions, dedicated enforcement authority, or monitoring practice exists, as there are no AI-specific rules to enforce.

Regulation Status: Tuvalu has no AI-specific legislation, no binding AI instruments, and no AI policy framework of any kind. The country's governance priorities remain focused on existential climate threats, digital connectivity, and basic infrastructure; there is no evidence of any AI-related law, draft bill, or regulatory proposal as of mid-2026.
Policy Lever: No binding instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies related to AI exist in Tuvalu; public-sector capacity is consumed by climate resilience, the digital nation project, and basic service delivery.
Governance Type: Tuvalu has no AI governance structure whatsoever; there is no regulator, no coordination mechanism, no sub-national tier with any AI role, and no independent body reviewing AI-related matters.
Actor Involvement: There is no domestic AI policy process for industry, civil society, or academia to engage with; Tuvalu has some international engagement through the Pacific Islands Forum and UN bodies, including climate-digital linkage discussions, but has not joined AI-specific multilateral declarations or standards bodies.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice exists in Tuvalu.

Regulation Status: As confirmed by a UNESCO stakeholder consultation on 26 May 2026, Uganda lacks a comprehensive AI-specific legal framework; the Artificial Intelligence Bill 2025 remains a draft and has not been passed by parliament. The Data Protection and Privacy Act No. 9 of 2019 provides some data governance but does not address algorithmic transparency or automated decision-making. No binding AI instruments are in force.
Policy Lever: Uganda's policy toolkit is limited to non-binding ITU-supported guidelines, a National AI Framework under development by NITA-U through the UDAP-GovNet project, and an exploratory fintech innovation sandbox in partnership with the Bank of Uganda. No mandatory compliance frameworks exist and the institutional infrastructure remains embryonic.
Governance Type: AI governance is handled informally by the Ministry of ICT and NITA-U, with the Personal Data Protection Office (under NITA-U) providing adjacent oversight via the Data Protection and Privacy Act; there is no dedicated AI regulatory body or formal inter-agency coordination mechanism. Sub-national governments play no AI regulatory role, and judicial or independent review of AI matters has not been reported.
Actor Involvement: UNESCO's AI Readiness Assessment Methodology (RAM) consultations in February and May 2026 brought together government, academia, civil society, the private sector, and development partners in structured but non-standing dialogues. Uganda is actively engaging with international organizations including UNESCO, ITU, the African Union, and UNDP, making international engagement the strongest axis, while domestic industry, civil society, and academic involvement remains consultative and ad hoc.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework, enforcement authority, or monitoring mechanism exists; the Personal Data Protection Office can enforce data protection obligations under the 2019 Act but has no explicit AI remit, and no AI-related enforcement actions have been reported.
Specific Laws: Data Protection and Privacy Act No. 9 of 2019, Artificial Intelligence Bill 2025 (draft, not enacted), Copyright and Neighbouring Rights (Amendment) Bill 2025 (passed March 2026, not AI-specific)

Regulation Status: Ukraine has no enacted binding AI-specific legislation as of June 2026: the standalone AI law is planned for submission to the Verkhovna Rada only in Q4 2026, and the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (signed May 2025) has not yet been ratified. Governance rests on the non-binding White Paper on AI Regulation (June 2024), the Roadmap for AI Regulation (2023), and a Cabinet of Ministers policy order on AI for priority sectors (April 2024). AI is explicitly and deliberately addressed in these dedicated instruments, warranting a higher ai_specificity score, but without binding force or broad sectoral coverage.
Policy Lever: All current instruments are non-binding: the White Paper, voluntary Code of Conduct on Ethical and Responsible AI Use (December 2024), sectoral guidelines for legal professionals and educators, and an AI labeling scheme. A regulatory sandbox operated by the Ministry of Digital Transformation and the Innovation Development Fund provides one concrete economic/testing tool, but broader funding programs are limited. The Ministry's Expert Advisory Committee provides some institutional capacity, though it remains thinly resourced relative to a full compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: The Ministry of Digital Transformation is the dominant single authority for AI policy, with the Parliamentary Ombudsman playing a supplementary human-rights oversight role and sectoral bodies having limited involvement; as a unitary state, Ukraine has no subnational AI regulatory role. Coordination across bodies is ad hoc rather than through a formal mechanism, and the Ombudsman provides occasional independent review but has not shaped binding AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry was consulted through the bottom-up roadmap process and the voluntary Code of Conduct attracted company signatories, while civil society and academia contribute through the Expert Advisory Committee and the EU4Digital workshops but lack standing formal roles. Internationally, Ukraine signed the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (May 2025), endorses OECD AI Principles, participates in EU alignment workshops, and adopted the GPAI voluntary code, indicating substantial international engagement.
Enforcement Level: Ukraine is in its voluntary Stage One phase with no AI-specific sanctions framework, no enforcement actions taken, no authority with a dedicated AI enforcement remit, and no formal auditing or monitoring program; compliance relies entirely on reputational and voluntary incentives.
Specific Laws: Concept for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in Ukraine (2020), Roadmap for AI Regulation in Ukraine (2023), White Paper on Artificial Intelligence Regulation (2024), Policy on Development of Artificial Intelligence in Priority Spheres of Economy until 2026 - Cabinet of Ministers Order (2024), Voluntary Code of Conduct on Ethical and Responsible Use of AI (2024), Law of Ukraine On Personal Data Protection No. 2297-VI (2010), Law of Ukraine On the Basic Principles of Ensuring Cybersecurity of Ukraine No. 2163-VIII (2017), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI Human Rights Democracy and Rule of Law STCE 225 - signed 2025 not yet ratified

Regulation Status: The UAE has no standalone federal AI law but has enacted several binding instruments touching AI: the PDPL (in effect January 2026, compliance by January 2027), the National Elections Committee Policy on AI Use (October 2025), and the National AI System Cabinet integration decree (January 2026); the DIFC Regulation 10 on autonomous/semi-autonomous systems has been enforceable since early 2026. Free-zone frameworks (DIFC, ADGM) are binding within their jurisdictions but do not extend federally, leaving horizontal federal AI-specific legislation absent. Binding coverage remains sectoral and piecemeal rather than comprehensive.
Policy Lever: The policy toolkit combines soft law (UAE AI Charter 2024 with 12 principles, National AI Strategy 2031, sector-specific guidelines) with binding instruments only in free zones and narrow domains; economic tools include large-scale infrastructure investment (Stargate UAE, US-UAE $1.4 trillion tech framework) and sandbox/licensing regimes in DIFC. The UAE Council for AI and Blockchain, TDRA, and Abu Dhabi's AIATC provide institutional capacity, though dedicated AI enforcement infrastructure remains thin outside free zones.
Governance Type: Governance is split between federal bodies (Minister of State for AI, TDRA, National AI System in Cabinet) and quasi-independent free-zone jurisdictions (DIFC, ADGM) that legislate and enforce AI rules autonomously within their boundaries, giving the UAE a distinctive multi-level structure. Coordination across these layers is partially formalised through the UAE Council for AI and Blockchain and Cabinet-level integration, but judicial and independent non-governmental checks on AI rules remain limited.
Actor Involvement: Industry input occurs through DIFC public consultations (e.g., the Regulation 10 certification survey) and working groups, but domestic civil society and independent NGOs play no formal role in AI policy-making. The UAE is highly active internationally—hosting GITEX AI summits, co-sponsoring UN AI resolutions, participating in Global Partnership on AI, and anchoring major bilateral AI cooperation frameworks (US-UAE Stargate, $1.4 trillion tech partnership).
Enforcement Level: Enforcement infrastructure is nascent federally: PDPL penalties (up to AED 20 million) are defined but the compliance deadline is January 2027; DIFC Regulation 10 carries fines of USD 25,000–50,000 per violation with enforcement commencing in early 2026, but no publicly reported AI-specific enforcement actions have occurred; outside free zones there is no dedicated AI enforcement authority and monitoring is ad hoc.
Specific Laws: UAE National AI Strategy 2031 (2017), Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021 on Personal Data Protection (PDPL, effective 2026), UAE AI Charter (2024), Abu Dhabi Law No. 3 of 2024 establishing AIATC, DIFC Data Protection Regulations Regulation 10 (2023, enforceable 2026), National Elections Committee Policy on Use of AI (2025), National AI System Cabinet Integration Decree (2026)

Regulation Status: The UK has no enacted horizontal AI-specific legislation as of June 2026; the Artificial Intelligence (Regulation) Bill [HL] (a private member's bill) was at final stages in Parliament as of April 2026 but is not government-sponsored law. AI is addressed through the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (Royal Assent June 2025, core provisions in force February 2026) and sector-specific rules from ICO, FCA, Ofcom, CMA, and MHRA, applying five cross-sector principles via the DSIT pro-innovation framework. The Regulating for Growth Bill (King's Speech, May 2026) would put regulatory sandboxes onto a statutory footing but adds no binding AI-specific rules.
Policy Lever: The UK relies on soft-law instruments—five cross-sector AI principles, sector-regulator AI strategic frameworks (FCA Mills Review, PRA SS1/23, ICO ADM guidance), and the AI Security Institute's evaluation work—supported by economic programs such as the AI Growth Lab and AI Opportunities Action Plan (Jan 2025), with no binding horizontal AI statute; binding instruments are limited to sector-specific rules under existing legislation such as the DUAA 2025.
Governance Type: Seven sector regulators (ICO, FCA, PRA, CMA, Ofcom, MHRA, and others) each apply the five DSIT principles independently within their domains, with the Digital Regulation Co-operation Forum (DRCF) providing ad hoc cross-regulator coordination; there is no single AI authority, no subnational AI regulation, and judicial/independent review operates through existing administrative law channels.
Actor Involvement: Industry has structured input through consultations, working groups, and the AI Opportunities Action Plan process; civil society and academia are consulted occasionally (e.g., AISI evaluations, BCS engagement with AI Action Plan) but hold no standing formal roles; internationally, the UK hosted the Bletchley AI Safety Summit (2023) and participates in OECD and G7 AI governance forums but declined to sign the Paris AI Action Summit declaration (February 2025), signalling a more selective international alignment.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement relies on existing sector-regulator powers (e.g., ICO fines under UK GDPR, FCA supervisory actions) rather than a dedicated AI enforcement authority; the AI Security Institute conducts model evaluations and risk monitoring but has no enforcement remit, and published regulatory actions touching AI are occasional rather than routine.
Specific Laws: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, AI Opportunities Action Plan (2025), Regulating for Growth Bill (2026, introduced), Artificial Intelligence (Regulation) Bill [HL] (2025-2026, private member's bill in final stages), UK GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018, Online Safety Act 2023, AI Safety Institute Framework (2024)

Regulation Status: The US has no comprehensive federal AI statute; the TAKE IT DOWN Act (May 2025) is the only federal AI-specific law, targeting non-consensual deepfakes. Several AI-specific state statutes are now in force or entering enforcement—California SB 53 Transparency Act (Jan 2026), Texas TRAIGA (Jan 2026), Colorado SB 205 AI Act (enforcement delayed to June 2026)—while Trump EO 14365 (Dec 2025) and a March 2026 White House National Policy Framework seek to preempt state rules, creating binding-force uncertainty at the federal level. Binding instruments exist but are narrow, fragmented, or contested, placing the US between 'drafted' and 'in force' at the federal level while multiple AI-specific state laws are operational.
Policy Lever: Multiple binding instruments exist across domains—FTC enforcement (Operation AI Comply, ongoing through 2025-2026), state AGs enforcing state AI statutes, and export controls on AI chips—but no comprehensive federal binding framework spans all sectors. Soft law is well-developed via NIST AI RMF, CAISI (formerly AISI, re-established June 2025) guidance, SEC AI disclosures, and sector-specific agency guidelines, though the institutional infrastructure lost momentum with Biden-era AISI's reorganization.
Governance Type: AI governance is extremely fragmented: FTC, SEC, NIST/CAISI, DOJ AI Litigation Task Force, and 45+ state legislatures and AGs all operate independently with overlapping remits and no single coordinating body. States regulate AI fully independently—1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states in 2026, with California, Texas, and Colorado enacting their own comprehensive regimes—while courts (e.g., xAI v. Colorado AG, Apr 2026) and independent bodies actively contest and shape AI rules.
Actor Involvement: Industry has substantial structured input through NIST RMF working groups, White House AI Action Plan consultations, and formal lobbying shaping EO 14365 and preemption debates; civil society and academia participate in public comment processes and CAISI advisory activities but lack standing formal roles. The US is an active leader in international AI governance via G7, OECD, the International Network of AI Safety Institutes, and bilateral AI partnerships, though it declined the Paris AI Action Summit declaration in February 2025.
