Enforcement Level: The FTC's Operation AI Comply has produced over a dozen enforcement actions by 2025-2026 and first federal criminal prosecutions occurred under the TAKE IT DOWN Act (May 2026), demonstrating real if still patchy enforcement; state AGs enforce state AI statutes with defined penalties, but no single resourced federal authority has an explicit comprehensive AI enforcement mandate and routine auditing remains limited.
Specific Laws: TAKE IT DOWN Act (2025), Executive Order 14365 - Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence (2025), California SB 53 AI Transparency Act (2026), Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act - TRAIGA (2026), Colorado SB 205 AI Act (2024/2026), NIST AI Risk Management Framework (2023), White House National AI Action Plan (2026)

Regulation Status: Uruguay has signed the Council of Europe AI Convention (September 2025), making it the first Latin American country to do so, but ratification and transposition into domestic law remain pending as of mid-2026. Agesic's July 2024 AI regulatory recommendations and the country's data protection law (Law 18.331) provide a foundation, but no dedicated binding AI-specific legislation has entered into force. Uruguay is actively developing an AI strategy and aligning with international frameworks, yet binding domestic AI rules do not yet exist.
Policy Lever: Agesic has published AI recommendations for the public sector and operates as the designated coordinating body, but binding AI instruments are absent. Soft-law guidance exists in the form of Agesic's 2024 recommendations and data protection frameworks, while economic tools remain limited to exploratory programs; institutional capacity is present but thinly resourced relative to frontier jurisdictions.
Governance Type: Agesic serves as the primary lead body coordinating AI and data governance across government, with internal data committees being established within public entities, but sectoral regulators have not developed distinct AI remits. Uruguay is a unitary state with no meaningful subnational AI governance role, and independent review of AI decisions is limited to general data protection complaint mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Uruguay's most prominent actor involvement is at the international level, as the first Latin American signatory of the CoE AI Convention and an active participant in OECD processes and Santiago/Cartagena Declarations; domestic consultation with industry, civil society, and academia has occurred during strategy development but without formal standing roles or institutionalized mechanisms.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement of AI-specific obligations is not yet operational; Agesic has a designated coordination role and data protection enforcement exists under Law 18.331, but no dedicated AI enforcement authority, sanctions framework, or monitoring practice specific to AI has been established.
Specific Laws: Law 18.331 - Data Protection Law (2008), Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI (signed September 2025), Agesic AI Regulatory Recommendations (2024)

Regulation Status: Uzbekistan's Law No. ZRU-1115 ('On Amendments and Supplements to Certain Laws in Connection with Artificial Intelligence Regulation') was signed by President Mirziyoyev on 21 January 2026, making it binding law; it amends the Law on Informatisation and other acts to define AI, establish principles, prohibit fully automated legally-significant decisions, mandate AI-content labeling, and introduce administrative penalties for unlawful AI-based data processing. Ethical guidelines (registered March 14, 2026, entering force June 17, 2026) complement the law, alongside the AI Development Strategy until 2030. Implementation is underway but secondary regulations and full enforcement infrastructure are still maturing — the ethics rules only enter force in mid-June 2026.
Policy Lever: One binding horizontal instrument (ZRU-1115) amends multiple existing laws; soft law includes registered ethical guidelines (No. 3787, 2026) and the 2030 Strategy. The Ministry of Digital Technologies and Uzkomnazorat are designated bodies, supplemented by the Center for AI and Digital Economy, but resources and compliance infrastructure remain limited for a lower-middle-income country; limited evidence of active innovation sandbox or substantial public AI funding programs beyond the $1.5B market target.
Governance Type: Governance is centralized: the Ministry of Digital Technologies leads policy, while Uzkomnazorat handles technical oversight and enforcement; coordination is ad hoc rather than through formal multi-body mechanisms. Uzbekistan is a unitary state with no sub-national AI regulatory role, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI decisions is nascent.
Actor Involvement: The draft AI law passed through parliamentary readings with ministerial consultations, and industry and civil society had some input (second and third readings included stakeholder revisions), but there are no formal standing roles; academia provides some advisory input via the AI Center. Internationally, Uzbekistan submitted its AI Strategy to the UN General Assembly, participates in CIS cooperation, and hosted ICT Week 2025 showcasing bilateral partnerships, placing it at signatory/declaration level.
Enforcement Level: ZRU-1115 introduces administrative penalties for unlawful AI-based personal data processing and prohibits fully automated legally-significant decisions, constituting a partial sanctions framework; Uzkomnazorat conducted 55 control measures in Q1 2026 (mostly telecoms/informatisation compliance), but no AI-specific enforcement actions have been publicly reported yet, and routine AI-specific auditing is not yet established.
Specific Laws: Strategy for AI Development until 2030 (2021), Law on Amendments and Supplements to Certain Laws in Connection with Artificial Intelligence Regulation No. ZRU-1115 (2026), Ethical Rules for AI-Based Solutions No. 3787 (2026), Law on Informatisation (as amended)

Regulation Status: Vanuatu has no dedicated AI law or strategy as of mid-2026; the Data Protection and Privacy Act No. 13 of 2024 (effective February 1, 2025) is the only binding instrument touching AI, via a provision on the right not to be subject to automated decision-making. The Digital Transformation Act was passed unanimously by parliament in November 2025 but is a general digital governance law with no explicit AI provisions, and its gazette/commencement status remains unconfirmed.
Policy Lever: Policy tools are limited to general digital instruments: the DPPA provides some binding data protection rules incidentally covering automated decisions, while the ICT Policy 2025 and National Cyber Security Strategy 2030 mention AI/ML in passing; no AI-specific sandboxes, funding programs, or dedicated enforcement infrastructure exist, and the Digital Safety Authority, though legislatively established, is not yet operational.
Governance Type: Governance is centralized through the Department of Communications and Digital Transformation (DCDT) and the Telecommunications and Radiocommunications Regulator (TRR), with the Digital Safety Authority established on paper but not yet operational; Vanuatu is a unitary state with no subnational AI role, and there is no independent judicial or ombudsman body actively shaping digital or AI rules.
Actor Involvement: The private sector has had some input through ICT policy consultations, but civil society and academia have no formal roles in AI governance; Vanuatu's international engagement on AI is limited to participation in Pacific Islands Forum digital discussions and UNESCO membership, without active AI treaty or standards commitments.
Enforcement Level: The DPPA defines some penalty provisions for data protection violations that incidentally cover automated decision-making misuse, but no AI enforcement actions have been taken, the Digital Safety Authority remains non-operational, and no AI-specific auditing or monitoring mechanisms are in place.
Specific Laws: Data Protection and Privacy Act No. 13 of 2024, Digital Safety Authority Act No. 15 of 2024, ICT Policy 2025, National Cyber Security Strategy 2030

Regulation Status: Vatican City State enacted binding AI guidelines via Pontifical Commission Decree No. DCCII (in force 1 January 2025), establishing enforceable prohibitions and principles covering Vatican City and Lateran Treaty territories; the May 2026 encyclical 'Magnifica Humanitas' adds doctrinal weight but is not civil law. The scope is limited to Vatican institutional operations rather than a general horizontal framework, and follow-up implementing laws promised within one year of the decree have not been fully confirmed as enacted, leaving implementation partially in transition. The instruments are AI-specific but narrow in territorial reach.
Policy Lever: The primary binding instrument is Decree No. DCCII covering Vatican operations, with a special internal AI experimentation commission established; soft law is robust, including the Rome Call for AI Ethics (2020), the doctrinal document 'Antiqua et Nova' (2025), and the encyclical 'Magnifica Humanitas' (2026), but there are no economic tools, sandboxes, or funding programs relevant to a microstate of this nature. An Interdicasterial Commission on AI was approved in May 2026 but remains newly formed and thinly resourced.
Governance Type: Governance is highly centralized: the Pontifical Commission for Vatican City State is the sole regulatory authority, with a newly created Interdicasterial Commission on AI (May 2026) coordinating across seven Vatican dicasteries including the Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development and the Pontifical Academy for Life. There is no subnational layer and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI use within the microstate.
Actor Involvement: Industry engagement is through the Rome Call for AI Ethics (Microsoft, IBM, Cisco, Qualcomm as signatories) rather than formal domestic policy consultations; domestic civil society has no structured role in the Vatican's closed governance system. The Pontifical Academy for Life and Pontifical Academy of Sciences provide formal academic input, and internationally the Vatican is a significant moral actor—signatory to the Rome Call, engaged at the G7, and partnered with UNESCO—though it does not participate in treaty-based standards bodies.
Enforcement Level: Decree No. DCCII sets out prohibitions but does not define a comprehensive penalty framework; an internal AI experimentation oversight commission was established but has no published enforcement actions, and monitoring is nascent with no public reporting regime in place as of mid-2026.
Specific Laws: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence – Decree No. DCCII of the Pontifical Commission for Vatican City State (2025), Rome Call for AI Ethics (2020), Antiqua et Nova – Doctrinal Note on Artificial Intelligence (2025), Magnifica Humanitas – Encyclical of Pope Leo XIV (2026), Interdicasterial Commission on Artificial Intelligence – Rescript of Cardinal Czerny (2026)

Regulation Status: Venezuela has no dedicated AI legislation, no binding AI rules in force, and no AI-specific regulatory framework of any kind. The severe ongoing economic and political crisis leaves the country with no capacity or apparent political will to develop AI governance instruments.
Policy Lever: No binding instruments, guidance documents, sandboxes, funding programs, or dedicated institutional bodies for AI policy exist in Venezuela; the country lacks the institutional infrastructure to implement any such mechanisms.
Governance Type: There is no AI-specific regulatory authority, no coordination mechanism across bodies, no subnational AI governance role, and no independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI matters in Venezuela.
Actor Involvement: Domestic industry, civil society, and academia have negligible structured involvement in AI policy; Venezuela's only traceable international AI engagement is as a signatory to the CELAC/Santiago Declaration (2023), reflecting minimal but non-zero international participation.
Enforcement Level: No AI enforcement framework, penalties, dedicated authority, or monitoring practice of any kind exists in Venezuela.
Specific Laws: Santiago Declaration on AI (2023)

Regulation Status: Vietnam enacted Law No. 134/2025/QH15 on Artificial Intelligence (effective March 1, 2026), a dedicated horizontal AI statute with risk-based classification covering all sectors, followed by implementing Decree 142/2026/ND-CP (effective May 1, 2026). However, the Prime Minister's official high-risk AI list remains pending, compliance grace periods extend to March–September 2027, and the National AI Portal and Database are still being stood up, placing implementation at partial/transitional stage rather than fully operational.
Policy Lever: Vietnam has two binding instruments (Law 134/2025 and Decree 142/2026) but the administrative-sanctions decree is still forthcoming; a National AI Ethics Framework is mandated but not yet issued, and MoST's AI portal and regulatory sandbox mechanism are established in law but only beginning to operate, with the Ministry of Science and Technology designated as the lead institution though still building its compliance infrastructure.
Governance Type: AI governance is highly centralized under the Ministry of Science and Technology as the single lead authority, with sectoral ministries playing supporting roles and no formal multi-regulator coordination body; Vietnam is a unitary state with no subnational AI regulation, and independent review of AI decisions relies on general administrative and judicial channels rather than dedicated AI oversight mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Industry participated in consultations during the law's drafting, but civil society and academia have limited formal standing roles given Vietnam's political structure; internationally, Vietnam is an active ASEAN participant—including in the WG-AI and the 2026 Hanoi Digital Declaration—and maintains bilateral AI partnerships with Japan, South Korea, and China.
Enforcement Level: Article 29 of the AI Law establishes a general liability and enforcement foundation but defers specific administrative fine schedules to a forthcoming government decree; MoST is designated as the lead enforcement authority with a National AI Portal for incident reporting and classification, but no AI-specific enforcement actions have yet been taken given the law has only been in force since March 2026.
Specific Laws: Law on Artificial Intelligence No. 134/2025/QH15 (2025), Decree No. 142/2026/ND-CP (2026), Law on Digital Technology Industry (2025), National AI Strategy Decision No. 127/QD-TTg (2021)

Regulation Status: Yemen has no AI-specific legislation, strategy, or binding instruments of any kind. The country remains in a state of protracted armed conflict and severe humanitarian crisis, with state institutions too fragmented and under-resourced to develop or implement any AI governance framework.
Policy Lever: No policy levers of any type exist for AI governance in Yemen; there are no binding instruments, guidance documents, funding programs, sandboxes, or dedicated institutional bodies capable of addressing AI regulation.
Governance Type: Yemen has no functioning national governance structure for AI; the ongoing conflict has produced fragmented political authority with no single body, coordination mechanism, or independent review process relevant to AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: No structured engagement by industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy exists domestically, and Yemen has not participated in any international AI governance declarations or standards-setting processes.
Enforcement Level: There is no enforcement framework, no dedicated authority, no sanctions, and no monitoring or auditing activity related to AI in Yemen.

Regulation Status: Zambia has no dedicated AI legislation in force or formally drafted as of mid-2026; AI-related governance relies entirely on general ICT and cybersecurity instruments. The Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act (2021) and the National ICT Policy do not specifically address AI, making all AI coverage purely incidental.
Policy Lever: No AI-specific binding instruments, guidance documents, codes of practice, sandboxes, or dedicated funding programs exist; ZICTA administers general ICT regulation without any AI-specific capacity or mandate.
Governance Type: ZICTA is the single dominant ICT regulator and functions as the de facto digital authority, with no formal multi-body coordination mechanism for AI and no meaningful subnational or independent review structures applicable to AI governance.
Actor Involvement: There is no structured domestic process involving industry, civil society, or academia in AI policy; Zambia participates peripherally in international forums through its membership in the Smart Africa Alliance and AU Digital Transformation Strategy alignment, but has not signed AI-specific international declarations.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement framework, penalties, designated authority, or monitoring practice exists; any incidental enforcement would fall under general cybersecurity or ICT statutes without AI-specific application.
Specific Laws: Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act (2021), Information and Communications Technologies Act (2009), National ICT Policy (2006, revised 2020)

Regulation Status: Zimbabwe approved a National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2026–2030 in October 2025 and formally launched it in early 2026, but this is a non-binding strategic framework with no dedicated AI legislation enacted. The Cyber and Data Protection Act (2021) and its 2024 licensing regulations are binding but address data protection generally, not AI-specifically; no horizontal AI law has been drafted or introduced into the legislature.
Policy Lever: Zimbabwe's main policy instrument is the non-binding National AI Strategy 2026–2030, supported by the Cyber and Data Protection Act (2021) for data governance; the strategy includes a planned regulatory sandbox ('Innovation Crucible') and a proposed National AI Implementation Office, though neither is yet operationally resourced. Soft-law guidance is provided through the strategy document and UNESCO-aligned readiness assessments, but a mature enforcement-backed standards suite does not yet exist.
Governance Type: AI governance is led by the Ministry of ICT with POTRAZ as the data protection regulator, with plans for a National AI Council and sector-specific Technical Working Groups under the 2026–2030 strategy, but these bodies are nascent and not yet formally operational. Coordination is largely ad hoc, no subnational AI regulatory role exists, and independent judicial or ombudsman review of AI-specific matters is absent.
Actor Involvement: The AI Strategy was developed through multi-stakeholder consultations in Harare, Bulawayo, and Masvingo (August–September 2025) involving industry, academia, civil society, and diaspora representatives, though these were one-off consultative events rather than standing formal roles. Zimbabwe participates in AU and SADC AI governance initiatives and aligned its strategy with UNESCO's AI Readiness Assessment Methodology, giving it modest international governance participation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific sanctions framework exists; enforcement capacity for AI is limited to POTRAZ's data protection mandate under the Cyber and Data Protection Act (2021), while the strategy's proposed AI compliance and monitoring mechanisms remain aspirational and not yet operationally implemented.
Specific Laws: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2026–2030 (2025), Cyber and Data Protection Act [Ch. 12.07] (2021), Cyber and Data Protection (Licensing of Data Controllers and Appointment of Data Protection Officers) Regulations (2024), Post and Telecommunications Act [Ch. 12.05] (2010)
